I want to begin by talking about what exactly an “x-ray” is. That discussion will lead into a
review of some high school science. Then we can pivot into how x-rays are produced and
manipulated for the purpose of medical imaging. Throughout the chapter, I’ll make an effort to
discuss how these topics can be tested.
What is this “x-ray” I’ve heard so much about ? X-rays are a type of Electromagnetic
Radiation” defined by a wavelength of a specific size (between 0.01 - 10 nm).
What is this “Electromagnetic Radiation? ” This is basically a wave of energy that does not
require a media to travel in (it can travel in a vacuum). Its’ velocity is fixed at 3 x 108 m/s
Remember these from general chemistry?
Velocity = Frequency x Wavelength
Energy = Frequency x some constant “h”
Because the velocity is fixed, frequency and wavelength have an inverse relationship. As one
goes up, the other goes down. As these factors change, the quality of the wave changes.
Depending on the frequency and wavelength - an electromagnetic wave has different
qualities. For example, long wavelengths (low frequency) can carry radiant heat. Whereas
shorter wavelengths (high frequency) can carry radio, tv, and radar signals. People who makes
their living begging other people for money (grant writing) decided to arbitrarily pick certain
ranges to have certain names.

Testable Point = MRI does NOT utilize ionizing radiation (the RF pulse is NOT ionizing)
WTF does “Ionizing” even mean ? As the frequency increases, the wave gets more energetic
and will have the ability to remove electrons from an atom. This is called “ionizing radiation.”
Since it takes 15eV of energy to remove an electron from an atom, a photon with energy
greater than 15ev is ionizing (one with less will be non-ionizing).

Remember when I said x-rays are defined by having a wavelength from 0.01 - 10 nm ? You can
drawn another line in the middle of that range and say any x-rays with a wavelength below
0.2-0.1 nm are going to be hard.

It is necessary to first have an understanding of atomic structure and the various movements
of electrons before we move to a meaningful discussion about the interactions between
tungsten targets and fast moving electrons. This topic can be confusing, so I’m going to take a
little extra time to explain things. The level of depth in this discussion is not likely to be
directly tested but it is my hope that an improved understanding of this process will make
more testable sections easier to understand and make the likelihood of being tricked on a
multiple choice question decrease.
Let us begin first by thinking about the atom as having an organization similar to a solar
system. Planets which orbit the sun. The sun being the nucleus, composed of protons and
neutrons. The planets being the electrons. Yes... I realize modem theory on atomic structure
disagrees with this simplification of the Bohr model, but for the purpose of multiple choice
exams: atoms are organized like a solar system.
The number of protons in a given atom is referred to as the ‘‘atomic number” and typically
referred to as the letter Z. The total number of protons + neutrons is referred to as the “mass
number” and typically referred to as the letter A. All atoms of a given element (carbon,
nitrogen, oxygen, etc...) have the same atomic number “Z.” However, they may have
different numbers of neutrons. This leads us to the introduction of “Isotopes.”
Isotopes (which will be important when we discuss nuclear imaging) have the same number
of protons “Z,” but have a different number o f neutrons and therefore different atomic mass
“A.”
Alright... now with those basics out of the way. We can pivot to a discussion of electron
orbits and energy levels. The very mention of electron orbits typically gives general
chemistry professors a raging hard-on. Personally, I never enjoyed the topic, but it is critical
to understanding x-ray generation. So let us proceed once more unto the breach, dear friends,
once more...
As we discussed above, the simplest way to conceptualize an atom is a central positive
nucleus with electrons orbiting in a circular orbit - similar to a solar system. However, unlike
a solar system an “atomic system” has a specific order to how things can line up.
Specifically, the “rules” state that you can only have 2 electrons in the inner most orbit, up to
8 in the second, 18 in the third, 32 in the fourth, and 50 in the fifth. Letters - K, L, M, N, O
etc... etc... are used to designate these orbital levels.

“K Shell” = Hold 2 Electrons
“L Shell” = Hold 8 Electrons

“Binding Force” -The attractive force between the negatively charged electrons and the
positively charge protons acts as the glue which holds the atom together. One might say that
this “binding force” surrounds us and penetrates us; it binds the galaxy together. The strength
of this force is inversely proportional to the square of the distance between the nucleus and the
electrons. In other words, a K shell electron has a larger binding force than an electron in the
L or M shells. The direction of this force is pointed towards the nucleus (that is where the
positive charge originates), just like the planets being pulled towards the sun.
Bound Particles Always Have NEGATIVE Energy - An object in motion is said to have
kinetic energy. Shifty-eyed Physicists have decided that kinetic energy must be described as
positive. Bound objects (like an electron in a certain orbital shell) have negative potential
energy. This is an important concept, because it helps you realize that energy must be added
to these bound objects to bring them to zero. At zero they have reached the “binding energy”
where they can break free of the previously desci

15

- X-Ray Production -
I want to first give a general overview of how x-rays are produced. Then I’ll go piece by
piece through the various components and discuss the trivia. I’ll wrap it up with a more in
depth look at the actual processes of x-ray production (which occurs at the target site).
Thermionic Emissions of Electrons - An Overview:
A “small” current is applied to a filament. The filament is typically made of Tungsten -
because Tungsten has a very high melting temperature (it can tolerate a lot of heat).
Sometimes Rhenium (10%) is added to keep the Tungsten from cracking during the repeated
cycles of heating and cooling. This current is considered “small,” but it still makes the
Tungsten really hot (over 2000 degrees). This extreme temperature means the electrons in the
Tungsten filament are very energetic (like seriously fucking energetic) - so energetic they
“boil off.” This “boiling off’ of electrons is referred to as “thermionic emission.”
The filament is a cathode (negatively charged). This repels the electrons as they boil off. They
fire down a vacuum sealed chamber towards a target (also usually made of Tungsten).
The free electrons accelerate toward this target (on the anode) because of the potential
difference between the cathode and anode. As they accelerate, they gain kinetic energy
(keV). When the energetic electrons strike the Tungsten target, they lose their kinetic energy
via 3 different methods (excitation, ionization, and radiative losses i.e. Bremsstrahlung).
Excitation just makes heat. The other two processes can produce x-rays. I’ll get into the nuts
of bolts of that in bit. First, let us go through the components in a little more detail.
16

Tube (Glass Enclosure / Envelope):
This is a bubble around the anode and cathode.
What’s the testable purpose o f the envelope?
The primary purpose is to maintain a vacuum.
Why do you need vacuum ?
There are a couple of reasons actually. Probably the most important (testable) reason is that
without the vacuum the electrons you are accelerating towards the anode would collide with gas
molecules. Now, I’m sure at least one of you nerds is thinking to yourself - yeah but wouldn’t
those interactions create secondary electrons which could produce even more interactions at the
target. They might. In fact, if you spent some time in a radiology museum you might find some
of these things called “gas x-ray tubes,” that were actually designed to take advantage of that.
The reason this “gas tube” was abandoned is you lose control over your process. These
secondary electrons will be of variable reduced speeds when striking the target, and that will
result in a wide variation in the energy of the x-rays produced.
The true purpose is to allow the amount and speed of the electrons to be controlled
independently.
Cathode: This is the negatively charged part of the tube. Why wouldn’t the test writer just
call it the filament? Short Answer: Because they are fucking with you. It’s basically the
filament. Long Answer: Technically, the cathode is the filament plus the wires which provide the
voltage / amperage to heat the thing up, and the focusing cup. I will talk (write) briefly about
the focusing cup, but I feel it’s probably a low yield concept. The filament is a much higher
yield topic, so lets start the discussion with that.
“The Filament” - This is a thin coiled wire of tungsten. When a current is placed through this
wire it gets hotter than Vulcan’s dick (like 2000 degrees). Vulcan as in the ancient Roman
God of Fire (not the star trek character). The extreme temperature conveys in the electrons
enough energy to escape the metal and form a cloud of electrons. This electron cloud will
hover closely to the filament and is called a “space charge. ” The space charge actually limits
the emission of electrons. This phenomenon is called the “space charge effect. ”
17

“Space charge effect” - The general idea is that in
a normal atom the charge is balanced (equal
protons and electrons). Since you are “boiling” off
electrons from tungsten atoms you are creating an
imbalance. Now you have more protons than
electrons and thus a net positive charge. This net
positive charge in the filament is going to exert
some “pull” on the electron cloud back onto itself.
“Electron Cloud”
As electrons continue to boil off the cloud reaches
an equilibrium size (the number of electrons in the cloud is
constant). As an electron is added to the cloud, one leaves
for the anode.
<a O
< O
But Prometheus?! How can the Tungsten filament have a slight positive charge? I thought the
cathode was negatively charged ? The definition of the word cathode is actually somewhat
confusing. In this situation the simplest way to think about the cathode is the place in the
device where the electrons enter. The filament is the source of electrons, so this is where they
enter the device. Electrons are negatively charged. The space charge (electron cloud) is
negatively charged. Thus this is the negative end of the tube, and will help create a potential
difference between the cathode and anode to drive electron flow towards the target.
in A
What is this “m. I " '? Il stands for “milliampere” - with “ampere” being the key word.
An “ampere” is a unit a measurement used to describe “current” or the movement of
electrons. In other words, it is a description of the QUANTITY of electrons moving
from the cathode to the anode.
Key Concepts:
(1) “mA” is the current of the tube. This is a fancy way of describing the number of
electrons per second moving from the filament to the target.
(2) This relationship is direct. In other words, a mA of 100 has twice the electrons as a
mA of 50.
(3) The quantity (total number of) x-rays produced by the tube depends totally on the
number of electrons that flow from the filament (cathode) to the target (anode).
(4) kVp affects both the quality (ability to penetrate and energy) and the quantity
(number of x-rays). mA and exposure time (mAs) affect only the quantity.
**77/ talk more about this later in the chapter.
18

“Focusing Cup” - Electrons have negative charges. Negative
charges repel each other. This mutual repulsion creates a
spreading / separation. If nothing was done to correct this
you’d end up with a big ugly splat on the target. For reasons
that I’ll discuss later, this is suboptimal. The solution is a
negatively charged metal “focusing cup” placed around the
filament.
What’s the testable purpose o f the focusing cup?
The purpose is to help the electron beam strike the target in an acceptable size.
Anode: The anode is the “target” of Tungsten that is assaulted by the stream of electrons. I
briefly mentioned earlier in the chapter that when the energetic electrons strike the tungsten
target, they lose their kinetic energy via 3 different methods (excitation, ionization, and radiative
losses i.e. Bremsstrahlung). Of these processes, only about 1% of the electrons which strike the
target will result in the production of x-rays. The rest of the energy is converted to heat (via
excitation). This is a critical concept. Electrons on the target = heat. Even though Tungsten has
a melting temperature of around 3300 C, the amount of heat generated at the anode can actually
be a limiting point. Flere is the dilemma - you need a large focal area (target zone) to disperse
the heat or you risk melting your anode. At the same time you need a small focal area to produce
diagnostic quality radiographs.
Solution 1: “Rotating Anode” - Anodes basically come in two flavors. There are stationary
anodes made of a small plate (target) of Tungsten embedded in a larger block of some other
metal (often Copper). Then you have rotating anodes. In this situation, you have a disc of
tungsten which rotates super fast (like several thousand RPMs) while the tube is active.
What’s the testable purpose o f a rotating anode?
The purpose is to spread the heat produced over a larger surface area (prevent the anode from
melting).
Solution 2: “Line Focus Principal” - The second solution is to create a slope or angle in the
target. This also allows for heat to be spread. The testable implications of this run deep because
spatial resolution, the heel effect, and several other factors become involved.
To understand these testable facts we need to dive a little deeper.
19

Focus on the Focal Spot:
Placing an angle in the anode will allow for a
greater surface area to disperse heat. However,
this angle will alter the size of the “effective
focal spot” - which has implications in spatial
resolution. There is some terminology for the
anatomy of this set up that is potentially
testable.
• Electron Stream: This is the
crowd of electrons being
pulled (pushed) towards the
anode.
• Actual Focal Spot: This is
where the electrons actually
land on the anode (the angled
part)
• Apparent (effective) Focal
Spot: This is where the x-rays
land on the patient. This
defines the amount of blur
(not the actual spot).
Electron
Stream
Actual Focal Spot
Apparent (Effective)
Focal Spot
So what is this “focal spot”? The focal spot is the area of electron bombardment (and x-ray
production) on the anode. The big take-home point is that a smaller anode has better spatial
resolution. The trade-off is that a smaller anode can’t disperse heat as well and can melt (so
you deal with more heat limitation). As we discussed, this is addressed in two main ways: (1)
angling the anode and (2) using a rotating anode.
Testable Trivia:
•
Mammo uses a focal spot of 0.3 and 0.1 mm
General X-ray uses a focal spot of 0.6 and 1.2 mm
•
Portable x-ray device often use a stationary anode (doesn’t rotate to dissipate heat). This
limits their tube rating.
20

Changes in Angle / Line Focus Principal:
The goal is the smallest focal spot possible (best resolution) - the trade-off is heat tolerance.
“Line Focus Principal” - Here is the general idea.... You want a small effective focal spot
(the spot on the patient). This is one of the rare situations where bigger is not better. The
smaller the better (that’s not what she said) - because that yields better spatial resolution. You
might think the way to achieve this would be to tighten up your actual focal spot (the spot
where the electrons hit the target) to then make a smaller effective focal spot. This would
actually work. The problem is you would bum up your target. You have to spread the pain
around. That’s where this idea of the “Line Focus Principal” comes in. Someone smarter
than me (and probably you) figured out that if you angle the target that it will keep your large
actual focal spot (for the purpose of dispersing heat) but narrow up your effective focal spot.
Critical (Testable) Trivia: Small Angle = Small Effective Focal Spot
Smaller

Focal

Focal
Trade-offs (also highly testable):
• If the target angle is made too small, then the x-ray beam might not be large enough to
expose a standard image receptor at a typical 40 inch source-to-image distance.
• The angle in the target creates a “heel effect” (I’ll explain what that means on the next
page), just know that Steeper (smaller) Angle = Greater Heel Effect. This is sometimes
called the “Heel Cut-Off’
21

The Heel (Anode-Heel) Effect:
Because x-rays on the anode side must pass through a greater thickness of the anode, you have
a reduction in the intensity of these x-rays. In other words, the intensity of the x-ray beam is
not uniform.
Critical Concept 1:
Smaller Angles = Worsening Heal Effect
The steeper the angle, the more abrupt the change in
intensity.
Critical Concept 2:
Cathode Side = Strong Side
The cathode side will generate the more intense
beam. This factor can be taken advantage of to
create balanced penetration in tissues of variable
thickness.
Critical Concept 3:
Larger FFD = Less Heel Effect
The larger the Focus to Film
Distance (distance between the tube
and the film) the less heel effect.

The idea is that the central beam is
the most uniform. A larger
percentage of the central beam

Characteristic X-Rays
Interactions at the Target (Actual X-Ray Production):
Excitation - This is the dominant process and represents the vast majority of the interactions at
the anode. This occurs when you have energy transfer from the incident particle to an orbital
electron in the target. The transfer moves the electron to a high energy state, but not high enough
to actually remove it from the shell. When it finally calms down (returns to a lower energy state)
there is emission of heat - but no x-ray production. I think about this process as “wiggling but
not moving.”
The other two processes (ionization and Bremsstrahlung) can actually produce x-rays. Before I
get into depth with those I want to discuss some general concepts on x-ray generation.
Concept 1: X-rays are generated through the conversion o f energy. You are taking the kinetic
energy of a fast moving electron and transferring it to the atoms of the tungsten target. Assuming
the conditions are correct that transferred energy will then be converted into an x-ray.
Concept 2: “Voltage” The easiest way to conceptualize voltage is water pressure. More water
pressure means the water is pushed out of the pipe faster. More voltage means the power source
(voltage generator) is exerting more pressure on the electrons through the electric circuit, making
them move faster. In Radiology you see the term “kVp” a lot. That term is describing the
PEAK kilo-voltage. It’s important to understand that not all the electrons are traveling at the
same speed (the system is too complex to be totally uniform) but the fastest electrons in the
stream will have the peak potential energy. If you set the kVp to 100 not every electron in the
stream is at 100 kVp (the number describes the peak).
What about keV ? Is that the same as kVp? No it is not. keV describes the energy of a single
electron in the beam. If the kVp was set to 100, a few of the electrons in the stream will have a
keV of 100, most will be at some value less than that. kVp describes the stream as a whole with
the number selected being the peak. keV is a description of a single individual electron within
that stream, of which you might have randomly selected out to describe.
Concept 3: “Electrons strike the target with different energies. ” This is an extension of concept
2. The idea that the electron stream is not uniform in energy. If you set the device to kVp of 100
some will be at that peak energy, but the others will be at variable energies levels less than that.
So, when they reach the target they will collide and transfer variable degrees of energy.
25

X-Ray Production - How Babies are Made
There are two processes that can result in the production of x-ray photons via the interaction of
high speed electrons and the target atoms.
Process 1: Bremsstrahlung “Brems” (radiative losses) - Think about it like this. You are
traveling across country and suddenly you are overwhelmed with the intense urgency to shit -
code brown - impending massive diarrhea. This is a real emergency. So you pull off the
highway and start looking for a gas station / fast food joint (preferably not a taco bell — as you
suspect that was the genesis of your problem). The sense of urgency and desperation is building
when you see a Hooters on your left. You slam on the brakes and cut across three lanes of traffic
to make it into the parking lot. This process of “breaking” causes your car to lose kinetic energy.
Now - for the parallel.
You are a fast moving electron going in a relatively straight line. You
see the nucleus full of protons. Even though your Dad told you to stay away from protons you
just can’t resist them. So you slam on the breaks and curve towards the nucleus. This process of
breaking causes you to lose kinetic energy.
As the electrons slow down and approach the nucleus they have basically three responses. They
can (1) strike the nucleus and give off maximum energy to the x-ray, (2) come close to the
nucleus and give off medium energy to the x-ray, or (3) travel distant from the nucleus and give
off a little energy to the x-ray. Most (80%) x-rays are produced via this process.
The amount of bremsstrahlung interactions is proportional to the energy of the incoming
charged particle and the atomic number Z of the absorber (larger Z = more).
High Energy X-Ray
Electron Travels
Near Nucleus -
Gives Off High
Energy X-Ray
Electron Travels
Farther Away From
the Nucleus - Gives
Off Low Energy X-Ray
26

The Spectrum: As we discussed, there is a spectrum of options for the breaking electron (maximum,
medium, low) depending on the interaction it has while curving at the nucleus. I think about this like
our friend who needs to poop. He could make it all the way to the bathroom and shit in the toilet
(best result). He could make it into the bathroom but fumble wildly with his zipper prior to shitting
himself just outside the bathroom stall (medium result). He could make it into the parking lot but
fumble wildly with his seatbelt prior to shitting himself in the car (low result). The pants pooping
could occur at any point from the parking lot to the toilet. Because you have this spectrum of results
you are going to produce a spectrum of x-ray energies that looks something like a door stop or hill
when graphed on a multiple choice exam.
The last point I want to make on this (for now) is that the highest achievable energy x-ray is dictated
by the original energy of the electron - which is your kVp. You can’t produce an x-ray with a higher
keV than your kVp. Testable Trivia: Max energy = Max kVp.
Gamesmanship: “Highest Energy” vs “Lowest Energy” X-Ray.
H: The Highest Energy X-ray leaving the tube will depend on the selected kVp. As above this sets
the upper threshold.
L: The Lowest Energy X-ray leaving the tube will depend on the placement of an x-ray filter (or low
energy x-rays absorbed by the envelope - if no filter is used). This is because a spectrum of x-rays
will be generated.
Testable Trivia: Higher Z = More Bremsstrahlung (more protons to make the electrons “break”).
Testable Trivia: In nuclear medicine, low Z materials (plastic) are used to shield Beta emitters
(classical example = 90 Yttrium) in order to minimize bremsstrahlung production. Lead would
make this much worse.
27

Process 2: Ionization - If the energy of the incident particle is enough to eject an electron then you
have ionization. This ionization will then lead to one of two process (only one of which results in the
generation of an x-ray)
Characteristic Radiation (characteristic x- rays): Characteristic x-rays are “characteristic” because
they depend on the binding energy of the shell involved, and this binding energy is different for
different targets. So it is unique... or “characteristic.”
Incident Electron Strikes K Shell
Incident Electron and Former K
Electrons cascade downward to fill lower
Electron with enough energy to
Shell Electron are expelled.
shell vacancies. Excess energy produces x-

You may recall I mentioned (page 13) that the K shell is the inner most shell, and therefore has the
largest negative potential energy. Yes, there are lots of different letter named shells (L, M, N, etc...)
but for the purpose of medical imaging nobody gives a shit about those. K Shell is where it’s at.
When a vacancy in an inner shell is created, an electron in an outer shell promptly jumps in to fill the
vacancy. Energy released in this process is equal to the difference in binding energies between the
two shells. The energy may appear as an x-ray photon. Since the binding energies have exact
characteristic values, the emitted x-rays carry exact and discrete energies and are called
“characteristic x-rays.” The characteristic x-rays are depicted as sharp peaks over the Bremsstrahlung
continuum. The peaks noted on the figure correspond to the energy difference between the inner
vacated shell (secondary to the K shell electron being ejected upon collision with an incident high
energy electron) and an outer shell which is vacated to fill the inner shell.
More Meat on the Spectrum: Test writers love spectrum questions so I want to be complete in my
discussion. I’ve diagramed out a spectrum of Tungsten x-ray production. Notice there are multiple
little spikes. This is because not every event is an L —> K transition to fill that vacancy. You can
have M —> K, N —-> K , etc... Remember that the difference between these two shells negative
potential energies is going to translate to the energy of the produced x-rays.
Also, remember that the energies of these x-rays
are “characteristic” to the target element. K shell
binding energies of Copper are different that
Tungsten. So, this pattern could be thought of as
a fingerprint for that element.
keV
28
Characteristic X-Rays

Testable Point: The K-shell binding energy of tungsten is -69.5 keV.
Gamesmanship: We said the binding energy of the K shell electron of Tungsten was -69.5 keV.
So we need to transfer at least that much energy to liberate that electron and start the process of
generating a characteristic x-ray. If we only had electrons with 69.0 keV we would not generate
K-shell characteristic x-rays.
A way to ask this would be to say “what percentage o f the x-ray spectrum will K-shell
characteristic x-rays make when the tube is set to 69.0 kVp ? ” The answer would be zero.
There are a few other ways to ask this same question by drawling spectrum diagrams, and I’ll
cover those tricks along with other spectrum diagram related fuckery later in the chapter.
kVp Manipulation Trivia:
• Below 69.5 kVp = Zero K-Shell Characteristic X-Rays
• Between 80-150 kVp = 10-25% K-Shell Characteristic X-Ray Contribution
• Between 150-300 kVp = Progressive Decrease in Contribution
• Above 300 kVp = Negligible Contribution
K-shell electrons prefer an energy that is fairly close to the binding energy. In other words, a
crazy high energy of like 300 kVp will actually make characteristic x-ray generation less likely.
“Beam Intensity” —This is described as the number of x-rays multiplied by the energy. You
will typically see this written as Roentgens per min (R/min). There are multiple factors that are
going to contribute to this process including; mA, kVp, target material, and filtrations. You can
imagine how easily this type of thing would lend itself to multiple choice.
Potential Fuckery: “Beam Intensity” is NOT the same as “total number of x-rays.” Remember
that beam intensity factors in the “pushing power” i.e. energy of the spectrum. Be very careful
when you read factor manipulation questions for what terminology they are using. More on this
later.
29

QUALITY and QUANTITY
~ Critical Concept ~
QUANTITY
The total number of x-rays or area under the curve in a spectrum diagram.
QUALITY
;
The overall energy of the beam.
Changes in average energy reflect changes in quality.
How Does the Target Material Influence Quantity and Quality ?
Short Answer: The larger the atomic number (Z) the more x-rays will be produced.
Long Answer: An increase in atomic number only applies to Bremsstrahlung generated x-rays
(which is still the majority of x-rays). Just think more protons, more pull means greater
breaking / curving, more curving means more interactions, more interactions means more x-rays.
A change in atomic number will effect the characteristic x-rays also but NOT the number
produced. Instead changing the Z will change the various energies at the shell level (most
importantly the K shell). Remember this is why characteristic x-rays are “characteristic.”
Large Z = More Overall X-Rays via Bremsstrahlung. This is a change in Q U A N TITY .
Large Z = Difference Characteristic Energy Peaks. No change in the amount produced. This is a
change is Q U A LITY .
Practice Target Change Example:
I’ll discuss this more in the mammo chapter, but this is a good place to introduce the concept.
Tungsten: This is the target used in general radiology. Tungsten has a relatively high Z (74).
This high Z is why it is chosen (that and its’ high melting point). Because the Z is relatively high
it is efficient at producing x-rays, but remember that Z only effects the QUANTITY of
Bremsstrahlung x-rays. In fact, the Tungsten spectrum is always all Bremsstrahlung (with some
variation at kVps in the 80-150 range).
Molybdenum: This is a common target in mammography. “Moly” has a lower Z (42) which
decreases the Bremsstrahlung contribution and in combination with the use of lower kVp
increases the characteristic x-ray contribution. The K shell binding energy is 20 keV (vs 69.5 for
Tungsten) - so the spectrum of characteristic peaks is much lower. This is done on purpose to
maximize tissue contrast.
30

How Does the kVp Influence Quantity and Quality ?
Remember the kVp is the maximum (or peak) energy of the electron stream. As this increases
the average energy of the electron stream will go up as well. The result of this increase has three
testable results:
(1) The maximum x-ray energy will go up to match the kVp.
(2) The average x-ray energy will go up - a change in QUALITY
(3) The total number of x-rays produced will go up (more energy was available in those
bremsstrahlung x-rays) - a change in QUANTITY. The beam intensity will increase as a
square of kVp.
Intensity is proportional to (kVp)2
There will NOT be a change in the position of the characteristic x-ray peaks with an increase in
kVp. Although, if you decreased the kVp you could potentially drop below the necessary K-
shell binding energy and lose them.
How Does the mAs Influence Quantity and Quality ?
Remember that the mA describes the number of moving electrons. In other words, it is a
description of the QUANTITY of electrons moving from the cathode to the anode. This
relationship is direct. In other words, a mA of 100 has twice the electrons as a mA of 50.
The “s” at the end of mA“s” is a measurement of time. So, what is really being said with mAs is
“there are ‘x’ amount of electrons moving towards an anode for a certain amount of time.” It is
all about QUANTITY. mAs has not role in the average energy or peak energy of the spectrum.
QUANTITY but NOT Quality.
15% Rule
This is a described rule for discussing the relationship between kVp and mA. It goes like this:
If you increase the kVp by 15%, you can decrease the mAby half and maintain the same x-ray
density on the film. If you decrease the kVp by 15% you need to double the mA to maintain
the same density on the fdm.
I will discuss the application of this more in chapter 2.
31

How Does the Voltage Generator Influence Quantity and Quality ?
We haven’t talked much about generators or generator waveforms because I believe this to be a
very low yield topic. However, the concept of “voltage ripple” does lend itself to multiple
choice because it is a factor that effects QUALITY and QUANTITY.
Let’s rapidly discuss single phase vs three phase generators.
Single Phase: This type of generator will produce a voltage that
/ \
r \ A
starts at zero, peaks, then drops back to zero again. This cycle
repeats throughout the exposure ( 0 - max - 0 - max - 0 - max ).
kVp will follow the curve of the rainbow - with the “peak” at the peak of the rainbow. This is
one of the reasons you have a spectrum of energies (poly) and not a uniform energy (mono).
Three Phase: This type of generator will produce three separate
repeating curves slightly out of phase with each other. The idea is
to try and maintain a uniform peak output. There is still some
variability - “ripple” - so it is not totally uniform.
Overall, since the voltage produced remains higher throughout the
exposure, the average electron energy will be increased. This is
going to result in a change in quality (increase in the average x-
ray energy), and quality (more energy electrons = more
Bremsstrahlung).
Single Phase vs Triple Phase Generator:
Keeping the kVp constant, the Triple Phase will produce more x-rays, and produce a curve with a
higher average energy, The peak of the curve will NOT change (as that is limited by kVp).
Single Phase:
More Ripple = Less QUANTITY and QUALITY.
Triple Phase:
Less Ripple = More QUANTITY and QUALITY.
32

We haven’t talked much about filters. That is not because it isn’t high yield. I just haven’t got to
it yet. This is actually very high yield because the topic overlaps Quantity / Quality questions
with Spectrum questions.
The best way to discuss filters is via the concept of “H alf Value Layer. ”
How Does the Addition of a Filter Influence Quantity and Quality ?
‘‘H alf Value Layer” is a measurement done to help
understand beam quality. It is the amount of material
required to attenuate an x-ray to 1/2 the original output.
The higher the average photon energy the more
penetrating it will be, and the larger its HVL.
If the beam is filtered the wimpy low energy photons will
be removed first (leaving the higher ones). This will
increase the average photon energy. Since average
photon energy determines penetration capacity this
will increase the HVL.
HVL of an x-ray beam does
NOT depend on mAs
HVL does depend on beam
filtration
HVL does depend on anode
material (e.g. Tungsten)
HVL does depend on kVp
More Filtration = Higher HVL
Less Filtration = Lower HVL
Testable Trivia: With each HVL the average photon energy goes up.
3rd HVL > 2nd HVL > 1st HVL

10th HVL (“TVL”) : This is the thickness of material that can attenuate an x-ray to 90%.

QUALITY and QUANTITY - Trivia Summary
Increased Target Atomic Number (Z)
Increases Quality and Quantity
Increased kVp
Increases Quality and Quantity
Increased mAs
Increased Quantity

Decreased Quantity
Testable Trivia Recap and Rapid Review
• Electrons from a heated filament will be pushed / pulled towards a target. When they strike the
target 3 basic things happen: (1) Heat Production, (2) Characteristic Interactions, and (3) Brems
Interactions
• “Characteristic Interactions” = Occur when an inner electron is forced out, and its’ spot is filled
by an outer electron. The exchange results in release of potential energy in the form of a
“characteristic photon.” This makes the spiky thing on the spectrum diagrams.
• The energy of the characteristic photon is specific to the shell and the target. The classic -69.5 K
shell Tungsten.
• Brems interactions occur from “Breaking” or “Slowing Down” of the electrons around a target
nucleus. It occurs in a spectrum - with different energy photons given off depending on high close
the electron is to the nucleus (how much slowing down it does). This makes the fat mountain
looking part on the spectrum diagrams.
• The average Brems energy is equal to one third of the kVp selected. The peak energy is the kVp.
• Quantity (total number of x-rays) is directly proportional to mAs. Double = Double. Quantity
varies as the approximate square of the change in kVp. Double = Increased Quantity by 5 ish.
Quantity decreases with distance - via the inverse square law (change as the inverse of the square
of the distance).
• Quality (penetrating power) is affected by (1) kVp -directly, and (2) Filtration. Quality is
measuring in “half value thickness” hence penetrating power.
• FIVE Factors will change the x-ray spectrum
• (1) mA = Beam Quantity
• (2) kVp = Beam Quality (and quantity)
• (3) Filtration = Beam Quality (and quantity)
• (4) Target Material = Changes Characteristic Peaks. Also increasing the atomic number of the
target will increase x-ray quantity * one of the reasons Tungsten is used. Also the quality is
changed or “shifted to the right”
• (5) Generator Type = Improving the efficiency (less ripple) of the generator increases the x-
ray beam quantity and quality.

Off-Focal Radiation: This is scatter from the anode outside the focal area. This leads
to an increase in patient exposure and blurring. This can be reduced with a small lead
collimator near the tube output. Tubes constructed with metal enclosures and/or with the
anode at electric ground potential will have less off -focal radiation (metal envelope attracts
the scattered electrons).
X-Ray Tube Insert: X-ray tube is sealed under high vacuum. This vacuum is needed to
prevent electrons from interacting with gas molecules. The x-ray port is necessary because
x-rays are produces in all directions. Only the x-rays heading towards the patient are
“useful.” The port is typically made of the same stuff as the housing (except with
mammography in where it’s made of beryllium to minimize low energy x-ray absorption).
Unwanted Radiation Vocab:
•
Leakage - X-rays that are transmitted though the housing
•
Secondary - Characteristic radiation that is made from electron interaction with materials
other than the target (glass, housing, etc.).
•
Scattered - X rays that are deflected in direction once they leave the tube
•
Stray - The sum total of leakage and scatter
Collimation: This is the process of restricting the size and shape of the x-ray beam
emerging from the port. It’s done to reduce both primary and secondary radiation. It
improves image quality (less fog). The tech can gauge how much they are collimating by
using a beam of light reflected by a mirror of low x-ray attenuation that mimics the x-ray
beam. By order of various regulatory bodies, the light field and x-ray field must closely align.
Trivia'. The terms “beam restriction ” and “collimation ” are used interchangeable. They both
means the same thing - decreasing the size of the projected radiation field.
• Increasing Collimation = Decreasing Field Size
• Decreasing Collimation = Increasing Field Size
Grid Controlled: This has nothing to do with an actual grid. Instead it refers to a way to
turn the tube on and off fast by using a little cup around the filament to “suck electrons out.”
You are not actually shifting the big voltage across the tube, just diverting the electrons. This
is how pulse fluoro is done (more on this in the fluoro chapter).
40

DEXA Scan
Now is as good of a time as any to talk about the DEXA scan. DEXA or Dual Energy
X-Ray Absorption is a technique that relies on the differences in transmission through
different tissues at different energies.
Let’s just look at it from an engineering standpoint - for the purpose of understanding
the principals, then I’ll talk about what I think is testable.
Basically you have two unknown quantities (1) Area of bone mineral, and (2) Area of
soft tissue. It’s easy enough to calculate how much a single beam will be attenuated
through the mass of both (you know what you put in, and you can measure what you
got out). But how do you tell which was attenuated by bone, and which was attenuated
by soft tissue?
There were some ancient methods that I’m not going to talk about (submerging people
in water, using gamma rays, etc..). The eventual solution was to use two different
energies - one high and one low. The idea is that the bone is gonna block the low
energy ones, but not the high energy ones. The soft tissue is gonna let them both
through. Then you can do math to subtract out what is just bone. You make a bunch of
measurements - average them out and come up with a density (BMD).
Trivia:
DEXA relies on transmission measurements made at two different photon
energies.
Two main methods for doing this:
o
Use a filter that drops the k-edge right in the middle of the spectrum -
essentially separating the curve into two peaks - or two different
energies (usually around 40 and 70).
o
Switch the tube voltage between low (70) and high (140) - placing the
effecting average energy around 45 and 100.
•
Dose for a DEXA scan is VERY low - we are talking 0.001 mSv (for
perspective, a spine x-ray is like 1.5 mSv)

The title of this chapter used to be called
“Interactions with Matter.” I decided to change it
because what “matter” really means is interactions
with “people.” X-Rays can interact with people
(matter) in 5 ways - only 3 of which occur in the
range of energy used with diagnostic radiology.
Occur at Dx Energy:
• Classical (Coherent)
• Compton
• Photoelectric
Occurs Way Above Dx Energy
• Pair Production
• Photodisintegration
For completeness (and because I cannot exclude some serious fuckery) - I’m going mention
all 5 - but focus on the ones that are more likely to be tested (Compton and Photoelectric).
44

Classical
There are a bunch of names for this; “Classical” , “Coherent”, “Thomson” - remember that
the one name you don’t remember will be the one they call it on the test. The situation is that
your x-ray strikes an orbital electron and essentially bounces off - changing direction.
technically it gets absorbed and re-emitted without a loss o f energy, but it’s easier to think
about it just bouncing o ff and changing direction. The key point is these x-rays are low
energy (less than 10 keV). Because they are low energy they don’t cause ionization (they
can’t shake an electron loose). This is the most important thing to remember - the vast
majority of these are absorbed by the body and DO NOT CONTRIBUTE
SIGNIFICANTLY TO THE IMAGE, BUT DO ADD A SMALL AMOUNT OF DOSE
TO THE PATIENT.
Key Points:
Does NOT result in ionization (no electron is liberated/ejected).
Does NOT result in the net transfer of energy.
Does NOT result in a contribution to the image
Only adds a SMALL (TINY) amount of dose to the patient.
•
Seen primarily at low energies - i.e. mammography; wasting about 15% of photon
interaction below 30 keV.
Scattered X-Ray
*No Energy Lost
*No Ionization
*Only a Change
in Direction
Incident Wimpy
(Low Energy) X-Ray
45

Compton S cattering (“The Asshole”):
Just so we are clear - this is highly highly testable.
Unlike Classical Interaction
(Coherent) which occurs at low
energies, Compton scatter occurs
at higher energies. The situation
is that your x-ray strikes an outer
shell electron. This interaction is
strong enough to remove it from
the shell (orbit). The incoming or
“incident” x-ray loses some
Incident X-Ray
energy and is reflected in a new
direction.
This interaction causes 3 things:
(1) It Frees an Electron (the one it knocked loose). This “compton electron” can go off and
wreak havoc elsewhere in the body
(2) It Ionizes an Atom (because it is missing an electron)
(3) It deflects the incoming x-ray “Scattered Photon”
What are the implications of this mess?
Overall it’s bad. Pretty much all bad. The free electron can go off and undergo other interactions
- none of which are meaningful for image creation and some of which could be harmful. The
ionized atom will likely behave badly with the potential for biological harm to the patient.
Lastly, the scattered photon may strike the image receptor in the wrong spot - and contribute zero
helpful information. In fact it’s just creating a “fog” to the image.
Bottom Line?
• Compton = Bad (Straight out of Compton... those guys are trouble)
• Compton Contributes to Dose, and Fogs Up the Image.
Additional Testable Trivia:
• Predominant interaction in the diagnostic energy range with soft tissue (100 keV and up)
• Probability of Compton scatter does technically decrease with increased X-ray energy,
although it occurs at all energies (in tissue) that arc used in medical imaging.
• Compton interactions are the DOMINANT force contributing to SCATTER/FOG
• Compton is the MAJOR SOURCE of OCCUPATIONAL Exposure - all the scatter inside
the patient is being emitted all around - towards you / the tech/ etc...
• Probability of a Compton scatter does NOT depend on the Z of the atom - because the energy
of these outer shell electrons is low.
• Probability of a Compton scatter is dependent on the density of the material - elements
contain around the same amount of electrons per gram - regardless of their atomic number.
• Hydrogenous materials have a higher probability for Compton scattering.
“Compton”
Electron
Scattered
Photon
46

Photoelectric In teractio n s (“T h e Good Guy”):
Just so we are clear - this is highly highly testable.
Unlike Classical Interaction (Coherent) which occurs at low energies (lOish), or Compton
scatter which occurs at higher energies, Photoelectric occurs throughout the diagnostic range
(20-120). BUT-and this is the major testable point - IT DOMINATES AT LOWER ENERGY
(relative to Compton).
The situation is that your x-ray strikes an inner shell electron. For the P.E. interaction to
occur the energy of the x-ray must be equal (or greater) than the orbital shell binding energy.
You have to have enough juice to let it loose. This interaction is strong enough to remove the
electron from the shell (orbit).
The incoming or “incident” x-ray loses all it’s energy and is totally absorbed. This interaction
is referred to as “all or nothing” - either it can release that inner shell electron or it can’t.
With this inner electron spot missing you get downward cascade and release of a
characteristic x-ray or you get the production of an Auger electron. Because Auger electron
production tends to dominate in biologic tissue (unlike Tungsten, a target with a high Z) you
still end up with biologic damage from these free electrons. P.E. is still good because this
actually contributes to image contrast (Compton just makes noise).

missing an electron)

When does the P.E. Occur?
• Energy of interaction - You have to have
an energy higher (or equal) to the inner
shell binding energy. Although (and I’ll
talk about this later) the peak energy for
P.E. is right around the binding energy for
that shell. The probably of a P.E.
interaction actually starts to decline after
that — x-rays (incident photon “IP”) with a
ton of juice just pass right through. The
function is inverse to the third power and
that is testable.
• Atomic Number (Z) of the Absorber -
Remember people aren’t all made out of the
same shit. You have fat, water, bone, etc..
(Basically “Soft Tissue” and “Bone”). The
higher the Z - the more likely you will have
a photoelectric effect, and the change is
significant - because it’s also cubed (to the
third power).
Why is this P.E. so important in Radiology?
It is the fundamental method by which contrast is developed. For example, bone has a
higher Z than soft tissue. Higher Z means more P.E. More P.E. means more absorbed
photons, so fewer photons hitting the imaging receptor. The end result?
Bones are white on an x-ray... it’s science.
More critical than that is the smaller differences between stuff are amplified because it’s not
1:1 - it is cubed. So a small change in Z is amplified. This helps with soft tissue delineation.
Using your practical understanding of bones being white on an x-ray, I’m going to try and
help you remember the energy differences. Ever see an x-ray that’s “burnt out” or “over
exposed” ? The reason it looks like shit is because the tech selected a kVp that was too high.
Remember that photoelectric effect dominates at or around the binding energy of the inner
shell electron - and above that it drops off rapidly. So, too much kVp = not enough P.E. and
therefore not enough contrast (everything looks the same).

energy and incident photon energy are
about the same (assuming IP still > BE).
Probability will drop off big time as
IP > > > BE.

Why Can’t Superman See What Color Lois Lane’s Underwear Are?
In the 1978 classic Superman movie - Lois Lane interviews Superman on the roof top
during which she challenges him to use his x-ray vision to tell her the color of her
underwear, but because she is standing behind a lead flower pot he can’t see them.
The reason he can’t see them is because Lead has a High Z.
High Z = More Photoelectric Effect (more absorption).
She then steps out from behind the pot... and he tells her they are pink. Then like a
promiscuous girl she says “do you like pink?” Girl, I’m a freak you shouldn't say those
things
Concept o f the flower pot is the same reason shields, gowns, gloves etc... made to shield
x-rays are made o f lead. It s all about the P.E.
What about these Secondary X-Rays?
You may have recognized that this P.E. process is the same process we talked about in the
first chapter with high speed electrons striking K shell Tungsten and creating a cascade of
characteristic x-rays. The difference here is that instead of using a high speed electron as our
projectile and Tungsten as our target we are using x-ray photons and human tissue atoms
(mostly carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and calcium).
So what about the characteristic x-rays that are produced during the P.E. interactions with
human tissues? Remember that the energy of the generated x-ray photons is dependent on
the K-shell binding energy ? Tungsten was around 70. Well, in the human body Calcium has
the highest atomic number (of any element found in a relevant quantity). The K shell photon
emitted from Calcium is 4 keV. That is the highest one. So, the Hydrogen, Oxygen, Carbon
ones are all less than that. What I’m getting at here is these secondary x-rays are total pieces
of shit. Those things are so wimpy they aren’t every going to make it to the film / detector
(typically they are absorbed within millimeters of their origin).
This is NOT the case with Barium and Iodine (contrast agents). Those higher Z elements can
actually generate secondary x-rays with enough juice to leave the patient and fog the image.
Shifty eyed PhDs will make a big deal about calling this “secondary radiation,” and not
“scatter radiation” although clinically the distinction is meaningless since they both result in
noise / fog.

I want to start by defining a term that we throw around a lot - but often don’t think about

tell that two things standing next to each other are different. The more contrast - the
easier it is to tell them apart. For example, the white lion and the black lion - standing
next to each other - the difference is obvious. Even if they were very small you’d be
able to tell them apart. There is a high “contrast” between white

In Radiology we try and maximize contrast - so we can tell things apart. The easiest
example would be the use of IV contrast or Oral Contrast. Both of these things have
high Z’s - and therefore more photoelectric effect. Remember more RE. = more
contrast. What you get is a white thing (contrast) next to darker things.

P ho toelectric In teractio n s
-It was the best o f times, It was the worst o f times-
I want to spend a little more time talking about the pros and cons of P.E. interactions in medical
imaging.
“The Best o f Times ”
Photoelectric interactions are great for producing crisp diagnostic images. Certainly this
interaction is superior to Compton Scatter (the asshole) which just makes noise. The reason the
P.E. interactions produce such high quality images is the amplification of natural contrast. The
human body is made up of different tissues and the entire point of imaging is to try and separate
out what is where. Since P.E. interactions depend on the Z of tissues, the absorption rates are
going to vary. The differences in these absorptions generates “contrast.”
This contrast is amplified by the third power of the atomic number -
essentially creating a magnification of the differences between tissues.
“The Worst o f Times”
Dose.
P.E. interactions are going to generate more dose that any other type of interaction. The reason
is all the energy of that incident x-ray photon gets absorbed. People talk a mess of shit about
Compton Scatter (the asshole) but only part of the energy gets absorbed by the patient in those
interactions.
To address this higher kVp settings are used. You still need / want some P.E. You don’t want
scatter (which will fuck up the image), but you will tolerate some to keep the dose reasonable.
Bottom line is that the general practice is to try and select the highest kVp possible that will still
yield a diagnostic image. This is part of the balance between dose and diagnostic quality.

Rule of 15
**Not to be confused with Darth Bane’s Rule of 2
Back in Chapter 1 - 1 briefly mentioned this thing called the “15% Rule of Thumb.”
Basically, it says that X-Ray production increases in a linear direction with mA.
Doubling mA will double intensity of the x-ray spectrum. BUT Increasing kVp by
15% will double the intensity of the spectrum. This is because kVp is increasing the
amount of x-rays produced (via Brems) and the average energy.
In other words - if you want to increase the x-ray exposure you can either
(1) double your mAs or (2) increase the kVp by 15%.
Why you might do one vs the other depends on the needs of the study. Don’t forget
that the average energy of the x-ray spectrum defines the attenuation characteristics
and defines the contrast between objects and tissue.
Want to visualize low contrast objects / tissue ? - Keep the kVp constant and increase
the mAs. You go too high on that kVp and you are going to lose all your RE.
Want to lower dose but maintain a constant exposure ? Raise the kVp by 15%, then
lower the mAs by 50%. *This works because the higher kVp x-rays will penetrate
more easily and delivers a lower dose (*skin dose) - less P.E. interactions (remember
we said they are worse for dose).
The study is contrasted ? Remember you are going to want the kVp set at least twice
the binding energy of the contrast agent being used. Iodine - K edge is 33 keV; so
you want it set at least 66 kVp. Barium - K edge is 37 kev, so you want it set at least
74 kVp
3rd Grade Math (something doctors don’t know how to do... usually).
If you wanted to increase the kVp by a certain percentage you need to multiply it by
the number with a “1.0” in front of it. Example:
kVp is 80. Want to increase by 15%. 80 x 1.15 = 92
If you want to decrease the kVp by a certain percentage you need to multiply if by
the number you would add to the percent amount to make 1.0. Example:
kVp is 80. Want to decrease by 15%. 80 x 0.85 = 68

Pair Production
I’m going to try and imagine a scenario where this could be practically asked on the exam.
OK.... Got one. Colossus - the superhero from X-Men (and recently Deadpool) arrives to get an
x-ray. Because his body is a form of organic steel he has an unusually high Z. One that is much
higher than normally seen in human tissue.
Pair production occurs when a very high energy photon (at least 1.022 MeV) is used on
something with a high Z. If it has a low Z (like human tissue) you’d need around 30 MeV to
make this the dominant process. Anyway - the very high energy photon will have enough juice
to shoot right past the electrons and interact directly with the nucleus. The result is complete
absorption of the photon and production of a “pair.” This pair is 1 electron, and 1 positron. The
electron is gonna go off and do it’s thing. The positron is going to react just like it would in a
PET Scanner. It’s going to travel a very short distance, collide with an electron (the enemy of a
positron) and annihilate giving off two 511 keV photons 180 degrees apart.
So, you’d want to bombard Colossus with very high energy photons and image him in a PET
Scanner.

Absorption
Differential Absorption = The difference between the x-rays that get absorbed (via P.E. etc..)
and those that pass right through. The word “differential” refers to the fact that not all body
parts absorb equally (example: bone > lung).
Vocab:
• Transmission = The X-Rays that make it through the body and reach the imaging receptor
• Absorption = The X-Rays that are attenuated by the body and don’t make it to Valhalla
(the imaging receptor).
Anything that increases P.E, increases absorption (and decreases transmission). Remember
the big ones are, kVp near the k-edge, higher Z, more density.
In other words - Attenuation in Tissue Depends on 3 things:
• Effective Atomic Number in Tissue
• X Ray Beam Quality (energy)
• Tissue Density
Linear Attenuation vs Mass Attenuation:
•
Linear: This is the actual fraction of photons interacting per unit thickness of an
absorber. In other words, it is the fraction of photons removed from the x-ray in a certain
distance factoring in effects from compton scatter, PE effect, and coherent scatter. In
contrast to “mass attenuation,” the linear attenuation of ice, water, and water vapor is
different (they have different lengths for the same amount of molecules).
Factors to consider
*
More attenuation occurs with denser object
*
More attenuation occurs with higher Z material
*
Lower attenuation occurs with higher kVp
*
Higher attenuation occurs at K-edge
•
Mass: This is the fraction of photons interacting - scaled per gram of tissue. This is
supposed to reflect the attenuation. The important point is that the mass attenuation of
ice, water, and water vapor is the same.

It is important to realize that a transmitted photon (one that makes it through the patient onto the
film / detector) is just as important as an attenuated photon (on that got blocked). For example,
if every photon made it through the film would be uniformly black. If every photon got blocked
you’d have a totally white film. You need a mix of both to make a diagnostic image.
Let’s imagine we (the techs) are taking an x-ray of a 400 pound Diabetic’s stinky foot. That
smelly sloppy foot is going to be composed primarily of infected osteomyelitis ridden bones,
soft tissue (atrophic fatty infiltrated neuropathic muscles, actual fat, water, etc...) and a candy

At a low keV you can see the contrast between the two is greatest. As keV increases the

V ariables and Entrance Skin Dose
Entrance Skin Dose (ESD) is a term used to describe the radiation absorbed by the skin as the
bean strikes the patient. Absorbed is a key concept here, and something we will continue to
discuss later in the text - I just thought this was a good place to sneak in this concept since we
already discussed a lot of the factors involved. Speaking of those factors, quick review of the
variables that will determine how much radiation gets blasted into the skin of the innocent
patient by the reckless and cruel Radiology department.
Factors that determine ESD:
•Tube Current (mA) - Proportion Relationship
•Time of Exposure (“s”) - Proportional Relationship
•Peak Kilovoltage (kVp) - Square
•Distance - Regulated for the inverse square law (double the distance, dose reduced by 4)
As above, the ESD will change as the square of the change in kVp. More kVp = higher average
energy and therefore more penetration. As we have discussed, this higher kVp could allow for
the use of a lower mA or reduced time of exposure “s’ - making those modifications could
actually reduce dose to the patient. This kind of complex thinking can get you into trouble on the
exam. Should I assume that the mA has been reduced??? N o... never assume. It is bad
manners. If you are lucky, the question writer will give the qualifier “all variables held
constant.” Now let us take a look at some possible math problems using this concept.
Warning! This will require 9th Grade M ath... so pucker your assholes
(Doctors almost always suck at math, otherwise they’d be engineers.

You should get 39.2 mR

CHAPTER I:
© IN IIR M I ° i A T C O N C E P T !
- PROMETHEUS LIONHART, M.D. -
In this chapter I’m going to fill in some knowledge gaps regarding general x-ray stuff
(what makes the image good vs crap).
There are 4 main image characteristics people talk about. (1) Noise, (2) Spatial Resolution,
(3) Contrast, and (4) Brightness. I’m gonna cover those, and related trivia.
Old people hate a lot of things - including (but not limited too) “Change” , Vegans, Popular
Music, Black Presidents, the Internet, Cold Weather, Gay People (except Ellen - everyone
loves Ellen), and “NOISE !!!”
Radiologist Young and Old also hate “Noise”. So what is this “Noise” ?
Noise is basically data that contributes nothing useful to the image, and serves only as a
distraction. “Quantum Noise ” is the more fancy term is the term / concern for digital
imaging. Think of it as background randomness. The piece of paper you are drawling on is
not clean and white, it’s got some background smudges on it. If you draw with a thin pencil
you’ll notice the background dirt a lot more than if you draw with a thick magic marker.
The key point to know is that this occurs as a function of the number of photons hitting the
imaging receptor.
• More photons (thick marker) = More Signal relative to Noise
• Less Photons (thin pencil) = Low Signal relative to Noise
That background variation (noise) is always there - you just drown it out with a heavier
exposure. Bottom line = anything that increases the number of photons on the imaging
receptor will cut down on the “mottle” and therefore reduce noise.
Analogy: You are in the waiting room trying to talk to a patient. In the waiting room there
is a TV and Fox News is playing very loud. To make sure your patient can hear you, you
speak very loud (you increased the signal, background noise is constant). The patient is a
nervous little guy and he whispers when he talks (low signal, background noise is constant).
You have trouble hearing him.
(1) Noise
60

“Quantum Mottle” - A very popular term thrown around by people pretending to be smart /
know physics. Basic concept is that the x-ray beam is not perfectly uniform and at low
doses you notice it more. To use another analogy think about rain on a sidewalk. When it
first starts to rain the few drops on the sidewalk might appear random, but once the
downpour starts the sidewalk is saturated and you’d swear the rain is coming down in a
totally homogenous pattern (even though its not really - but almost).
Scatter - Remember the definition of “noise” is data that
does not contribute to the image. So not all “noise” is
quantum mottle (although that is the major contributor).
Let us discuss further... for the purpose of multiple
choice.
The vast majority of the scatter is via Compton
interactions. Essentially no characteristic radiation is
going to make the film because it is so low energy. The
only exception would be the characteristic radiation
generated from heavier elements like Iodinated contrast
and Barium.
What determines the about o f scatter? There are 3 primary
kVp: As discussed in the prior chapter, RE. interactions
dominate at lower energy levels and Compton scatter
plays a much large role in the higher diagnostic ranges
{above 26 kVp in soft tissue, and above 35 kVp in bone).
Higher kVp — More Scatter
Field of View: A smaller field of view will irradiate a smaller volume of tissue and
therefore create less scatter interactions. Additionally, a smaller FOV will have a larger
“angle of escape.” The angle of escape basically refers to the idea that scatter photons can
exit in any direction, but only the ones that land on the film matter. So, smaller film size (or
FOV) smaller target for the scatter to land on.
Thickness: Similar to the field size, a thicker body part will have more irradiated tissue and
therefore more Compton scatter interactions.
factors.
Scatter is Maximized With:
- High kVp Technique
- Large Field of View
- Thick Parts (or People)
Vocab
“Radiographic Density”
Also called “optical density”,
“photographic density”, or
“film density” by various
geriatric practitioners.
This is essentially a
description of how dark the
film is. More x-rays on the
film = darker = more
radiographic density.
61

Noise Reduction - Part A - “Collimation”
-Dealing with Scatter-
Collimators are a type of x-ray beam
restriction device that is attached to the
housing of the x-ray tube.
Beam Restriction or Collimation is done
to limit patient exposure and reduce
scatter. If you increase collimation you
decrease field size, and if you decrease
collimation you increase field size.
Increased Field Size (decreased
collimation) decreases quantum noise —
although dose and scatter are increased.
To compensate for the overall decreased
photons hitting the image receptor
(decreased signal to quantum noise ratio)
when you collimate - the mAs is typically
increased.
For the purpose o f multiple choice:
Collimation decreases noise.
62

Noise Reduction - Part B - “Grid”
-Dealing with Scatter-
Grids are used to improve contrast (by reducing scatter). Scatter is a “violation” of the most
basic law of radiography, because it doesn’t originate in a straight line from the focal spot.
Instead it is the result of Compton scatter in the body (which will squirt out like a wild
fireman’s hose or radiate like light bulb).
Off Angle
How does the Grid work? A grid is a
v Scatter Is
device that has multiple thin metallic
4 Blocked
(usually lead) strips with interspersed
radiolucent spaces (holes). It is
placed behind the patient and in front
of the image receptor (I.R.). The
primary action of the grid is to block
off angle (presumed scattered) x-rays,
and allow the ones the are coming
straight in to contact the image
receptor.
Patient
Grid
ll.R .
Grid Ratio: This is a term to describe how dense the grid is. It’s basically the ratio of the
height of the lead to the distance between them. The higher the ratio the less scatter and the
better the contrast.
What does the Grid do to Dose ? The tradeoff to using a grid is
that dose is increased (because the automatic brightness control
turns up the juice to compensate). A higher grid ratio will create a
larger increase in dose. There is a thing called the “bucky factor”
which is basically the mAs required with the grid / mAs without the
grid.
“Bucky Grids ” - This is a moving grid. It wiggles back and forth rapidly - too fast to be
seen. If the grid motion fails (and it stops moving) you will end up with grid lines.
Grid Cut Off: This is a possible “risk” of using a grid. Essentially what happens is that you
block so many photons that you cause a quantum mottle - or noisy image. This is
classically described as happening when the grid is aligned incorrectly (put on upside-down,
off level, off center, or off focus / at a bad source to image distance).
Air Gap Technique — There is another method similar to a grid that involves separating the
patient from the film / receptor. In this strategy scatter radiation decreases not from
filtration but from the radiation missing the target. More on this in the mammo chapter.
Dose Increases
(1) Using a Grid
(as opposed to
not using a Grid)
(2) Using a
Higher Grid Ratio
63

mA and kVp: This is where the money is. Quantum Mottle’s mortal enemy is more photons.
Less quantum mottle = less noise. So logically increasing both the mA and kVp would
decrease mottle - but there is some confusion and some potential trickery.
If given the choice between increasing mA or increasing kVp to decrease noise you should
choice increase mA. Why not kVp ??? I thought there was a rule of 15% and all that bullshit.
Yes... increasing kVp does increase the exposure more the mA - pound for pound, BUT if
you go too high you will shift over into Compton scatter territory and actually create more
nose via scatter. So technically increased kVp would probably decrease mottle - but may
increase overall noise. You can see how this would lead to a very poorly written multiple
choice question.
• Not enough mAs = Quantum Mottle
• Excessive kVp = Excessive Scatter Radiation and a loss of contrast.
Testable Trivia: Noise will increase as the distance between the tube and detector increase,
with the increase in noise described by the inverse square law.
Noise Reduction - Part C - “Increasing Dose”
-Dealing with Quantum Mottle-
What is this “inverse square law ” ? Essentially
this says that energy twice as far from the
source is spread out over four times the area (in
other words l/4th the intensity).
Signal to Noise Ratio (SNR)
This is a quantitative method for comparing the signal (stuff that contributes to
the image) vs the noise (stuff that doesn’t contribute to the image). If your
signal is really strong - you can tolerate more noise. If your signal is weak -
even a little bit of noise can wreck your image. Sometimes the term "contrast
to noise ratio” is used with a similar meaning.
This is how people typically talk about noise. If you say I’m improving/reducing
the “noise” - you are really improving the SNR. It’s a ratio. Things like higher
kVp may increase scatter - but do also improve overall SNR. I’ll talk more
about this in later chapters.
64

Gamesmanship Summary:
If the question is - "which of the following will reduce noise ?” All of the
following would be correct:
• Use a Grid
• Use an AirGap (this works like a grid — it’s mostly a mammo thing)
• Increase the mA
If all the choices seem correct and you have to pick just one - I’d pick increase
the mA. How could that happen? If your text writer is using noise and mottle
(photon starvation) interchangeable. Really noise should include scatter - but
there is no guarantee that the people writing the questions actually understand
physics.
Be weary of (1) Questions about field size / collimation questions, because that
mixes the results. Collimation reduces scatter, but it also reduces photons
hitting the target. Typically there is an automatic increase in mA with collimation
so your signal to noise ratio improves - that’s why collimation is good (plus the
reduced dose). But there is the potential for mind reading / fuckery - because
if you don’t increase the mA although you may reduce scatter the overall SNR
may worse secondary to mottle. Just be careful - read the wording of the
question carefully.
Be weary of (2) Any answer choices asking you to reduce noise with post
processing - unless you thinks it’s talking about iterative reconstruction
technique - in which case they better say it. Just be careful, it seems like this
could be a trap. “It’s a trap!” - Admiral Gial Ackbar
If the question mentions a grid - and you suspect a “grid cut off” - the next step
is to reposition the grid. Don’t immediately jump to increasing the mA.
Remember most grid cut offs are from incorrect placement of the grid.
“Slap the tech around” - would also be a good option - if that’s available. It’s
second order, assuming you know the tech put the grid on wrong.
If the question mentions increasing (or decreasing) the distance between
source and detector, remember this relationship is not 1:1 - it’s governed by the
inverse square law - as described on the prior page.

about this as how close two lines can be
to each other and still be visibly resolved
as separate things.

actually 2 things

W hat is this “unsharpness ? ” For all intensive purposes “unsharpness” means a loss of
spatial resolution. PhDs describe 3 subtypes of unsharpness
(1) Motion Unsharpness - This is basically loss of spatial resolution that is attributable to
patient m otion. A good way to ask questions about this would be to say “how do you
decrease motion unsharpness” or address motion artifact? Good Answers would be
(a) To say something like “Sir!! Hold Still!! Yes I know you’re leg is broken - but you
don’t see me screaming!” (b) Tie his ass down, (c) Decrease the exposure time. Less
“s” on the mAs.
(2) System Unsharpness - 1 prefer to blame someone anytime something goes wrong. If I
was an academic I would blame the resident / fellow. Since I’m not an academic I
usually blame the tech or the nurse (for farts I usually blame the dog). In this case
system unsharpness is the fault o f the detector. Every detector type has a limiting
factor which determines the maximum spatial resolution.
• Film = Size of the Grain of Photographic Chemical
• Computed Radiography (CR) = Size of the Laser used to read the phosphor plate
in the cassette reader
• Digital Radiography (DR) = Size of the individual thermoluminescent transistor.
(3) Geometric Unsharpness - This is caused by geometry. *More on the next page.

Modulation Transfer Function (MTF)
Sharpness and Resolution are two terms that are often used synonymously but are slightly
different. “Sharpness” describes the ability of the x-ray system to define an edge. Resolution
describes the ability of the x-ray system to differentiate two closely approximated things.
Modulation Transfer Function (MTF) describes the relationship between sharpness and
resolution. This is a confusing and often poorly taught subject that simultaneous seems to be
high yield on multiple choice exams. I will now give my best effort to help you understand
this M F’r (Modulation Function).
Let us first try and understand what “MTF” is actually describing. Imagine you have a jar of
peanut butter and you want to know how much peanut butter is in the jar because that will
help you budget not starving to death on your resident salary. You look on the label but it’s
in grams and because you are a real American you don’t use the fucking metric system - take
that shit back to Canada or France. That label should say ounces! So you are forced to
“transfer” the peanut butter one teaspoon at a time into another jar which is properly labeled
in ounces - like peanut butter should be. At the end of the transfer there will still be a small
amount of peanut butter stuck to the sides of the jar. So, when you count up the total number
of ounces in the second container the number you get will be slightly less than the original
amount of peanut butter you started with. Your “transfer” has been “modulated. ”
If you were going to describe this as a mathematical “function ” you might say
Peanut Butter in the First Jar (labeled in grams)
MTF (peanut butter) — -------------------------------------------
Peanut Butter in the Second Jar (labeled properly in ounces)
You would get some ratio less than 1. You could never get a number greater than 1.0 because
you couldn’t end up with more peanut butter than you started. A perfectly disciplined peanut
butter transfer would land you at exactly 1 .0 , but we both know that is just not possible (you
gotta lick those fingers).
Now - in Radiology the transfer we are talking about is the original / perfect x-ray data that
has exited the patient to the product that the digital detector / film has produced. Just like the
peanut butter transfer the data is never perfectly replicated ( 1 .0 ).
Information Recorded
MTF (image) = -------------------------------------------
Information Available
