70

D etective Quantum Efficiency (DQE)
DQE is essentially an estimate of the required exposure level that will be necessary to create
an optimal image. In other words, it’s a “prediction of dose.” DQE is evaluated by
comparing the image noise of a detector with that of the “ideal detector,” and in that way it is
a measure of the efficiency of a detector in converting x-ray energy into an image signal.
People who like math calculate this by comparing the signal to noise ratio at output with the
signal to noise ratio at input as a function of spatial frequency. In a perfect world you want
this to be “ 1 .0 ” (all radiation energy is absorbed and converted to image), at any spatial
resolution - but this isn’t what happens. Factors that influence it are the radiation exposure,
spatial frequency (resolution), modular transfer function, detector material, kVp, and mAs.
The better your DQE the less radiation you need to maintain your signal.
• High DQE = Low Dose
• Low DQE = High Dose
Here are some things to remember about DQE:
• It’s a measurement of efficiency
• DQE is directly proportional to MTF
• DQE is inversely proportional to the Signal to Noise Ratio
• DQE is better at low spatial resolution
• In general the DQE of DR is around 0.45, significantly better than that of CR or plain
films - which is around 0.25. ** more on this in the next chapter.
DQE and spatial resolution:
DQE is inversely proportional to spatial resolution (frequency).
Why would that be? Just reason that you would need more dose (more photon density) to see
fine details. If you are asking the system to see more fine detail - then you are going to need
more juice per square inch - therefore a lower DQE.
Remember : High DOE = Low Dose
71

Spatial Resolution Concepts Related to Digital Im aging
I’m going to talk all about the digital imaging receptors in the next chapter, but for completing
the discussion of spatial resolution I want to touch on a few points regarding the hardware. I’ll
rehash this again in the next chapter so if it doesn’t make sense you don’t need to spaz it will all
make sense by the time you finish the book (probably).
Even though film is ancient technology relative to digital imaging one of the few (probably only)
advantage it sort of has is in spatial resolution.
Film has the advantage of being composed of a continuous thing, where as digital receptors have
discrete information in the form of pixels. Now these pixels are really fucking small (they are
measured in microns), but they are still separate things. I’ll touch on this again later but the pixel
size is a function of the matrix (more pixels in a matrix - the smaller they are). The more pixels
you have per unit area “pixel density’’ the better your spatial resolution. Another vocabulary
word worth knowing is “pixelpitch” - which is the measurement from the center of one pixel to
the next (more easily understood as the pixel spacing).
• Testable Point: A major determinant of spatial resolution of digital images is the pixel size
and spacing (pixel pitch).
• Testable Point: Increased Pixel Density = Better Spatial Resolution
• Testable Point: Decreased Pixel Pitch = Better Spatial Resolution
72

(3) Contrast (“Contrast Resolution”)
I introduced the concept of contrast in the prior chapter by showing you 3 lions of different
shades standing next to each other. Remember the general idea is that it is easier to tell two
thing apart if they are totally different shades (black and white) and it is more difficult to tell
things apart when they are close to the same shade (one shade of grey vs a slightly lighter
shade of grey). This is the idea of imaging contrast. More is good.
kVp - Remember that the kVp (kilovoltage peak) doesn’t just play a role in how much the
beam penetrates but also plays a large role in image contrast because of the photoelectric
effect and k edge concepts discussed in prior chapters. Another way a person who writes
multiple choice questions may ask this is by using the term “quality.” Remember that quality
is a kVp thing (where as quantity is a mAs thing ... and a kVp thing).
• Testable Point: Low kVp = High Contrast
• Testable Point: High kVp = Low Contrast
Scatter - Anything that increases scatter reduces contrast. So use of a Grid and
Collimation (smaller field of view) reduces scatter, and improves contrast resolution.
Vocab Radiographic Contrast” - Radiographic contrast is a result of both “subject
contrast” and “image receptor contrast” (or film contrast - if the year is 1973).
Film Contrast - This is controlled by the design and manufacturing of the film components.
“Sensitometric curves” are a thing that is used to describe the inherent quality of the film -
which compare changes in density and exposure etc... If I lived in rural Africa or the year
was 1973 then 1 might give a shit about this. Since neither of those things are true - 1 give
exactly zero shits and more importantly I think it’s very low yield for the test. Just know that
film and the subject play a role and they had curves to describe this quality in film.
Vocab “High vs Low Subject Contrast” - Certain anatomy regions are intrinsically referred
to as either “High” or “Low” contrast. For example, the chest has bones and lung - two
things with very different density (and Z) resulting in big contrast difference - white bones
next to black lungs. The chest is a region of “high subject contrast.” An example of “low
subject contrast” would be the belly - where you have soft tissue organs, next to other soft
tissue organs and everything is a blob of grey. The breast is also a classic example of low
contrast, where the difference in tissue composition / density between parenchyma and
cancer are very very small.
73

On the prior page, I defined the term “subject contrast ” Let’s look at an example of how this could work
if we only considered the variable of thickness. For example, two blobs of fat on a table. One that is 10
cm thick, next to another that is only 3 cm thick.
Choosing the correct kVP is critical to exploiting the difference in this regions and allowing for a balance
between dose and adequate diagnostic quality.
In this example, think about contrast as the the ratio of penetrating x-rays that can get through a thin part
over a thick part when exposed to the same kVp.

Frat Version: Higher kVp —» Smaller Attenuation Coefficients —> Less Contrast
74

Image Receptor Contrast - Digital
imaging has an advantage over film
with regard to contrast. I’ll touch on
this again in the next chapter - but it’s
worth knowing the depth of the pixel
is determined by the number of bits
(10, 12, 14 etc..). This determines the
number of shades of gray available for
imaging. More bits = more contrast
resolution.
Window Width - I’ll talk about this in more depth in the CT chapter - but I am going to touch
on it here too, because it’s an important contrast topic. You can adjust the radiographic
contrast - post processed - by adjusting the range of densities visible.
• Testable Point: A narrow (decreased) window width increases contrast.
• Testable Point: A wider (increased) window width decreases contrast.
• Testable Point: The center point (or midpoint) of the level is going to determine the
brightness
Digital Imaging Specific Concept “The LUTs”- A major difference between plain film and
digital is the primary factor influencing contrast. With plain film kVp is the primary factor.
With digital the primary factor influencing contrast is the Look-Up Table or “LUT.”
WTF is a “LU T”? The LUT is a histogram of known input intensities and corresponding
grayscale. A rescaling process allows for adjustment to present an optimal image
(underexposed images are scaled darker, overexposed images are scaled lighter). As a result
it’s less of a problem to adjust the kVp to correct for dose when you are using digital - rather
than plain film.
Key Points Regarding Digital Imaging / LUTs
• The primary factor influencing image contrast (in digital systems) is the LUT.
• The primary factor influencing image contrast (in film systems) is the kVp.
• kVp still influences contrast, but digital systems have a much wider dynamic range.
• Imaging-processing software is capable of producing an image with a specified level of
contrast even when higher kVp
• A higher kVp can be used (adjusted by the 15% rule) and a quality image still produced.
WTF is a “B it” ? Digital Transistors (like a Sith
Lord) only deal in absolutes, it’s either a zero
(off) or a one (on). Each 0 and 1 is called a bit
and refers to the computer’s basic unit of
information. These binary digits are used to
display the shades of gray in a digital image.
The greater the number of bits - the more shades
of gray available.
75

(4) Brightness
Brightness can mean slightly different things depending on if you are talking about film or
digital. For film “brightness” refers to how many x-rays are blocked. In other words, “bones are
bright.” White Stuff = Bright Stuff.
Digital is a little more complicated. Well not really... for the purpose of multiple choice what
you need to know (or may not need to know) isn’t very fucking much. It’s like this much -
imagine me, a 7 foot tall red bearded man with steel blue eyes, holding up my hand with my
thumb and my index finger pretty close to each other. This much...
Q: Prometheus are you really 7 foot tall???
A: Of course 1 am! A short person could never write a physics review book this good!
Here is the idea - if you have managed to make a digital image then you are gonna be reading it
on a monitor (not a stupid light box). When you are dealing with a monitor “brightness ” refers
to the amount of luminance (light emission) of the display.
Digital imaging provides a wider dynamic range than film screen. Because of this you have the
ability to adjust for exposure errors, and make acceptable image densities - optimized for the
human eyeball. This process of optimizing is “windowing.” Specifically, the window level (or
center). This lets you set a midpoint in the range of densities that are visible on the image.
Setting the level (center) allows the image brightness to be increased or decreased throughout the
entire range of densities. Level Up (high pixel value) lets you see darker regions (like the lung)
better - by increasing the overall brightness. Level Down (lower pixel value) lets you see lighter
regions better.
Frat Version / Trivia Summary:
• Digital imaging provides a wider dynamic range than film screen
• Window Level (or Center) = Thing you change for Brightness
• Window Width = Thing you change for Contrast
• Level Up (higher pixel value) = For lookin’ at dark stuff (lungs)
• Level Down (lower pixel value) = For lookin’ at light stuff (bones)
76

Prometheus, if a film is overexposed does it mean both
mA and kVP are high or can it be either or both?
An overexposed film can be described as having a “high density number” or suffering from
“blackening” of the film. In other words, too many x-ray photons have made it to the film /
detector.
As we have discussed, the density and contrast of an image on film is controlled by the kV,
mAs and other exposure factors. Having said that, in general an over exposed image will have
an mAs that is too high, and/or a focal spot to detector/film distance that is too small. The kV
primarily determines the contrast of an image - but does play a role in putting X-rays on the
detector (higher energy = more on the detector). However, there are contrast factors to
consider depending on the structure you are trying to optimize.
Short Answer = increasing the mA and the kVp will both make the image darker - however
you want to use kV to maximize your image contrast. If forced to choice a single factor I
would probably pick mA - but this would be a very shitty question.
77

Techs talk a lot about “film density” - or how black the image is. The training
they get basically has them focus on balancing “density” (blackness) and
contrast.
Part 1: “Density”
mAs is the weapon of choice for controlling density.
Film isn’t “black” enough = increase the mAs.
How much? Techs use a thing called the “30% rule.” This is different than the
“15%” rule for kVp and mAs. The “30% rule” states that you have to increase
the mAs by at least 30% if you want to make a visible change in density.
kVp also has an affect on density. Increasing the kVp will increase the quantity
(and quality) of the x-ray spectrum. But, remember that a shift into higher kVp
ranges is going to result in increased Compton scatter (relative to PE
interactions). Another issue that is stressed in Tech training is that increases in
kVp are not all equal. You get more bang for your buck increasing the kVp at
lower energy levels.
Example: 40 kVp increased to 50 kVp = big increase in blackness (density).
Example: 95 kVp increased to 105 kVp = trivial increase in blackness.
Changing kVp while maintaining density (blackness) is performed with the
15% rule. As discussed previously, that would mean that if you decreased the
kVp by 15%, you would need to double the mA to keep the same degree of
“blackness” on the film.
Image Optimization Tactics
-Things You’d Do If You Were a Tech-
Fat People and Fat Parts:
In general, Techs will say that every 4 cms of patient tissue will require them
to double the mA - starting from a standard mA technique for that particular
study.

A few things worth noticing. First, when you have a structure that has high
internal contrast (lungs vs ribs - in a CXR) you can use a higher kVP because
you don’t need to accentuate that intrinsic contrast as much. When we look at
the abdomen (which in this example is similar in thickness) you can see the
kVp has dropped. Abdominal soft tissues are closer in density so you need to
rely on your P.E. interactions. Remember, those will occur more at a lower keV.
More P.E. will also mean less scatter. Scatter is the bad guy (Skeletor). Also,
notice the mA was increased on the ABD film. This was done to maintain
“density / blackness” on the film given the lower kVp.
The grid is an awesome tool for reducing scatter. If you are wondering why the
foot x-ray isn’t getting a grid, it’s because the foot is much thinner. Thicker
parts make more scatter. Thinner things (extremities, babies) typically don’t
get a grid. Grids are typically used when the object is thicker than 10 cm or
you are using more than 60 kVp. Don’t forget the grid will add dose.
79

Image Optimization Tactics
-Things You’d Do If You Were a Tech-
Part 3: “Contrast Continued”
“S/P Ratio” — On occasion you may hear people mention a thing called the
"S/P Ratio. ” This is a ratio of the Secondary X-Rays (scatter) vs the Primary X-
Rays (useful information) that strike the film / detector.
So “improving your S/P Ratio” means reducing scatter. Low numbers are better
(because the bad guy is the numerator).
S/P Ratio essentially depends on two things:
(1) The Thickness of the Body Part, and
(2) The Area of the Beam. Thicker bodies and bigger areas are going to
create more scatter - and increase the S/P ratio.
Ways to Reduce Scatter (Improve Contrast):
1 - Collimate: This reduces the area of the beam.
2 - Compress the Part: This reduces the thickness (this is why mammograms
are done in compression).
3 - Lower kVp: This reduces Compton scatter (increase P.E.) - trade off is
increased dose (skin*)
4 - Grid / Air Gap: Reduces the number of scatter photons that strike the film /
detector.
Part 4: “Clinical Scenarios”
Scenario 1: The Patient is a Big Fat Ass - Whale / T-Rex
*Remember Techs will says that every 4 cms of patient tissue will require
them to double the mA.
*Using a higher kVp of a “thicker” patient will only add scatter.
80

Image Optimization Tactics
-Things You’d Do If You Were a Tech-
Part 4: “Clinical Scenarios Continued”
Scenario 2: Newborns
When you x-ray an infant you are going to do a couple of things differently
(which makes them testable).
(1) You do NOT use a Grid.
(2) You lower the kVp - “good technique is around 65 kVp.” They are
small and don’t require a lot of juice to penetrate. *Most adult CXRs are
around 120-140 kVp.
(3) You use around the same or lower mAs - “good technique is around
2-4 mAs.” Adults are typically around 4 mAs (portables are usually
closer to 2.5).

Digital vs Analog
Long ago, when dinosaurs roamed the earth, radiology was done on film screen. Now in the
modem age it is digital. The comparison of film screen vs digital radiology could lend itself
to multiple choice questions.
“Larger Dynamic Range”
One of the major limitations of film is its limited dynamic range. What that means is that there is
a limit to the range of exposure intensities that it can accurately detect. In other words - at the
extreme ends (not enough and too much) film screen sucks, leaving it very sensitive to under or
overexposure. The consequence of that is more repeat studies (more radiation for the patient). A
second consequence of that is for situations when you want to look at two structures that differ
greatly in x-ray attenuation (example - bones and soft tissue) - film doesn’t optimize this as well.
PhDs love to diagram this comparison of the dynamic range - this vs that, and I’m a firm believer
that comparative radiology makes for good test questions, so I think it’s worth being familiar
with the graph.
Testable points:
• Digital = Larger Dynamic Range +
Linear Response to Exposure
Detector
• Film = Narrow Dynamic Range +
Response
Curvilinear Response to Exposure
X-Ray Exposure
I touched on this at the end of the last chapter (repetition = memory); remember that because
digital has this larger dynamic range you can correct moderately under or overexposed
images by adjusting the brightness (via the window level / center). I also mentioned in the
prior chapter that you can try and fix the contrast by adjusting the window width.
Trivia: Digital lets you try and fix contrast and brightness. If you have film you have to
break out the fucking “hot light” - yes that was a thing - google it.
Dynamic Range
Digital
(Linear)
Film
(Curvilinear)
84

Prom etheus, I don’t understand why film screen is not able to optimize seeing bone
and soft tissue (structures that differ greatly in X-ray attenuation) ?
By popular demand, I’m gonna make an attempt to
explain this confusing topic.
Before we discuss the limitation, we should quickly
review this concept of “contrast.” When we talk about
contrast we are referring to the differences in density
(white vs black) that are present on a single image.
For example, if you have big differences (example 1)
you would say the image has “high radiographic
contrast.” If you have a more narrow range of
differences (example 2 ) you would say the image has
“low radiographic contrast.”
Example 2 :
So why might one image have high contrast and another image have low contrast? Well, in the
setting of an old fashion film screen it is going to depend on multiple factors including the
choice of intensifying screen (the actual film is a poor x-ray detector) and kVp. A high
contrast exam (example - MSK plain film of an extremity) would be performed by choosing a
narrow film/screen dynamic range and a low x-ray beam energy (kVp).
In general, film screen combinations produce curves that look something like this.
Exposure to X-Rays
Narrow Latitude - High Contrast Potential
Wider Latitude - Lower Contrast Potential
Now, what this is showing is the change in density (how white exposed parts of the image
look) in response to exposure to x-rays. The broader (wider) the latitude the lower the
contrast. In other words, if small difference in exposure result in large differences in density
change, you will see more dramatic shifts from black to white.
85

This is why the fixed narrow dynamic range issue is usually cited as the primary weakness in
film imaging.
If you look at the curve the bottom
part (the toe) will result in an image
with no significant contrast as the
image is UNDER exposure.
The top part of the curve (the
shoulder) will also result in an image
with no significant contrast as this
image is OVER exposed.
So - again this is the primary advantage of digital imaging over screen film. The wide
dynamic range allow you to compensate for sub-optimal settings, and allows you to
maximize contrast regardless of the technique that is used (within reason).
86

Dose
Another element of “Digital vs Analog" that I want to point out is the superior dose efficiency - given:
(1) The wider dynamic range, which would allow you to underexpose a little bit knowing that you
can correct it by windowing,
(2) You don’t have to depend on kVp so very much for contrast. With film kVp was super super
important for getting your contrast (remember small differences in Z = big differences in
photoelectric absorption). But with digital, since you can adjust contrast and brightness
afterwards, you can go up on the kVp (by 15%) and half your mA to decrease dose.
Frat Version = Digital has the potential to have lower dose relative to film because of post processing
options.
Gamesmanship = Another way to word this is “Higher Dose Efficiency.” Thats a fancy way to say less
radiation.
Spatial Resolution — “Pixels”
Film radiographs were/are actually made up of tiny deposits of black metallic silver (imported from
the Byzantine Empire), smeared on as a single blob-like unit. This is different than a digital image
which is recorded in individual units called “pixels” as a “matrix.” Unfortunately, no one can be told
what the matrix is... you have to see it for yourself.
OK fine. The matrix is a combination of very small (usually) “picture elements” arranged into rows
and columns. These “picture elements” are your “pixels”. Think of each one of these pixels as a
recorded numerical value, which is going to be used to represent a brightness level on the display. The
matrix size varies - so the amount of pixels you have varies. For example, a 1024 matrix has
1,048,576 individual pixels (1024 x 1024).
So as I mentioned previously pixel size is a function of the matrix (more pixels in a matrix - the
smaller they are). The more pixels you have per unit area {“pixel density”), the better your spatial
resolution. Another vocabulary word worth knowing is “pixelpitch” - which is the measurement from
the center of one pixel to the next (more easily understood as the pixel spacing).
• Testable Point: A major determinant of spatial resolution of digital images is the pixel size and
spacing (pixel pitch).
• Testable Point: Increased Pixel Density = Better Spatial Resolution
• Testable Point: Decreased Pixel Pitch = Better Spatial Resolution
• Testable Point: Plain Film may have better spatial resolution than digital, because they don’t have
the limitation of individual units (pixels).
87

Rapid Review - Differences in Plain Film vs Digital
• Digital has “larger dynamic range” which means you can over or under expose the
film (within reason) and it’s still diagnostic. This means less repeats (so less
radiation overall).
• Digital has a linear response curve, Film has a curvilinear response curve.
• Film depends on kVp for contrast. Digital doesn’t as much - so you can use the
15% rule (increase your kVp by 15%, decrease your mA by 50%) and cut down on
dose.
• Spatial Resolution of Film may still be better than Digital
Autom atic Exposure Control - (AEC)
Honestly, I probably should have described this thing already - but I might need to mention it
later in the chapter so I’ll go head and do it now. During the Cretaceous Period the exposure
timing was done with an actual timer. Now a thing called the automatic exposure control (AEC)
is used. It essentially serves the same role as a timer but operates differently depending on the
patient body part - to determine the ideal amount of exposure. The underlying mechanism is
actually an ionization chamber - which I’ll talk about again in the nukes chapter. Basically, when
x-rays interact with the atoms within the chamber they ionize them (through the same
mechanisms discussed earlier in the book). These ionized electrons are now charged, which
creates a signal to terminate the exposure. The AEC is calibrated to know how much of a charge
is good enough to terminate exposure.
Things to know:
• AEC acts like a timer which decides when to terminate exposure
• AEC works by using an ionization chamber, placed between the patient and the image
receptor
• The duration of the exposure is determined by the density and thickness of the area of the
patient placed over the ionization chamber.
• AEC controls only the quantity of radiation reaching the image receptor - no effect on kYp.
The “timer” function is dealing with the “s” in mAs. (Fluoro Automatic Brightness systems -
can mess with kVp)
• AEC must be re-calibrated if that receptor changes (you upgrade from film to a CR digital
system)

Let us do a quick rundown on some vocab. I already introduced the idea of a matrix and a pixel.
Now is probably the right time to go into a bit of depth on bit depth.
So what is this thing called “bit depth ” ?
Bit Depth
Pixels have what is called “bit depth, ” which is
basically the number of bits that determine the
number of shades of gray that can be displayed on
a computer monitor. For example a 12-bit depth
can display 4096 shades of gray, whereas a 14-
and 16-bit depth can display 16,384 and 65,536
shades of gray, respectively.
Where do those numbers come from ? It is
basically 2 to the “x” power. So 2 12 = 4096.
Take-home point: The greater the pixel bit depth
(the bigger the number) the more shades of gray
available for image display. The more shades of
gray (“punish me”) available to display on a
digital image, the better the contrast resolution.
Just don’t forget your safe word.

89

- CR - Computed Radiography or “The C assette Based System"
Storage Phosphors (CR) : These can work with a
conventional x-ray machine (just like a normal cassette)
The system uses a special phosphor (Barium
Fluorohalides) that does not emit the absorbed energy
as light after interaction with an x-ray. Instead, the x-
ray causes an electron in the phosphor to change to a
metastable state (one that it can hold for several days).
Essentially when the plate is exposed to x-rays some of electrons within the phosphor are
removed. Those liberated electrons get trapped in a “conduction band.” A magical metal called
“europium” helps convince some of the liberated electrons to stay trapped in this conduction
band for hours.
The idea is that the storage phosphor is holding a “latent” x-ray image.
When the cassette is returned back to a docking station a readout is performed. You can think of
this like developing the film. The process is called “photostimulable luminescence ” -
To read it out, the plate is exposed to a laser that sweeps back and forth. The red light of the laser
liberates the trapped electrons and they return to their shells. As they return they release energy as
blue-green light. The light is directed onto a photodetector which then amplifies the light signal
and converts it to an electronic signal. The signal is then digitalized and divided into a matrix (a
bunch of squares). Each one of these squares is called a “pixel.” Remember the size of the matrix
is going to determine your spatial resolution (larger matrix = greater number of pixels , because
each pixel is smaller, = greater spatial resolution).
“Sampling Pitch” is a vocab word that describes the distance between laser positions as it’s
reading out the plate. With direct systems (not CR) it means something slightly different - and
instead refers to the distance between adjacent DELs (Detector Elements).
“Pixel Pitch” is a vocab word I’ve mentioned before that means the distance between adjacent
pixels. Remember this is also important for spatial resolution.
Testable Trivia:
• The amount of light detected is proportional to the intensity of the incident x-ray.
• The photostimulated phosphors have a wide detectable range, tolerating x-ray intensities 100
times higher and 100 times lower than the 5 micro Gy needed for an old school screen film.
• The plate is reset by forceable exposure to bright white light - which erases it. If you forget
to do this you will get ghosting artifacts.
WTF is a “Phosphor ” ?
A phosphor is a substance that
can emit light when exposed to
a radiation source.
“Luminescence,” they call it.
90

- DR - Digital Radiography or “The C assetteless System "
Flat Panel Detectors (DR): When most people say “digital detector,” this is what they are
talking about. It’s much faster than conventional fdm development or CR plate reading.
As mentioned before you can have direct or indirect versions of digital systems. What does
that mean again ? Indirect systems (like CR) have an intermediate step where the latent image
has to be released as light and then converted into a digital signal (0 ’s and l ’s) to be displayed
on the screen.
•
Indirect (scintillators) = X rays — ► Light — ► Charge
•
Direct (photoconductors) = X rays — ► Charge
Indirect: The x-ray activates the Thallium-doped Cesium Iodide (Csl). Csl is the poster
child scintillator.
Scintillators Gonna Scintillate (emit light in
response to an absorbed x-ray)
Light gets emitted - which is then converted
by a photodiode into an electric charge. The
electric charge is in turn captured and
transmitted by the TFT (Thin-Film
Transistor) array to the workstation for
processing / display.
Is a “Scintillator” the same as a “Phosphor” ?
Pretty much - 1 mean you can get into the weeds with some major PhD bullshit trying to
distinguish them. For the purpose of this exam all you need to know is they are processes of
light emission. If you really need to know - this is the way I think about them:
• In radioluminescence, the ionizing radiation produces free electrons which recombine at a
luminescence center to produce luminescence.
• In scintillation, the ionized atom itself relaxes via a series of excited levels to produce
luminescence.
(Readout Process)
Indirect DR System
91

Lateral Dispersion of Light = Loss of Spatial Resolution
A plague upon the indirect method is this idea of lateral light dispersion.
What you hope would happen
What actually happens
Light goes straight down
Lateral Dispersion of the Light
What is this “Lateral Dispersion ” ? This is a problem you run into with indirect
methods that use phosphors. Light tends to diffuse laterally after it leaves the site of
conversion from an x-ray. This creates issues with spatial resolution, which get worse
with increasing thickness of the crystal. However, making the crystal thin has its own
problems as your sensitivity for collecting x-rays is going to drop off (they just fly right
through).
Is there a difference in “Lateral Dispersion ” between types o f phosphors? Yes -
Gadolinium Oxysulfide has more lateral dispersion (light scatter) than Csl.
How does one solve the problem o f “Lateral Dispersion ” ? Luckily, someone much
smarter than you or me already did. They invented a columnar structure that forms a
“lead pipe” like matrix. With this design you can make a nice thick crystal without
worry. Having said that - a better answer would probably be to use a Direct System
which doesn’t deal with phosphors / scintillation etc...
92

Direct:
As mentioned on the prior page, one of the issues with indirect methods is this extra step where
x-rays are converted to light first, prior to being converted to an electric signal. This step is
removed with direct methodology.
• This uv That: Indirect uses a scintillator. Direct uses a photoconductor
• This vs That: Indirect uses Cesium Iodine (a scintillator). Direct uses Amorphous Selenium,
(the photoconductor)
• This vs That: Both Indirect and Direct use a TFT read out array
How it Works:
Step 1: Apply a homogeneous “bias” charge to
the surface of the selenium.
Step 2: Fire the x-rays through the patient, into
the selenium. The x-rays are absorbed by the
selenium and electrons are released.
Step 3: Released electrons ( “electron hole pair”)
travel to the surface of the selenium and
neutralize a portion of the applied charged. This
is done in proportion to the radiation intensity.
An important testable point is there is no lateral
dispersion.
Step 4: These electrical charges are drawn in
along the electric field lines to the charge storage
capacitor electrodes connected to the TFT.
Step 5: The pattern of charges is scanned and
converted to a digital signal stored by each TFT.
Amorphous Selenium
The material is magical, likely originating from the underground dwarf mines of middle
earth. Selenium is essentially a photoconductor. When it is exposed to radiation, its
electrical conductivity is altered in proportion to the intensity of the radiation.
It is very susceptible to humidity and temperature.
Don’t try and use it outside in the Amazon jungle.

F ill Factor
What is this “Fill Factor” ? This is the area of the detector which is sensitive to x-rays (in
relation to the entire detector area). The higher the fill factor, the more efficient the detector.
Fill Factor Differences:
With DR systems, the electric field shaping essentially allows for a fill factor of nearly 100%.
This is not seen with Indirect (CR) systems. Remember, differences between things make
good multiple choice questions.
Direct - Amorphous Selenium
Indirect - Cesium Iodide
Detective Q uantum E fficiency (DQE) -
In the prior chapter, 1 went on and on about this confusing topic. As a rapid recap DQE is
essentially an estimate of the required exposure level that will be necessary to create an
optimal image. In other words, it’s an efficiency measurement. The better your DQE the less
radiation you need to maintain your signal (High DQE = Low Dose). Another important
point is that your DQE struggles as your demand for spatial resolution increases.
As I mentioned above, DR systems have a better fill factor (near 100%). That means they are
more efficient. So you could correctly infer that DR has a higher DQE.
Testable Trivia:
• DQE of DR » CR (and plain film)
• DQE of DR is around 0.45, significantly better than that of CR or plain films - which is
around 0.25.
D isplay Trivia: The typical standard o f care for a digital display is 3 M ega-Pixels.
94

Trivia C harts I Tables
Specific Factors Affecting the Spatial Resolution of CR
• Laser Spot Size: Smaller = Better
• Phosphor Plate Density / Thickness: More Thick = More Light Spreading = Less Resolution
• Sampling Frequency (Rate of Light Sampling): Increasing the sampling frequency results in a
smaller pixel pitch with improves the spatial resolution.
• Imaging Plate: You are supposed to use the SMALLEST plate size reasonable for the anatomic
area of interest. The reason is that for fixed-matrix-size CR systems, using a smaller plate for a
given field of view improves your spatial resolution.
• Increasing x-rays will NOT improve Maximum spatial resolution
Specific Factors Affecting the Spatial Resolution of DR
• Spatial Resolution for DR is Superior to CR because the pixel detector is built into the DR flat
panel -
• Direct systems that avoid lateral dispersion of light have better spatial resolution
• Spatial Resolution for Flat Panel Detectors is limited to the DEL (detector element); smaller
detector elements = better spatial resolution.

Gamesmanship - When you are studying mammo for physics I want you to concentrate on
what makes mammo unique - relative to regular plain old diagnostic x-ray. Remember by
learning via comparison you are training yourself to exclude distractors - a key tactic for
multiple choice exams.
Overview —
Why does mammo need to be different? The difference in attenuation of a
breast cancer and just regular breast tissue is very small. So you have to use lower energy and
a nearly mono-energetic beam to enhance the attenuation differences. Additionally, an
increase in spatial resolution is required to see micro calcifications.
fill®
Low er Energy
Why lower energy? - Remember the photoelectric effect dominates at lower kVp.
Small differences really amplify the chance of the P.E. because of the cubed
mathematic relationship (probability o f P.E. directly proportional to the atomic
number cubed: Z 3 and indirectly with energy 1/E3).
Glandular Tissue
Cancer

D ifferent T arg et
Target Anodes: Mammo uses a molybdenum or rhodium anode (general radiology
uses tungsten).
Why? Remember, characteristic x-rays are “characteristic” because they depend on the
binding energy of the shell involved, and this binding energy is different for different
targets. So its unique... or “characteristic.” Tungsten , which is used in conventional
imaging, has characteristic x-rays around 69.5 keV. Mammo needs energies way
lower than that (ideally between 16 -23 keV). So the solution is to use a low atomic
number target. The classic examples being molybdenum and rhodium. These targets
generate lower K-characteristic x-rays which are required to generate all those false
positives and unnecessary biopsies that mammo uses to fund its section. I mean... to
find the cancers.
The Normal Spectrum o f Molybdenum:
The combination of low kVp and a low Z give
Moly a high peak of characteristic x-rays, with
low Bremsstrahlung. Notice that energy is around
18 keV (instead of 69.5 keV like Tungsten).
99

' J
K-Edge Filtration
K Edge Filtration - A K. edge filter is placed on the outside of the tube with the goal of
creating a nearly mono-energetic beam in the target range of 16-23 keV. Moly is
classically used for this, taking advantage of the 20 keV K edge and subsequent rise in
photoelectric absorption. Energies lower than 15 and greater the 20 get filtered out.
:
Spectrum of Molybdenum with Superimposed Mo Filter
18 keV
18 keV
Rhodium: An alternative to Moly is Rhodium, which has a similar (slightly stronger)
spectrum, and a binding energy of 20.2 keV.
High Yield:
•
Filters block BOTH high and low photons, with the benefit of removing the low
energy photons which only give you dose and the high energy photons which
won’t help with contrast.
•
Rh/Rh is used for larger or denser breasts - because it’s higher energy compared to
Mo/Mo.
•
Mo anode with Rhodium filter is also used to produce an intermediate energy
spectra between a Mo/Mo and Rh/Rh combination.
•
NEVER use a Rh Target (21 kev) with a Mo Filter (20 Kev K edge)
•
Mo anode can also be combined with an aluminum filter, for a harder beam to
penetrate denser breasts.
•
Some digital systems will use Tungsten / Rho and Tungsten / Silver- this creates a
higher energy spectrum for increased penetration and lower dose. Contrast is lost
- but some post processing can bring it back.

Focal Spot Size: As mentioned before, mammo requires a better spatial resolution
than you get with normal imaging. To achieve this you use a smaller focal spot:
High Yield:
• Mammography uses a focal spot of 0.1 and 0.3 mm
• General X-ray uses focal spots of 0.6 and 1.2 mm
A smaller focal spot is not going to tolerate heat as
well. To address this you have to use a lower mA
(otherwise you’d melt the anode). The mA is
limited to 50 for 0.1 mm, and 100 for 0.3 mm.
Another issue is that since you are using a lower mA
you need a longer exposure time. Generally you
still try and keep the exposure time under 2 seconds.

Com pression
Mammography is performed in compression (another unique feature).
Why do they do that? There are lots of advantages. Mainly it makes for a more uniform
tissue - gives you less overlap, less geometric blurring, and better detail. You would have
a TON of call backs if you didn’t do it... even more than you do already .The most testable
advantage is probably the dose / scatter reduction.
Here is the full list of reasons:
• Reduced Thickness = Less Scatter = Lower kVp can be chosen
• Lower kVp and Less Scatter = Improved Contrast
• Reduced Thickness = Less mAs needed = Less Dose
• Breast Doesn’t Move = Less Motion Artifact
• Breast Smashed Closer to Bucky = Less Geometric Magnification
• Less Motion and Less Geometric Mag = Improved Spatial Resolution
• Less Tissue overlap

kVp (both of which intrinsically reduce scatter) a smaller grid ratio is used.
Mammo uses a 4-5 grid ratio (general x-ray uses a 6-16).

There are a few more testable details related to the Mag View - so let us fill in those gaps.
The goal is to magnify the boob - so you can see those calcifications better. You don’t

said that - the “exposure time” is increased to compensate for the greater distance
between the boob and the image receptor.

• Less mA (about a quarter o f conventional “contact” mammo)
• Longer Exposure Time (about triple that o f conventional “contact ” mammo)

C larification 1: M am m ogram s have high o p tical density
com pared to general radiology. W hat does th a t m ean ?
Unfortunately this question requires some discussion of the old fashion screen film. I get
diarrhea cramps when I have to talk about screen films so I’ll try and be brief.
If you can find a text book describing this stuff (usually they are written
in ancient Sumerian) you might find a graph of what used to be called
the “characteristic curve. ” This curve was the optical density and log
of exposure. In other words in showed the relative changes in
^
“blackening” you’d get on the film at different exposure rates - for that g
particular film. You see now all film is the same - the emulsion
thickness, size, distribution of silver halide crystals... all these things
change the characteristics of the film, and therefore it’s “characteristic
curve.”
Mammography film was traditionally composed of a single emulsion with very fine crystals and
as a results had a very high sensitivity. You might hear people say the mammography film has a
“high D Max” or high maximum optical density. This is useful especially at the periphery of
the breast (the skin) where the thickness rapidly decreases.
C larification 2: W hy does m am m a have a higher recep to r a ir
kerm a (AK) com pared to general radiology ?
As a refresher, AK is the density of x-rays (how many x-rays per unit of space - i.e. square
millimeter). The further away from the source you get the less of them there is (they spread out).
This obeys the inverse square law. Not all Air Kerma is the same. For example:
• Entrance Air Kerma is the radiation incident on the patient
• Receptor Air Kerma is the radiation used to generate the image.
Receptor AK is gonna be less than entrance, as the x-rays get reduced by the attenuation of the
patient and how far away the receptor is from the source. So, it is a little complicated but
mammography is done at a closer distance. For example, the source to image distance for
mammography is usually around 65 cm, vs the 100-cm SID used in normal radiography.
Remember the inverse square law, even a little distance makes a big difference.
107

D igital M am m ography
Digital systems have the major advantage of allowing you to change the contrast and
brightness after the acquisition of the image by adjusting the window and level.
Pixel Size and Spatial Resolution: Remember that smaller pixels = better spatial resolution.
Additionally, you can lose spatial resolution from spread of electronic or light signal inside
the digital or computed detectors.
In general, most digital systems have lower spatial resolution (around 5-11 lp/mm) -
relative to analog (1 1 -1 3 lp/mm).
•
Another point of trivia is that the MQSA does NOT have specific line pair
requirements for digital; instead they are linked to the manufacturer’s specs.
Dose: Digital machines have 15% less dose compared to analog (1.8mGy for analog, 1.6 for
digital). This is because the beam quality is better. Digital also has fewer repeat exams
(because you can window and level). Also, the dose is not fixed - like it is with screens; you
can turn the juice up and down as needed.
Noise Limited Images: The primary factor affecting the total noise is the number of photons
interacting with the detector. Although digital images can have variable contrast, the noise is
fixed after the exposure is taken.
Dark Noise: This is an additional type of noise from electronic fluctuations within the
detector element. The effect is proportional to the temperature of the detector - that is why
coolers are needed. You see these more with underexposed regions.
Flat Field Test: Imaging of a large piece of acrylic. This is done to improve image quality,
and calibrate the digital detectors.
Digital Artifacts:
Ghosting: This is caused by a residual image from the prior exposure - burned into the
detector. You see this when highly attenuating objects are placed in the beam. This is why
lead is not allowed on flat panel digital systems.
Pixels Gone Bad: This can manifest as a square or a streak.
108

This vs T h at - “Last Im age Hold” vs Spot Im age
If you tell the computer to hold the last fluoro image you have on the screen that is
referred to as a “last image hold” or a “Fluoro Frame Shot.” This is different than a “spot
image” which is basically a regular x-ray.
The key differences worth knowing are:
• Spot Image = Conventional X-Ray
• Spot Image = More Dose
• Spot Image = Large Focal Spot (because greater tube current is needed)
• Fluoro Image Shot = More Quantum Mottle (less photons)
• Both use a grid.
• Spatial Resolution of a Fluoro Image Shot = around 2 Line Pairs per mm
• Spatial Resolution of a Spot Image = around 3 Line Pairs per mm
• **Spatial Resolution is better with the Spot Image

The purpose of an image intensifier is to convert x-rays to light and then back to electrons.
Along the course of this conversion, the electron flux and energy are amplified by the image
intensifier with the end result being a greater amount of light emerging from the output
phosphor. To actually view the pictures, you must couple an image intensifier through lenses
to a TV camera.
Noticing the word ‘‘Gain ” is showing up on that diagram? Yup - Lots of “Gains” - all of
which would make good distractors for each other. Complete discussion to follow.
But first, lets have a more in-depth looks at what happens between the Input Phosphor and the
Output Phosphor - the so called “Electrostatic Focusing Lens. ”
114

E lectro static Focusing Lens:
The “electron lens ” is a series of electrodes that are used to focus the electron beam as it flows
from the photocathode towards the output phosphor.
Starting at the Input Phosphor:
• X-Rays have recently been converted to Light.
• Light to be converted into electrons via the Photo Cathode.
• Notice a slight curve in the Input Phosphor?
This is to maintain the same travel distance to the
central region (necessary to undistorted focusing)
Input Phosphor
Focusing Electrodes:
• A series of electrodes work to focus the beam and
speed up the flow (“flux gain”).
Output Phosphor:
• Output Phosphor works to convert electrons
into a pattern of light.
• Note that we are not dealing with X-Rays. Relative to a conventional
phosphor this can be much thinner (because it is electrons to x-rays).
• The benefit of keeping this thin is improved (or maintained) resolution in the shrunken
“minified” image.
Output Phosphor
When landing on the output phosphor the image is:
1) Inverted and Reversed
This occurs as a result of “point inversion” - as all the electrons pass through the same focal
point in their journey from input to output phosphor.
2) Reduced in Size.
This process increases the light photons about 50x relative to the input phosphor.

Gain
“Brightness Gain” - This is an old term describing the ability of the I.I to convert x-ray
energy into light energy and then increase the brightness. Think about this as the increase in
emitted light from the I.I. compared to the amount of light you would have obtained if the I.I.
was never invented (and you just directly exposed the phosphorescent screen).
Testable Point: Brightness Gain is the combined effects of Flux Gain and Minification Gain.
Brightness Gain = Flux Gain x Minification Gain
“Flux Gain”
This describes the increase in magnitude
of light coming from the output phosphor,
relative to the input.
This increase in magnitude is
accomplished with a high voltage between
the photocathode and the output phosphor
(25-35 kV). The voltage causes electrons
to accelerate and yields a gain in energy.
Frat Version: Accelerating Electrons
Increases Brightness
“Minification Gain”
Electrons from the large photocathode
surface are concentrated on a small
output phosphor. This increases the
number of electrons per unit area, and
therefore the energy per unit area.
Q: When you “Mag” something you are
actually doing what?
A: Minifying it less
Q: Does minifying make the image
brighter?
A: Yes - because it is the same number
of electrons being concentrated on a
smaller surface area.
Frat Version: Reducing Image Size
(Output vs Input) Increases Brightness
**/’// review all this again later in the chapter.
“Conversion Gain” (Gx)- This is a term describing the efficiency of an I.I. in changing
incident x-rays into light at the output surface. This is considered to be a “new term” that is
often used to replace brightness gain as an expression of efficiency. An important concept is
that the older an I.I. is, the more it sucks at this.
116

Dealing w ith th e E lderly
Nothing is worse than having an elderly relative. It’s such a drag to have to try and keep
them from falling, and pooping everywhere. Now, obviously we still want them to be full
codes - even if they have metastatic cancer and end-stage Alzheimers — gotta keep cashing
those Social Security checks. It is definitely ok to ablate 1.0 cm RCCs in the demented
population as well - it’s like 40 RVUs.... uh I mean... it’s good for patient care. But I think
we can all agree that it’s best if they are locked up in old people jail (“nursing homes” we call
them).
Just like a human... the I.I. also falls apart with advanced age and becomes a burden.
On the prior page, I mentioned the terms “brightness gain ” and “conversion gain ” as
measures of efficiency - which are sorta similar. Regardless of which term you are using, the
important trivia to remember is that the ability of the intensifier to increase brightness
deteriorates with the age of the tube.
Q: What are the consequences o f this?
A: More and More Dose is Requiring to Produce the Same Level of Output Brightness.
Critical Point: Older I.I. = More Dose.
So what do you do about this old I.I. situation?
• Solution 1: Use an aperture with a large hole in it. The downside to this is it increases the
image noise.
• Solution 2: Let the Automatic Brightness Control system do its job and crank up the juice
(use more radiation). The downside to this is it increases the dose.
• Solution 3: Put it in a nursing home (old person jail), then get another one. The general
rule for this is the Image Intensifier is usually replaced when the conversion gain falls to
50%. The down side to this is that it costs money to replace things, but it’s ok you can
offset the cost by cutting the Resident’s benefits / salary....and hire a million$/year VP or
chairman to oversee the cost savings transition.
117

Geometric vs Electronic Magnification
Not all Mag is created equal. There are two primary types - and obviously the difference
is testable.
Geometric Magnification - This is the type of magnification that I discussed previously
in the the book (most recently in the mammo chapter).
As a quick refresher:
to magnify
something, you
generally bring it
closer to the x-ray
source
Source to Object
Distance
MORE Magnification
Magnification
Object to
Detector
Distance
People usually think about the “inverse square law” for reducing radiation, but obviously it
works the other way too. If you get closer to the tube you get more radiation— and it
doesn’t double, it squares.
Electronic Magnification (Zoom) - If you decrease the input field of view by half, then
only one fourth of the input phosphor will be irradiated. If all other parameters are held
constant, the brightness will also drop to one quarter. This gets the attention of the
automatic exposure control which will ramp up the juice (on average 1.4 - 2.Ox dose per
setting increase).
Testable Trivia: Both Electronic and Geometric Magnification Increase Dose (Geometric
increases it more). *Remember focal spot blur is going to occur with Geometric.
Quick and Easy way to reduce dose = Don’t use Mag unless you need to.
Automatic Brightness Control System (ABC).
I mentioned this thing earlier. Remember it is a device
that works to optimize image exposure. In fluoro -
think about it watching the light from the output
phosphor and adjusts accordingly - for contrast and
density (via turing up the mA, kVp, or both). Note that
in regular X-Ray automatic exposure control systems
primarily messed with the mA (quantity) and not so
much the kVp (quality). For fluoro, you typically only
see ABC used during electronic mag.

More Meat
- S patial Resolution vs M agnification -
This can be confusing, so let me spend a little extra time on it. I’ll start by talking about
electronic or zoom magnification.
The image intensifier can, via various forms of magic and spell casting, vary the size of the input
radiation field, but still keeping the output field the same size.
If for example the input field is cut in half then the size of the object being viewed is also cut in
half but the output is still fixed. So relative to the original setting, the object has undergone a 2x
magnification - and a double in spatial resolution.
**Remember the dose goes up because halving the input field o f view, while keeping all the other
factors the same, would cut the image brightness to a 1/4 o f the original brightness. To
compensate for this, the amount o f radiation at the input o f the image intensifier must be
quadrupled to compensate for reduction in juice. More Radiation = More Dose. This kind o f
trade off is highly testable. Question writers love this stuff - really gets their dicks hard.

The automated brightness device used in fluoro is making changes for both contrast and density, and as a result it
is making changes in mA, kVp, or both. This is a little different then how it works in regular x-ray automatic
exposure - where it is mostly a change in mA and not so much kVp. Because it's a mixed change - the AVERAGE
increase in dose (per setting) will increase 1.4 -2.0 times.
If we are talking about raw numbers in exposure (quantity) then in needs to be quadrupled as explained above.
So - to be clear: The real dose increase via the ABC (for both contrast and density) is on average 1.4-2.0x. The
theoretical quantity increase needed to maintain brightness is quadrupled. The take home message is dose goes
up.

(2) Keep the focus-film distance as large as possible

Another Way to Conceptualize This:
If the objects are moved farther away from the detector, the divergence of the beam will increase
and allow the objects to be projected onto different detector elements. Therefore resulting in
greater ability to resolve them as two (instead of one) which is the definition of spatial
resolution.
T
This leads me into my final point on this topic. The idea of ‘''Receptor Unsharpness. ’
Technically are three contributors to “unsharpness.’
(1) Geometric - the stuff we have been discussing
(2) Patient - Patients are not the ideal mathematical target. This is because you have a
thickness that is going to vary - typically decreasing x-ray attenuation towards the edges.
The gradual change in attenuation at the edges can (and does) produce an indistinct edge.
You also have organs moving inside the body which create blur and contribute to unsharp
edges. You get the idea...
(3) Receptor - Limitations in the ability of the system to record exact resolution. I think
about this like a wooden ruler. How long is an inch? (hold up fingers).... But is it really
that long? Get a microscope and the line on the ruler is wavy right? So you need a laser
for exact measurements. Remember real precision is based on the speed of light and all
that bullshit from undergrad physics — i.e. the meter is technically defined as the
distance travelled by light in a vacuum in 1 / 299,792,458 seconds.
121

OK ...so where am I ’m going with this ? You can think of receptor unsharpness as the ultimate
limit on resolution.
Resolution in a digital system is limited by the size of the pixels of the captured image.
The relationship between how good the receptor “intrinsic Receptor resolution” is and the Focal
spot size (R/F) will ultimately determine if geometric magnification will increase or decrease the
spatial resolution.
If R/F is greater than about 0.5, then magnification radiography will reduce unsharpness, a value
less than 0.5 and magnification will increase unsharpness.
For the purpose of multiple choice, when dealing with general radiography R/F « 0.5 (intrinsic
receptor resolution ~0.1 mm, focal spot size ~1 mm) so resolution decreases (blurring increases)
with increasing magnification.
In mammography with 0.3 mm (contact) and 0.1 mm (magnification) that focal spot size -is
gonna be small enough that you are going to achieve a ratio that is allows for unsharpness to
decrease.

Dose & C om pensation For G eom etric Mag:
So, if geometric mag is so bad - what do you do about it?
You can try and collimate. Collimation is electronically controlled with positioning changes
made to irradiate a small region of anatomy - you change to a smaller input surface. The trick
is that you are actually minifying the image less, as a smaller input surface is projected on the
same output surface.
Best Position o f the I.I. and X-ray Tube? X- ray tube far away, with the I.I. close.
Having the image intensifier as close to the
patient as possible does 3 things:
(1) Decreases patient dose.
•The ABC tries to make pretty pictures and
more incident x-rays are detected when the 1.1.
is close so the ABC doesn’t increase mA (to
increase the number of x-rays generated) to
make an adequate image.
(2) Decreases scatter to the operator.
•Less x-rays are made to scatter and less scatter
escapes to irradiate the operator.
(3) Increases image sharpness. Decreases focal
spot blur and magnification.
Raising the Imaging Receptor will ? Increases the dose - the machine will increase dose to
compensate for the “source to image” receptor distance
What is the general radiation change with each magnification? -Increases dose by 1.4 — 2.0
times -You get less light out, the ABC turns up the juice to maintain the picture
Where is the ideal place to stand? On the same side of the patient as the imaging intensifier
-It is preferable to position the C arm x-ray tube under the table
Double the distance from the tube does what to dose? Decreases it by a factor of 4 (inverse
square law).

A S m aller Field of V ie w Does W hat to Dose?
So, the important (testable) distinction here is smaller FOV from collimation alone vs smaller
FOV from magnification.
Sm aller FOV - Collim ation
Collimation is a type of beam restriction. In the most general sense, a
restrictor is a device that “restricts” the x-ray beam at the opening of the x-ray
tube. They come in three main flavors with collimators being the most
testable.
Collimators do two things well and have one negative side effect.
• Pro: They Reduce Dose
• Pro: They Reduce Scatter
• Con: They Alter Exposure
• Dose Reduction:
The smaller the field of view, the less volume of the patient is irradiated. Remember that even a
little bit of collimation goes along way because area is a square function.
• Scatter Reduction:
The smaller the field of view, the less scatter radiation (per unit
size). The opposite is true also - bigger FOV, more scatter. Now,
this is not the same with primary radiation.
Think about it like this picture =>
Scatter is like an “additional” radiation tacked onto the primary
cost (which is not change by collimation alone).
• Altered Exposure Factors:
With the reduction in scatter we also have a decrease in image “blackening.” To maintain
contrast you will have to increase exposure factors. This causes confusion... for the purpose of
multiple choice collimation reduces dose and scatter.

cut the image brightness to a 1/4 of the original brightness. To compensate for this, the amount of

Now... to nearly complete this journey lets discuss the idea of “Brightness Gain.”
Brightness Gain is the product of two unrelated things.
1- Minification Gain = Same energy in concentrated on a smaller target (like the
magnifying glass) increases the density of the energy (Brightness).
2- Flux Gain = A gain in the ability of the electrons in the I.I. system to produce light
photons. You may think, 1 electron in = 1 photon of light on the way out. BUT because
of the acceleration process (1 electron can = 50 light photons). So this amplifies the
process (or reduces it if you are using less electrons).
Text Book Answer: Brightness Gain = Minification Gain x Flux Gain
Practical Answer to Reason Through MCQs = Minification Gain = Brightness Gain
•
Less Minification (Smaller FOV) = Magnified = Less Brightness
More Minification (Larger FOV) = Less Mag = More Brightness

But Prometheus! How Does that Cause More Dose ?
Automatic Exposure Rate Control (AERC) circuit (the
artist formerly known as Automatic Brightness Control
- ABC). This guy has one job... keep the signal to
noise ratio happy. As long as signal to noise ratio is
happy everything will be fine.
It does this by monkeying around with the X-Ray
exposure rate on the input phosphor of the I.I. (or flat
panel detector).
Per my life changing discussion above, the brightness
gain of the I.I. decreases as the magnification increases.
This fucks up the SNR (less signal), and the AERC
circuitry spazes out by boosting the exposure rate.
How much does it increase the rate ?
For the purpose of multiple choice, I would say equal to the ratio of FOV areas.
Example = It’s a typical day on Fluoro. You are performing a post op swallow study on a 600
pound, unemployed, Hep C positive, chain smoker who has eaten through her gastric pouch
procedure (again...). To improve the spatial resolution you decide to change from the standard
30-cm mode to the 23-cm magnification mode.
This change (30 cm to 23 cm) will cause the AERC to increase the x-ray exposure rate equal to
the ratio of FOV areas. In other words, by a factor of (30/23)^ or 1.7x.
Ok... don’t freak out. The chance of having to work the problem out is very unlikely (although
you never know what these clowns will ask). I’m just including this because I want to give you a
very complete answer.

Into the Weeds with Electronic Mag and Dose
To touch on the next bit of fluoro related dose trivia, I need to briefly introduce the idea of
KERMA and Kerma Area Product (KAP). I’ll mention it again later in the text.
KERMA = Kinetic Energy Release per unit MAss.
The best way to understand KERMA is to think of it as the TOTAL amount of energy deposited
from the ionizing radiation. With that TOTAL amount divided by a unit of mass to give it a more
quantifiable dose. Why did I capitalize TOTAL? Because when the radiation strikes a unit mass it
will not only create some movement of particles (kinetic energy), but also some amount of heat.
Just measuring what goes on with the electrons is not the TOTAL amount of energy (there is also
that heat). As a result, some smart people figured out that the best way to measure both of these
components was to measure it at the moment just before energy was transferred. Which brings us
to the concept of “AIR KERMA” - which is an estimation of how many photons are in a unit of
air prior to the energy striking the skin. The idea is that you are estimating the peak skin dose
based on the potential transfer of energy.
Kerma-Area Product (KAP) - Is the amount of Kerma
(potential dose) multiplied by the cross sectional area of the x-
ray beam. This provides a better way to measure the total
radiation potentially incident on the patient. Think of it more
as a measurement of total radiation used in the exam, more
than the actual dose in the patient.
KAP = Dose x Cross Sectional Area
Conceptually understand that if you increase the juice, but
concentrated it in a smaller area that you might not change
your KAP (even though you increased the juice).
Now that I’ve got that out of the way - the testable trivia related to mag and fluoro:
• Electronic Mag increases the Air Kerma (and therefore skin dose). Why ? Should be
intuitive - you turned the juice up. More juice concentrated on a smaller spot.
• Electronic Mag does NOT increase the KAP. Why? Because even though you increased the
juice you also decreased the cross sectional area. They cancel out - keeping the KAP the same.
130

2.

High Level Control
The US Government has one solution to all problems: Regulation - and not the good kind of
regulation Warren G used to talk about (when he hooked a left on Two One and Lewis).
Along those lines, the Uncle Sam has set regulations to limit Air Kerma rates - to minimize peak
skin doses. That regulation is 87 mGy per min.
WTF would they pick 87 and not 90 or 100 ? Seems pretty random. W ell... as is expected,
government bureaucracy continues to lag about 30 years behind reality so they chose an official
exposure limit of 10 “Roentgens” per min - which then coverts to about 87 mGy/min. Just
remember 87 mGy.
• 8.7 mGy or Air Kerma is about 1 Roentgen of Exposure.
But what if your patient is getting disability payments because he/she is too fa t to get out o f bed?
In that situation, even an entrance dose of 100 mGy/min (over the line!) would still likely result
in an inadequate image. Fluoro machines have what is called “high level control, ” which allows
you to turn up the juice on these fat cows. Uncle Sam allows this HLC up to 20 Roentgens/min
(176 mGy/min).
Testable Points:
• Normal Air Kerma Limit = 87 mGy/min (10 Roentgens per min)
• High Level Control (Really Fat Level Control) = 176 mGy/min (20 Roentgens per min)
• In “high level mode, ” you must have audible or visual alarms (in addition to the normal time
alarm used in normal fluoroscopy.)
Gamesmanship - Watch your units!!! It’s the oldest trick in the book to change up units and keep
the numbers the same. Don’t just memorize numbers - you gotta have those units on there.

Artifacts
Pincushion Distortion: With a large field of view you can sometimes get the appearance of bent
lines at the periphery (lines that should be straight). The inward bowing pattern is said to
resemble a pincushion.
S Distortion: This is a similar artifact
to the pincushion, also seen more in
larger fields of view. The etiology of
this artifact is an interference of the
earth’s magnetic field on the flow of
electrons heading towards the I.I.
Making it better? The addition of a
mythical material called “mu metal”
supposedly can deflect the magnetic
field (and protect against vampires).
Pincushion Distortion
- Due to Large FOV
S-Distortion
- Due to Earth’s
magnetic field
Flare or Glare Artifact: If you have a transition from
heavy attenuation to minimal attenuation - you can see
bright white “Glare” at the periphery near the decreased
attenuation. This is from an overproduction of x-rays
in this thin area, to compensate for the nearby thick
area.
Another artifact (saturation) which is described on the
following page can look similar with a gradient
appearance - see discussion on the next page.
Flare / Glare Artifact
- Image becomes brighter with
transition to less attenuation
Lag Artifact: You move the I.I. and the ghosted image remains superimposed from the prior
field.
133

Vignetting Artifact: Because distances
from the focusing point to the outer
phosphor tend to vary, with the closest
path in the center and the farthest path at
the edge, you can end up with a dark
periphery and a light center.
Vignetting Artifact
- Edges are darker than center
Saturation Artifact: If the dose is cranked
up to try and penetrate a very dense object
(classically metal), you can end up with
regions around the metal appearing very
bright.
Saturation Artifact
The I.I. System has many limitations - one of which is dynamic range. The way to think about this
is that the receptor requires a goldilocks amount of radiation - too much and it saturates (turns
totally white) too little and it under penetrates (too dark).
You will see this saturation - which they can also call "flare" or "veiling" - in two scenarios
1- the incident X-ray beam passes through low density anatomy (like the lung), resulting in a
higher flux than can be tolerated — makes it all white, which limits evaluation
2- the incident X-ray encounter metal — they all get blocked. The automatic brightness kicks in and
turns up the juice. The metal still block the X-rays, but the soft tissue around it gets extra radiation
- saturating the system - and making the white band / flare.

Vocab:
“Pitch ” There is a thing called pitch. Essentially it is the linear dimension of a detector element.
This is different than the “pitch” you think about with CT (more on that later).
“Fill Factor” There is a thing called fill factor. The system isn’t 100% efficient, only a portion
is actually sensitive to light. The ratio of the sensitive area over the total area is the fill factor.
As the detector element gets smaller, you get better spatial resolution, but the “fill factor”
decreases. In other words, a smaller detector element will have superior resolution but will
require more radiation.
“M atrix” - The matrix is the number of detector elements (or pixels) on the surface of the FPD -
in each dimension (horizontal and vertical).
1 Detector Element = 1 Pixel
Math problem: If the matrix size is 1100 x 1100, and the FOV is 25cm, what is the pixel size (or
detector element)?
the formula is: Pixel = FOV / Matrix.
But, I was trying to get sneaky with the units (a very common trick): 25cm = 250mm.
250/ 1100 = 0.23mm
136

Binning: There is a thing called binning. Taking several detector elements (DELs) and
making a large DEL. The idea is the you reduce the amount of data (and reduce Quantum
Mottle - less variation in x-ray photons from pixel to pixel).

Less Mottle means you can reduce radiation and keep the same noise.
Larger DEL does reduce the spatial resolution
When is “Binning” especially useful? -W ith large FOV (when there are too many pixels in the
image)
If there is no “binning, ” the spatial resolution changes in what way for different fields o f view (in
a FPD system)? No change. If you have binning, large FOVs will have less spatial resolution
(but can use less radiation to maintain the noise level). Remember “binning” is a FPD thing, not
an I.I. thing
What usually happens (without employing binning) with smaller FOVs? Dose is increased to
reduce quantum mottle. If you employ binning, dose can be reduced. For example, if you
combine 4 DEL you can reduce dose by 50%
“Frame Averaging (Recursive Filtration) ” - This is an image process feature that adds several
images together with different weight factors.
Pro: It’s done to reduce quantum mottle and increase the signal. SNR improves.
Cons: It increases the susceptibility to motion artifact and ghosting.
*Critical Point = Binning Improves SNR

A rtifacts (FPD):
Are artifacts seen with I.I.s the same as FPDs? Nope. FPDs do NOT have pincushion,
S distortions, vignette, glare, or saturation artifacts
Bad Pixel - appear as either white or black spots. One method to correct for this (often built
into the system) is to interpolate in order to fill the data.
Lag Artifact (Ghosting) can also occur with FPD. This tends to occur if the exposure uses
very high radiation.
S patial Resolution:
What limits spatial resolution o f an I.I. fluoro system? -The television display system. The
resolution of a TV depends on the raster (scan) lines, the bandwidth, and the FOV.
Does the display limit the spatial resolution o f a FPD system? Nope, FPDs usually have
displays with the same matrix as the image receptor.
Vertical Resolution is limited by? The number of raster lines that the display monitor uses.
You have to alternate white and black to reproduce a line pair image on the I.I. or FPD, so the
max number of line pairs in the vertical direction is half the number of actual lines used.
Because the bars in the line pair pattern may not totally line up with the raster lines, it’s less
than that so you need to correct with the Kell Factor (Kell Factor is some derived factor: 0.7).

Now here is where the testable trivia, and potential trickery comes in. If you keep the rate at 30
pulses/second your dose is UNCHANGED. What? I thought pulse fluoro reduced dose? It
totally can, but doesn’t have to. Here is what is happening:
Regular Fluoro: Continuous low mA with higher “S” = mAs
Pulse Fluoro: Pulsed high mA with lower “S” = mAs
Regular Fluoro mAs = Pulsed Fluoro mAs.
“S” = Time
i.e. mAs
So when does Pulse Fluoro reduce dose? If you drop the rate below 30 pulse/second. People
usually use 15 pulse/second as an example - and I’ll show you why in a second. But first I want
you to look at this diagram and understand why the dose starts to drop below 30ish.

Testable Trivia:
• Pulse Fluoro is good for moving patients (Wiggling Babies) - Gives you sharper images with
less motion blur
• Pulsed Fluoro can reduce dose - when the frame rate is below 30 frame /second
• People always use a drop of 30 to 15 frames per second as an example because that equals a
dose reduction of 30%. Math to get there isn’t important, just understand it’s not a direct 1:1
thing. 50% reduction in pulse rate = 30 % reduction in dose.
• Be careful how the question is worded - because a lower frame rate will have more mA per
individual pulse - but the overall mAs will be decreased relative to regular fluoro below 30
frames per second.
