Harmonics
-A Promethean Dialogue-
A lot of discussion in ultrasound physics centers around how the sound changes as it goes
through the body, and the various artifacts and such that result (so on and so forth, etc..ect..)
- and there will be some of that in this dialogue, but first we need to have another look at the
machine. Specifically, we should to focus on how the sonographic system is going to take
received signal and make a diagnostic image. A basic way to look at it is like this:
^
Beam
L Signal
j L
Image
Former
♦
—
As sound waves return to the probe the “Beam Former” will amplify and combine the
contributions from the various of individual elements. The next step is the “Signal
Processor. ” The “Signal Processor” will do several things - but the main thing relevant for
this discussion is the task of filtering out undesirable noise. The way this works is the probe
knows what frequency it sent out, so it has a general idea of what frequency is coming back.
There will typically be some slight changes in frequency as the sound wave travels through
the body and returns - and to deal with that a certain range of frequencies are deemed
acceptable. But - anything totally out of whack is gonna be considered irrelevant dog shit and
eliminated from the final processed signal. Something like this:
Raw Signal
This is the basic idea of how the signal processor can filter out unwanted frequencies to
reduce noise. Harmonics can be viewed as a similar process - but with a slightly different
goal. This time instead of filtering out the frequencies that are out of the expected range of the
primary harmonic (the transmitted frequency) - the system does the opposite, deliberately
filtering out the primary signal and instead preserving the signal from the second harmonic
echo.
209

Before we discuss why you might want to
do this - let us discuss how harmonic
frequencies are generated in tissues (who
does #2 work for ?) You can think about
sound waves as area of compression (high
density) and rarefaction (low density)
occurring in repeating cycles.
The areas of high density (compression) correspond with the positive direction of the wave
or “high pressure” and the areas of low density correspond with the negative direction of the
wave or “low pressure.” The reason I bring this up, is that as the wave travels through a
medium (i.e. the cirrhotic liver of an IV drug user who is salivating at the chance to sue
you), it doesn’t do so uniformly. The high pressure portion will travel faster than the low
pressure portion. As this happens the shape of the wave changes, eventually forming even
and odd “harmonics” from the original (fundamental) wave.
1st Harm onic
2nd Harmonic
It’s important to realize that you need a significant
difference between the high pressure and low
pressure portions of the wave to actually form a
harmonic under physiologic conditions. This is why
harmonics are only formed from the highest
intensity portions of the beam.
Raw Signal
Filter
Also - for this change to happen you need some time for the wave to morph. This is why
harmonic generation at the skin is basically zero. It’s a deep phenomenon.
OK... so why filter out that primary harmonic and accept a “lesser harmonic’
210

There are 3 primary advantages to doing this:
(1) Improved Lateral Resolution.
The reason for the improvement in lateral resolution is that the harmonic beam more narrow.
Fundamental Beam with 2nd Harmonic
Just the 2nd Harmonic (notice it is skinner)
Remember that Lateral Resolution is limited by the WIDTH of the pulse. People will often write
that as “beam width” rather than pulse width - but we are talking about the same thing since
“beam” is just a series of identical pulses given off in rapid succession.
Small beam width- reflectors
close together are still
resolved.
Machine correctly identifies
two objects
Large beam width- reflectors
close together are NOT
resolved.
Machine thinks it is just one
reflector
NARROWER BEAM WIDTH = BETTER LATERAL RESOLUTION
211

W hat about Axial Resolution ?
Remember that axial resolution is limited by the length of the ultrasound pulse - the “Spatial
Pulse Length” or “SPL,” ? Axial Resolution = Spatial Pulse Length / 2.
When the bandwidth (Bw) is narrowed (via filtering out the
fundamental frequency) you are going to lengthen the pulse
length.
Pulse Length = 1/ Bw
Shorter SPL: PL = 1/Bw
1 st Harmonic
Summary: Harmonics have a shorter bandwidth. This results in a longer spatial pulse length.
Longer spatial pulse length = reduced axial resolution.
Trivia: The solution to this problem is a technique called “pulse inversion.” The nuts and bolts of
how “pulse inversion” works is probably beyond the scope of the exam. Probably...
Before you spaz out and start googling it consider this - if the difference between you passing
and failing this test comes down to knowing the inner mechanics of obscure ultrasound
algorithms, as Kurt Osiander would say “you fucked up a long time ago.”
212

OK... so I said there were 3 Advantages. The first was IMPROVED LATERAL
RESOLUTION. Then we had a brief tangent, so I could point out that harmonics can actually
reduce axial resolution.
Now - to finish up with these last two potential advantages.
(2) Grating Lobe A rtifacts are Reduced / Elim inated.
I guess before I get into why they are eliminated I should answer the burning question.
“What the fuck are grating lobe artifacts ” ?
The simplest explanation is that they represent off-axis sound. Normally the beam shoots
straight down. However, sound energy can be emitted far off-axis (not on purpose - it just
happens sometimes) - and these things are called “side lobes” or “grating lobes” - and the
difference between those is two words is meaningless — seriously, don’t waste time going
there.
Main Beam
Usually the presence of a grating lobe doesn’t mean shit because they aren’t strong enough to
produce an echo. Having said that, if the grating lobes is directed at a strong reflector (gas,
bone, etc...), their echoes have a chance of being received and recorded - creating an artifact
(computer thinks the reflector originated from the main central beam - and will position it as
such).
So why are they eliminated ?
Remember when I said you need a sufficiently strong beam to have a large enough difference
between the high pressure and low pressure portions of the wave to actually form a harmonic
under physiologic conditions ? Grating lobes are usually lower energy - so this difference isn’t
present. In other words, they aren’t strong enough to produce a 2nd harmonic - and since that
is the data we are using to make the pictures, they get filtered out with the primary harmonic.
Grating / Side Lobe
Grating / Side Lobe
213

(3) Superficial Reverberation is Reduced / Elim inated.
There are several reasons why this occurs. The first one is depth. Remember Harmonics are
generated deep in the tissue (it takes time from the high pressure - low pressure differences to
morph the wave). As a result artifacts that are generated in the superficial tissues — for
example reverberation between ribs will be removed.
~ RIB
Reverberations - from
strong reflector (ribs)
Fundamental Beam with 2nd Harmonic
NO Reverberations
RIB
Just the 2nd Harmonic - notice it is skinner
Another reason is the energy of the reflected sound.
As a quick refresher, reverberations occur when the
sound beam is repeatedly reflected between two
interfaces with a high acoustic impedance mismatch
(difference). So you get a strong bounce back to the
transducer face. At that point the computer who lives
inside the probe recognizes that first echo and
localizes it accurately. But... because it was such a
strong echo some of it actually bounces off the
transducer face and returns to that reflecting
interface (as if it was a second transmitted pulse -
although now at a lower energy). When that second
pulse returns it’s location is falsely placed deeper in
the tissue (since it took longer to make it back). This
can happen multiple times - making a bunch of
horizontal lines.
The points that matter are that this strong reflection is almost always within the primary
harmonic (which is filtered out), and the subsequent reflected pulses are not high enough energy
to make a second harmonic. Therefore - harmonics can reduce or eliminate reverberation.
Some people will refer to a “speckle” artifact - from wave interaction - that is also decreased
with harmonics.
214

Harm onics Rapid R eview & Sum m ary
As the ultrasound beam travels into the patient’s tissues the transmitted
pulse is progressively distorted. However this occurs primarily in the
central zone of the main beam where the intensity is high. A distorted
pulse will give rise to distorted echoes and these have significant energy
at harmonic frequencies. Tissue harmonics are made using the second
harmonic component of the echo signal, with the fundamental frequency
excluded. Undistorted echoes coming from lower intensity areas (fringes
of the beam, side lobes, superficial tissues) are not seen.
The beam
changes as it
passes
through
tissues
The receiver can
be set to accept
this altered
frequency
What you need to know:
• Harmonics involves transmitting at one frequency and receiving at
another - “the second harmonic” they call it.
• Possible because body tissues cause some distortion of the wave.
• The wave has to travel some distance to become distorted enough to
generate harmonics - as such harmonics are NOT produced in the
near field (they haven’t traveled far enough)
• This process IMPROVES lateral resolution (and can worsen axial
resolution)
• Reduction in artifacts - specifically reverberation & side lobe / grating
but also the legendary “speckle” artifact - which I only mentioned once
in passing because it’s probably low yield....probably.
• Because you are dealing with a higher frequency, you do lose some
depth penetration (remember higher frequency attenuates - is
attenuated - faster).

Safety
First let us discuss some vocabulary related to
Amplitude: This is the difference between the
average value of the waveform.
This is not constant throughout the beam. If you cut the beam in cross
section you’d see that it’s more intense in the center, and less on the
edges. I bring this up because it illustrates the need to discuss “intensity.”
Intensity = power / area.
• If the beam power increases the intensity increases.
• If the beam area decreases (which happens during focusing) the intensity increases.
• If you square the amplitude you quadruple the intensity.
Some other important vocabulary words:
• Spatial Peak Intensity = The greatest
intensity found in the beam
• Temporal Average Intensity = The
average intensity over pulse times and
listening times.

218

Relative Intensity of Modes
Remember the smaller the beam area the greater the intensity. Intensity = Power / Area.
This is the easiest way to keep these straight - from a conceptual point of view.
“B-Mode ” - Also known as “brightness mode” or “2D mode” - This is the standard grey
scale mode that you use all the time. The field of view is fairly large, i.e. a big square.
“M-Mode ” - This is more of a cardiac ECHO thing (although you can also use it for fetal
HR). This mode focuses on the ability to see “motion” - for example a heart valve. It uses
rapidly emitted pulses over a narrow beam to determine the velocity of that specific structure
in question. Think about it as zeroing in on a very small structure to see if it is moving.
Smaller beam = more intensity.
“Doppler” - Think about the Doppler sample as occurring over a tiny little line - which is
necessary to see those little shifts. Smallest beam = most intensity.
This is a general way to think about these numerically:
B-Mode is 10
M-Mode is 4 0 ---------------------------Doppler is 500
Within the doppler family spectral pulsed doppler tends to have the highest temporal average
intensity. The reason is that the beam is held in a fixed position - not “spread.” Color flow
mapping and power doppler mapping tend to have some beam scanning - they tend to fall
between M mode and spectral pulse doppler.
• Biggest Bad = Spectral Pulsed Doppler Mode
• Made Worse With = High pulse repetition frequency (high limit on the frequency scale),
and a shallow range gate.
219

Two acoustic output parameters are used as indicators of the potential for biologic effect.
•Thermal Index (TI) - this is the maximum
temperature rise in tissue secondary to
energy absorption. This is based on a
homogeneous tissue model with certain
instrument parameters.
•Mechanical Index (MI) -this is how likely
it is that cavitation will occur considering
peak rarefaction pressure and frequency.
This index is the indicator of mechanical
bioeffects (streaming and cavitation). This
matters the most with contrast enhanced
US.
Cavitation - Sonically-generated activity in compressible bodies composed of gas and/or
vapor. This can be sub-classified as either stable or transient.
•Stable Cavitation - Micro bubbles are already present in the media. They expand and
contract as the waves cycle (responding to pressure). This occurs at low and intermediate
ultrasound intensities (as used clinically). The MI is an estimate for producing cavitation.
•Transient Cavitation - “The violent one” - Bubble oscillations become so large that the
bubbles collapse. This collapse results in shock waves rippling through tissue planes
causing tissue damage.
Testable Trivia: The rate of energy absorption increases with frequency. Eventually the rise
in temperature slows secondary to conduction and perfusion with an eventual steady state.
Testable Trivia: Thermal-induced damage is a threshold phenomenon (you get no tissue
damage until a certain temperature is reached).
Testable Trivia: Cavitation is most likely to occur with low frequency and high pressure.
Testable Trivia: Spectral Doppler deposits more heat compared to gray-scale ultrasound.
Testable Trivia: Per the NCRP - a risk-benefit decision when the TI exceeds a value of 1.0
and the MI exceeds a value of 0.5.
Thermal Index for
Soft Tissue (TIS)
Used for soft tissues
- i.e. OB scanning up
to 10 weeks after
LMP.
Thernal Index for
Bone (TIB)
Used when the
ultrasound beam
impacts on bone at
the focal region - i.e.
OB scanning 10
weeks after LMP.
Terminal Index for
Cranial Bone (TIC)
Used when the
ultrasound beam
impacts the skull -
i.e. Trans-cranial
scanning.

Obstetrics:
The bottom line is ultrasound has been around for fucking ever, and there has never ever ever
been a single case of a human fetus injured by it. Having said that, Radiologists are massive
cowards so caution is still advised.
Since temperature rises are most likely at bone surfaces and adjacent soft tissues, a baby with
increasing mineralization (2nd and 3rd trimester) has the theoretical risk of the possibility of
heating sensitive tissues such as brain and spinal cord.
Sensitive tissues
• Embryo less than eight weeks after conception
• Head, brain or spine of any fetus or neonate
• Eyeball (in a subject of any age).
Testable Trivia: There are actually different “thermal indexes” for soft tissue, bone, and
cranium. “Soft tissue Index” should be used in early gestation, “Bone index” should be used
after 10 weeks (2nd and 3rd Trimester), when bone ossification is evident.
General Recommendations (not at all evidence based) for 1st Trimester:
- Pulsed doppler (spectral, power, and color) should NOT be routinely used
- M-mode US is recommended instead of spectral Doppler US to document heart rate
- Keep the TI under 1.0 (some sources say 0.7).
- Scanning maternal uterine arteries with doppler is probably ok (as long as the fetus is
outside the beam)
Thermal Index:
-Below 0.7 - for OB imaging
-Between 1.0 - 1.5 - US should NOT exceed 30 mins
-Between 2.5 - 3.0 - US should NOT exceed 1 min
-Greater than 3.0 - US should NOT be used.

Nuclear medicine is unique in that instead of passing a beam of energy through the patient,
you are injecting a tracer into the patient and observing a normal or abnormal distribution of
that tracer.
Stability / Decay: Everyone wants to be happy. I want to be happy, you want to be happy,
and even isotopes want to be happy. We seek it in different ways. Some of us incorrectly
thought that going to medical school would make us happy. Isotopes seek happiness through
stability.
There is this thing called the “band o f stability, ” that demonstrates the happy place atoms go
to when they have a certain balance of protons and neutrons in the nucleus. It’s not exactly
1:1, but it’s pretty close.

Situation 1: “Lots of N eutrons, Not Enough Protons”
Beta Minus Decay: The solution to the
problem is to take one of your neutrons
(you’ve got extra) and turn it into a
proton.
This change from a neutron to a proton
results in the emission of a “beta
particle” from the nucleus.
WTF is a “Beta Particle ” ? - It’s
basically an electron. Why not just call it
an electron? Because “Real Electrons”
don’t come from the nucleus. This pansy
ass electron is a “beta male” electron - being
Why is an electron (beta particle) being ejected? There are two reasons. (1) You need to change
the charge of a neutron from neutral to positive. It’s best to think about neutrons as being 1/2
negative and 1/2 positive. If you eject the negative part, then you become all positive. The
second reason (2) is to balance out the energy loss - going from a neutron to proton. In this task
the proton doesn’t always get it done, so you have to emit a massless piece of shit called a “anti­
neutrino” just to balance out the energy.
What kind o f a process is this (what fancy general chemistry vocabulary word) ? This process is
an ISOBARIC transition. WTF does that mean? It means that the MASS DID NOT CHANGE.
Yes, the atomic number changed (it increased - since you gained a proton), but because the mass
of a neutron and proton are about the same (and the mass of an electron is pretty much nothing)
the mass number remained constant.
Is Beta Minus Good For Imaging? N o... it’s shit. But it can cause some damage.
I f you had a Beta Emitter in a syringe would you want a lead shield or a plastic shield to block
those beta particles (electrons) ? You want plastic - because plastic has a low Z. Lead’s high Z
will actually result in Bremmstahlung x-rays (remember that’s one of the reasons why Tungsten
is used as a target - high Z).

Situation 2a: “Lots of Protons, Not Enough Neutrons”
There are two situations that can occur when you have too many protons and not enough
neutrons, and just like in real life your choices depend on how much money you have.
“The Rich Guy” - Beta Positive Decay
It’s no big deal for the Rich Guy to buy a Ferrari or take an expensive vacation. It’s also no big
deal for him to undergo beta positive decay. Why? Because he has 1.02 MeV and has no
problem blowing it. Why does he have no problem blowing it? Because he didn’t work for it -
his Daddy gave it to him.
So here is how it goes down: A positively
charged proton is converted into a neutral
neutron. How does it do that? By giving up
it’s positive charge in the form of a
“positron” Just like in beta minus decay an
addition POS neutrino is also emitted just to
keep the energy balanced.
What is this “Positron ” ? It’s basically an “anti-electron” or the bizarro superman (his exact
opposite). It makes sense if you think about the proton needing to strip it’s positive charge away
to become neutral.
Is that the end of the story? Nope
The Positron travels a
Very Short Distance -
Then it meets it’s arch
nemesis, a real electron
They fight and all our cold war
fears are realized as they
mutually annihilate each other.
The result is the product of
TWO 511 keV photons
emitted exactly 180
degrees apart from each
Where does this “511 ” number come from? Remember the 1.02 MeV needed to be considered a
“rich guy” ? Well 511 keV + 511 keV = 1022 (or 1.02 MeV). Basically, they divided the money
up and each photon got half. Positron emission is ALWAYS 511 keV and that’s why.
226

Situation 2b: “Lots of Protons, Not Enough Neutrons”
“The Poor Guy” - Electron Capture
If you weren’t bom with a silver spoon in your mouth it may be necessary to steal (capture) to be
happy.
Remember you are trying to change a
positively charged proton into a neutral
neutron. The rich guy had enough money
(1.02 MeV) to bribe the positively charged
part of the proton to leave. The poor guy
doesn’t have that option. So instead of
subtracting a positive from a positive to make
a neutral - the strategy here is to ADD a
negative to a positive to make a neutral.
How do you pull this off? An electron is lured
into the nucleus from the K shell. I imagine
the Nucleus says something like “do you like
candy?... I have some in the nucleus.”
What kind o f a process is this (what fancy general chemistry vocabulary word) ? This process is
an ISOBAR1C transition. Remember, just like Beta Minus, in Beta Plus the MASS DOES NOT
CHANGE. Yes, the atomic number changed (it decreased - since you lost a proton), but the mass
number remained constant.
Is electron capture good for imaging? Yes - it’s excellent because it’s often coupled to a process
referred to as “isomeric transition,” which results in the emission of characteristic gamma
photons that can be imaged under a gamma camera.
227

T e ll M e M o re A b o u t th is “Is o m e ric T ra n s itio n ”
After undergoing an isobaric transition (beta emission, positron emission, or electron capture),
there is often left-over energy. This energy needs to be emitted prior to achieving final stability.
One process for achieving this final stability is isomeric transition - i.e. emitting energy and
dropping to the ground state.
Gamma Emission: In most isomeric transitions, a nucleus will emit its excess energy in the
form of a gamma photon. The energy of these photons is variable and depends on the energy
differences between the intermediate and final states of the nucleus undergoing isomeric
transition. Although the TOTAL energy emission for a given nuclei of a specific nuclide is the
same, many times you have more than one intermediate state - with multiple different energy
gamma photons.. This is why you see multiple peaks for different tracers (Gallium has 4, Indium
has 2, etc...).

Long vs Short - “Metastable” - In most cases, the time spent in the intermediate state is very
short. The transition from isobaric to isomeric transitions is like “bam-bam.” However, there is
a situation where the intermediate state is prolonged - a so called “metastable state.” Ever notice
that Technetium is called “99m” ? Well that “m” stands for metastable. The main value of this
is the ability to separate out the electron radiation (bad) and the photon radiation (good).
Therefore by using a nuclide that has already undergone an isobaric (election emitting)
transition, you can expose the body to the useful gamma emissions from the isomeric transition.

Tell Me More About this “Isomeric Transition” - Continued
Nemesis - Internal Conversion
Gamma emission has an arch nemesis called “Internal Conversion”. The details of this
probably aren’t super important so I won’t go into excruciating detail: just know that under
certain conditions, the energy you would normally get rid of via gamma emission can be
transferred to an electron within the atom. This transfer of energy is more than the binding
energy and will expel the electron. The vacancy will lead to a downward cascade from a
higher level - and result in either the emission of a characteristic x-ray or an Auger electron.

For Completeness - Alpha Decay = This tends to occur in heavier unstable atoms. Alpha
particles are basically Helium nuclei (2 protons, 2 neutrons). Alpha particles are slow and
fat. They can’t even penetrate a piece of paper, and are worthless for imaging. They can
however fuck stuff up that is within close proximity - and therefore can be used in treatment
situations (i.e. bone pain cancer mets via Radium 223).

Production
There are two main ways you can create tracers, both of which alter the Neutron/Proton ratio
to make them unstable:
(1) Bombardment (in a nuclear reactor or cyclotron)
(2) Fission
Bombardment: This is basically striking target elements with either neutrons (in a nuclear
reactor), or with charged particles (alpha particles, protons, or deuterons) in a cyclotron. The
downside to the nuclear reactor method is that you have to clean up the leftover parent
element. This is referred to as not being carrier free. The cyclotron has the advantage of
producing elements via transmutation, therefore you don’t have any parents to clean up -aka
“Carrier Free.”
-----------------------------—

.........
......
“Neutrons"
I
Nudeai Reacloi
Example: 9842Mo + Neutron
^ " 42M 0 + Gamma
This usually results in transmutation,
so you don’t have to clean up the
parent.
-CARRIER FREE
Fission: Neutrons are fired into large atoms (like Uranium and Plutonium) and split them into
pieces. This basically just results in a bunch of random crap being made; Iodine-131,
Xenon-133, Strontium-90m, Molybdenum-99, Cesium-137. Obviously the desired isotope
also has a bunch of fission products (contaminants) which have to be separated out - can be
done with chemistry. These guys demonstrate a bunch of different decay methods.
Neutron Activation: Target atoms eat up neutrons to form a new isotope. These neutrons don’t
really even need to be accelerated. Because the products are isotopes of the target atoms,
they cannot easily be separated from each other. As a result these are NOT carrier free. These
neutron rich products tend to decay via beta emission.
231

Radioactive Decay
“Half Life” - Which usually means the physical half-life. The amount of time necessary for a
radionuclide to be reduced to half of its existing activity. In addition to physical half life, you
have biologic half life. The biologic half life is basically how long it takes to shit or piss half
the tracer out. The “effective half life” takes both of these things into consideration.
Let’s do a couple o f practice questions:
1) The nukes department has a supply o f 10 Ci o f 99mTc at 8:00 AM. The patient is showing up
the next day at 8:00 AM the following morning — how much activity will be left?
To answer this question you need to know the half life of the agent. They are NOT going to
tell you that (see the list at the end of this chapter & book). The second thing you’ll need to do
is figure out how many half lives are going down in the time period in question.
The half life of Tc is 6 hours. 8am to 8am is 24 hours.

Ci. Now lastly I want
you to watch those
units! It’s the oldest
trick in the book to
change them up.
So it could be 0.6 Ci,
or 600 mCi
2) 1-131 has a biologic half life o f 24 days. What is the effective half life?
To answer this question you again need to know the physical half life of the agent. You have to
memorize those. The biologic half life is something no one knows - if they want you to solve
for effective half life they will give that one too you. The second thing you need to know is the
formula of effective half life.
The half life of 1-131 is 8 days. The formula is 1/Effective = 1/Physical + 1/Biologic
1/Te = 1/8 + 1/24 = 1/6 , or 1/Te = 3/24 + 1/24 = 4/24, reduced is 1/6 — so 6 days
Gamesmanship: If you have a large mismatch with biological and physical half life, effective
half life basically becomes the short one. In other words, if you breath the Xe out in 15
seconds it doesn’t matter what the physical half-life is. On the other hand, if your biological
half-life is 10,000 years then you are 100% counting on physical half-life to clear it.
How long do you have to keep radioactive material? The general rule is 10 half lives.
232

“Activity” is defined by the amount of disintegrations per second. Historically this was
measured in Curie (Ci) which is 3.7 xlO10disintegrations per second. The new SI unit is the
Becquerel (Bq), which is one disintegration per second.
“Specific Activity” is defined as the activity per unit of mass (Bq/g). The longer the half-life,
the lower its specific activity.
How the Gamma Camera Works:
A gamma camera takes photons emitted from the radionuclide, turns it into a light pulse, and
then takes that light pulse and makes some voltage. The voltage makes a picture.

Collim ator:
There are a lot of unwanted photons (background, off-axis, Compton scatter, etc...). The purpose
of the collimator is to reduce scatter and allow for correct localization of radionuclide events. It
works by discriminating based on direction of travel. It can NOT tell the difference in photon
energy (that’s done by the photon height analyzer).
Parallel Hole Collimator: “The Work Horse” This can be modified based on the energy of the
tracer being used.
Low Energy (l-200keV)- Thinner Plates: 99mTc, 1231, I33Xe, 201 TI
Medium Energy (200-400keV):
67Ga, 111 In
High Energy (> 400keV) - Thicker Plates: 1311 (technically most energy peaks are medium)
Sensitivity uv Resolution: These two guys have an inverse relationship. As one goes up the
other goes down. A high sensitivity collimator will allow twice as many counts to be imaged but
will degrade the spacial resolution. High sensitivity collimators are important with dynamic
imaging (like the flow phase of a bone scan).
Effects o f Distance on Sensitivity and Resolution: Distance has NO effect on Sensitivity
(increased distance reduce counts by inverse square, but the increased distance allows for a
greater field of view. NO change occurs in the net counts). Distance DOES affect Resolution
(septa are no longer able to eliminate photons from oblique angles as distance increases).
Septal Length (collimator depth): Short septa give a crappy spatial resolution, but better
sensitivity. Long septa give excellent spatial resolution, but crappy sensitivity (noisier image).
Hole Diameter: Wide holes = Highly sensitive, low resolution.
Narrow holes = low sensitivity, high resolution.

Resolution Pearls:
Pinhole Collimator: Magnifies and inverts image. Used for
thyroids and other small parts. It’s usually cone shaped.
Magnification occurs at a ratio of: pinhole to detector
“F” / pinhole to patient “B”
If F = B there is no magnification
If F > B = there is magnification
If F < B = object gets smaller.
An increase in object-to-
collimator distance degrades
the system spatial resolution.
The practical application is
patient positing.
The closer the detector is to
the patient, the better the
resolution of the images.
In other words, if you move the pinhole far enough away from the patient it will make the image
smaller. Magnification at the front (anterior) is greater than the back (objects are 3D), this is why
large objects get distorted and pinholes work best on small things. Sensitivity for pinhole cameras is
garbage.
Converging Hole Collimator (Cone Beam): Holes are close together on the object side and far
apart on the crystal/camera side. Magnifies WITHOUT inverting the image.
Diverging Collimator: The opposite of converging. Holes are far apart on the object side, and close
together on crystal/camera side. This takes a large object and minimizes it. The result is that you
can image a large part of the body on a small crystal. So increased area of imaged object, decreased
sensitivity and resolution.
Collimator Type
Parallel Hole
“The work horse.” You want the collimator and detector as
close as possible to the patient for the best spatial resolution
(this is affected by distance). Sensitivity is NOT affected by
distance.

Magnifies and inverts image - used for thyroids and other
small parts. Large objects get distorted (front is magnified
more than back). Sensitivity is garbage.

Scintillation Crystal- Once the photon emerges from the collimator, it impacts on the crystal.
The crystal is made of sodium iodine doped with thallium. The crystal has the property that
when struck with a photon it produces a pulse of light.
The thicker the crystal the less photons will be wasted (just pass through). On this same
principal, thin crystals will let more photons through (without generating light). Energy of the
photons themselves also plays a similar role. Higher energy photons will pass right through the
crystal (lower ones won’t). A thicker crystal is obviously better for high energy photons. So
why not just make all the crystals super thick? Apparently you sacrifice spatial resolution with
a thick crystal, because with a thinner crystal the photomultiplier tubes can sit closer to the
event and more accurately localize it.
*
Thick crystal = Better Sensitivity, But Worse Spatial Resolution
*
Thin crystal = Better Spatial Resolution, but Worse Sensitivity
Photomultiplier Tubes (PMT) - The PMTs detect the light and convert it into an electric
signal or measurable magnitude. The more PMTs you have, the more light you pick up and the
greater the resolution. The PMTs record two things: (a) location - on x and y axis, and (b)
signal intensity “Z.” The X and Y coordinates go straight to the computer. The Signal
Intensity (Z) goes to this gadget called a pulse height analyzer (discussed next).
Pulse Height Analyzer - The function of this thing is to discard background crap, and only
look at the photons from the tracer you are looking for. The background crap can come from
multiple sources (compton scatter, iodine escape from the crystal itself, backscatter).
The texts make a point in distinguishing the difference of background from a point source
versus background from a person. Basically you should know that the Compton scatter in a
person is a lot closer to the energy you want to image, but really degrades the images.

You can set a window that will exclude all that crap higher or lower to the desired peak. Some
cameras allow you to record multiple peaks (great for things like 67Ga and 11'In which have
multiple peaks or dual tracer studies).

Key Concept: “Dow nscatter”-
There is a thing called downscatter, which is super super high yield. Essentially high energy
photons can spill into the window of a low energy emitter, mainly resulting from Compton
scatter effects.
Example 1: You are doing a V/Q scan using 133Xe and 99mTc. Xe has an energy of 8IkeV,
whereas Tc has an energy of 140keV. The Tc Compton scatter is gonna range down from about
135 to 90 (as in the above diagram “from a
person”). So if you inject the Tc first and
image that will turn out just fine. But when
you give the patient the Xe they will still
have the Tc on board so you will be getting
Xe peaks at 81, with a bunch of Tc
Compton scatter all over it. So you totally
hosed yourself, because the Pulse Height
Analyzer can’t tell what is Compton scatter
from the Tc and what is signal from the Xe.
The solution is to image with the Xe first,
because the 81 keV and downstream
Compton effects from the Xe would not
affect the peak at Tc (140) at all. Hopefully
that makes sense. Otherwise just
remember, LOWER PHOTON ENERGY
TRACERS MUST BE USED FIRST.
Example 2: If you are using 99mTc and 67Ga for a bone scan of the spine looking for
osteomyelitis. Gallium has a bunch of photopeaks (93, 184, 300, 393) and an effective half life
of 50 hours. You have to give the Tc first and image, otherwise you’d end up waiting 50 hours.

SPECT and Matrix:
Gamma camera imaging can be “static” or “dynamic.” When improved spatial resolution is
needed “SPECT” can be performed by rotating a camera 180 or 360 degrees around the
patient. The longer you image on SPECT the better it looks, but patients just can’t hold still
forever - and movement degrades the images. Another thing to consider is matrix size.
Matrix size: Size matters in most things in life, matrix being no different. A matrix size of
128x128 has superior resolution to 64x64. However, a larger matrix size means longer
acquisition time (which patients hate), and reduced count density per pixel (which impacts
image contrast)
S tar A rtifact:
If you have very focal intense energy you can sometimes see a star artifact, caused by septal
penetration of the hexagonal collimator holes. This is typically seen in the thyroid bed after a
high therapeutic dose (using a medium energy collimator, instead of high).
/
I
MJ
Star Artifact - From Septal Penetration
*Star Pattern is seen if collimator holes are arranged in a hexagonal pattern

Quality Control for Gamma Cameras
Possibly the most dreaded portion of radiology multiple choice exams (right up there with safety,
and mammo MQSA). Daily, weekly, and quarterly tests are done on the camera (BY A TECH!
NOT A RADIOLOGIST!!!) to make sure it’s functioning properly.
As if this wasn’t bad enough, pinning down “official” recommendations is difficult as the NRC
essentially says hire a medical physicist and do whatever the fuck they say. Honestly, if I was
running the NRC I’d give similar advice. The problem is choosing consensus information for the
purpose of multiple choice. The following is my best attempt at guessing “the right answer” for
trivia that could be asked.
Four parameters are usually tested for: field uniformity, window setting, image linearity and
spatial resolution, and Center of Rotation (COR).
Field Uniformity: So the photo multiplier tubes can have subtle variability in what voltage they
assign to a given photon of light. Additionally, the crystal isn’t totally uniform, and has subtle
variations in thickness. For exam purposes, you want to try and keep these two things as uniform
as possible. A 2%-5% non-uniformity is allowed (1 % if SPECT).
The test is known as a “Flood”, and is used to see if the camera can produce a uniform
image along the entire crystal surface. It’s done two ways:
(1) Extrinsically (with a collimator), and
(2) Intrinsically (with NO collimator). You can use either a Na99mTc04 source or a Co57 source
to perform the flood. The recommended counts for both extrinsic and intrinsic range
somewhere between 5-10 Million (Siemens recommends 10 Million).
•
Extrinsic (WITH Collimator) - is done daily. This tests the collimator and crystals.
•
Intrinsic (WITHOUT Collimator) - is done weekly.
A computer will check to make
sure the field is uniform.
You can also visually inspect
the test images, to see if there is
an obvious problem like a
bunch of bullseyes, or a
segmental region of failed
PMTs.
Norm al
Bull’s Eye
-“Flood” - For
Appearance of Tubes

239

Energy Window: The correct window needs to be used prior to each study, therefore this test
should be performed daily. The most common approach to testing this is to use a symmetric
window centered at the peak energy used in the imaging test. The source can be a syringe, a
vial, or if absolutely necessary the patient. For Tc you would use a 20% window centered at
140keV.
Image Linearity and Spatial Resolution: Lead
bar phantoms with parallel lines, placed
between the collimator and a 57Co sheet, are
used to test image resolution and linearity. This
only needs to be done weekly. Resolution is
defined by the ability to differentiate between
two distinct points (can you tell the bars are
separate). Linearity is tested by looking to see if
all the bars are straight (some distortion at the
edges is ok). The bars should NOT look wavy.
Quality Control for Gamma Cameras Continued
Center of Rotation: Gamma cameras that are used for SPECT have to be routinely monitored
for alignment offset at the COR (Center of Rotation). The test is done with 5 small 99mTc
point sources along the axis of rotation. The axis should be straight, with minimal
deviation. Most sources will say this is performed monthly.
Bar Phantom
— Lines should be straight (NOT WAVY)

Safety
This is also an excruciating topic for Radiologists to learn about. As previously stated nukes
is full of random trivia, and let’s be honest academic nuclear medicine tends to attract
eccentric personalities... and they are writing the questions.
Lead Aprons / Film Badges / Ring Badges
Why don’t nuclear medicine techs wear lead?
Two reasons: (1) Thin lead doesn’t stop gamma rays. To stop them it would have to be so
heavy you couldn’t move. (2) When high energy gamma rays collide with a dense material
that is not thick enough to stop them (lead apron) they rapidly slow down and lose energy
which turns them into deeply penetrating Bremsstrahlung x-rays - which actually makes the
dose worse.
Correct Positioning on the Film Badge and Ring Badge
*
Film Badge: Should be worn on the collar at the chest / neck level
•
Ring Badge: Worn on dominant hand, index finger, label in towards source
(usually this means towards the palm), under a glove (to avoid contamination)
Instruments:
• Sodium Iodine Well Counter - This is basically a small gamma camera, with just one
PMT. There is a hole in the block of Nal crystal into which the sample is placed (so it’s
surrounding the sample). So this gives you great efficiency in detection, but has a problem
with being overwhelmed. If the sample exceeds 5000 counts per second (a lot less than a
micro curie), it will be under reported. It’s good for in-vitro blood or urine samples. It’s
good for "wipe test ” samples.
• Thyroid Probe - This is a modified version of the Nal well
counter. Its primary use is to calculate thyroid uptake values. The
device has shielding, with a small opening that is pointed at the
patient, at a precise distance. Dose is compared to a calibrated
capsule of the same radionuclide.
Well Counter
241

• Geiger -Muller Counter - this is a device used to detect small amounts of radioactive
contamination. The device has a gas-filled chamber, which when it comes in contact with
radiation becomes ionized and creates some voltage between a cathode and an anode - lots of
beeping and clicking occurs. There are two ways that questions about this device are classically
asked; (1) by just showing you a picture of one and saying “what is this”, (2) by testing the
concept of “dead time.”
What is this “dead time” ? Although the device is very sensitive, it is also vulnerable to
being overloaded by a large dose of radiation. Then you have “dead time” as the
ionization must dissipate before it can respond again. The maximum dose it can handle is
about lOOmR/h. So if the device clicks once and stops it might be because of “dead
time” (it might also be “dead time” for you).
Trivia - The GM Counter can NOT provide info on radiation type
• Ion Chamber - This is what is used when higher doses are expected. You don’t have the
dead time problem, for reasons that don’t matter. Ion chambers can be used to detect exposure
rates from 0.1 to lOOR/h (note the unit change). The dose calibrator used in most nuclear
medicine departments is actually an ionizing chamber.

gamma)
For measuring dose rate.
Used with higher rates.

for accurate estimates (or exposure).

Readout of activity is made in mCi. The range of most devices is 30 microCi to 2 Ci. Dose
should be within 5% of computed activity, and this should be checked daily.
*
Consistency - should be within 5% of computed activity. Checked with reference
sources (checked Daily)
* Linearity - accurate readout for activities over the whole range of potentially
encountered activities - checked with a large activity of Tc (around 200mCi) and
decaying it down to less than the smallest activity you would measure for use. Or -
the easy way - use a Calicheck© or Lineator© kit (which contains sheets of varied
thickness of lead, simulating decay over time), (checked quarterly).
* Accuracy - Standard measurements of radiotracers measured and compared to what
the activity should be (performed at installation of the device and annually).
* Geometry - Correction for different positioning and size (different volumes of liquids)
of the sample (performed at installation and any time you move the device)

Regulations:
No bureaucracy is complete without lots of rules and regulations (and of course agencies to
police such regulations). The Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) is where you will want to
look for all your rules and regulations. Specifically part 19 (inspections), part 20 (radiation
protection), and part 35 (human use of radioisotopes) . The “NRC” is the governing body that
has been charged with the task of enforcing all these various directives. It is possible for
individual states to reach an agreement with the Federal Government to enforce these rules on
their own. These are called “Agreement States”, and the main thing to know is that they can
be more strict, but not less strict than the national agency.
NRC Trivia:
- 10 CFR part 19: Notices, instructions, and reports to workers.
- 10 CFR part 20: Standards for protection against radiation.
- 10 CFR part 35: Medical use of by-product material.
Radiation Safety / Contamination
Accidents happen. Rules to know for answering questions about
spills: First Things First - is it a MAJOR spill or a MINOR spill ?
MAJOR Spills
*
Activity level greater than 100 mCi of Tc-99m is considered a major spill.
*
Activity level greater than 100 mCi of TI-201 is considered a major spill.
*
Activity level greater than 10 mCi of In-111, is considered to represent a major spill.
*
Activity level greater than 10 mCi of 1-123, is considered to represent a major spill.
*
Activity level greater than 10 mCi of Ga-67, is considered to represent a major spill.
*
Activity level greater than 1 mCi of 1-131 is considered to constitute a major spill.
Major Spill - what do I do ???
1. Clear area.
2. Cover spill with absorbent paper. Do NOT clean it up.
3. Clearly indicate boundaries of spill area. Limit movement of
contaminated persons
4. Shield source if possible
5. Notify the Radiation Safety Officer immediately
6. Decontaminate persons
Radiation Safety
Officer needs to be
notified
immediately when
a major spill
occurs, to direct the
decontamination
process.
Minor Spill = You
Clean It Up
Major Spill =
Don’t Clean it Up,
Call the Radiation
245

MINOR Spills
(1) Protect the Patient: If a spill occurs while a patient is in distress, address the patient
first. Once he/she is stable then address the spill.
(2) Confine the Spill / Limit the Spread: Secure the area and make sure people aren’t
tracking it all over the place.
(3) Clean Up the Spill: Use gloves (wear shoes), and all other personal protective
equipment including a radiation badge. If possible tongs or forceps are even better for
grabbing stuff. Use a damp absorbent material to clean the spill (working from the
outside to the center).
(4) Survey Cleanup Items: Anything used in the clean up needs to be surveyed or
presumed contaminated (held until they decay to safe levels - rule is 10 half lives).
(5) Survey Cleanup People: People also need to be surveyed by the radiation safety officer
(in a different area - to avoid count interference).
What if
?

contaminated. Clothes will be held by the RSO until
activity has decayed to safe levels.. ..at which point
they will be worn to the club.
• What if it gets on my skin ? Wash with soap and water
(don’t scrub so hard you break your skin .... you

• What if there is a Xenon leak?
• Without alarming the patient (i.e. don’t yell “the
radioactive gas is leaking!!” - even though that
would technically be true), instruct all individuals to
leave room as quickly as possible. Close the door.
• Testable Trivia = The wipe test does NOT work on
Xenon contamination.

Regulations Affecting the General Public
Regulations demand the following:
• Annual Dose limit of 100 mrem to the public
• Not greater than 2 mrem per hour - in an “unrestricted area”
• “Restricted Area” = Any place that receives a dose greater than
2 mrem/h

• Very High Radiation Area: Any place you could get 500 rads

Receiving, Storing, and Disposing of R adioactive M aterial
Within 3 hours of receipt (3 working hours) you (the tech) have to survey packages when they
arrive. This process involves a GM counter test at the surface and 1 meter from the package, as
well as wipes of all surfaces of the package (>6600 dpm/300 cm2 is not allowed). Keep the
package in a controlled area, like the hot lab (just in case A1 Qaeda invades your hospital).
Contact both the shipper and the NRC if beyond allowable limits.
Package labels and allowable limits
White 1: No special handling, surface dose rate < 0.5 mRem/hr, 1 meter ~ 0 mRem/hr
Yellow 2: Special handling required, surface dose rate <50 mRem/hr, 1 meter < 1 mRem/hr
Yellow 3: Special handling required, surface dose rate < 200 mRem/hr, 1 meter < 10 mRem/hr
*Seriously, these assholes couldn’t pick another color? - instead they choose “yellow 3 ”
“Transport Index” - “T.I.” - This is the
measured max dose at 1 meter. This is an
actual dose rate measured at 1 meter (not an
allowable dose rate), at the time of shipping
•
Radioactive Label 1: White 1
- There is no T.I. because the rate at 1
meter will be so low.
• Radioactive Label 2: Yellow 2
- The T.I. is < 1.0 mRem per hour.
• Radioactive Label 3: Yellow 3
- The T.I. is > 1.0 mRem per hour.
“Common Carriers” - A truck that carries regular packages and radioactive material. The T.I.
should not exceed 10 mRem / per hour. The surface rate should not be more than 200mRem.
“Multiple Packages ” - Those shipped together; the sum should NOT exceed 50 mRem.

I now want to transition into discussing Tc-99. It’s the workhorse of nuclear medicine, and its
production / purity is likely high yield for multiple choice.
First let’s talk (read) about how Tc is made.
Vacuum
s '
Step 2 : Radionuclide Purity Test
You want to make sure you just have Tc, and you didn’t wash any Mo off as well. Mo that is in
the sample is called “break through. ” The basis for the test is to look for the different photo
peaks of the radionuclides. The sample is placed behind a lead shield. Mo is assayed for first.
The high energy photons of Mo (like 740 keV) will NOT be attenuated by the shield, but the 140
keV Tc photons will be.
• NRC allows no more than 0.15 micro Ci of Mo per 1 milli Ci of Tc, at the time of
administration. Few pitfalls here:
• (I) note the unit change from micro to milli,
• (2) note the “time o f administration ”
* If ratio is less than 0.038 at the time of elution, the material will be suitable for injection for at
least 12 hours.
Step 1 "M o / 99mjc Generation
Because Molybdenum’s half life is longer than Tc
(Mo = 67 hours, Tc = 6 hours), it can be made and
shipped in a Tc generator. Mo adheres to the
aluminum column tightly. As it decays, it does not
stick as tightly and can be washed off with saline
across the column. When it comes out, the Tc is
stuck to Na (Na99mTc04). The piece of trivia to
know is that it’s in a +7 valence state, and must
be reduced to be used. This is accomplished
with stannous ions.

Step 3: Chemical Purity Test
Remember that the column is made of Aluminum Oxide, which
can wash off, clump up with the Tc, and show up as liver activity,
or cause sulfur colloid aggregation and show up in the lungs on
liver spleen scan.
The test for purity is performed with pH paper. The allowed
amount is < 10 microgram A1 per 1 ml.
Step 4: Radiochemical Purity Test
As mentioned above, Tc comes out of the generator as Na99mTc04. So, to use it for anything
useful it needs to be reduced (accomplished by adding it to SnCh). Thin layer chromatography
is used to assess for purity.
Limits for Free Tc
*
95% Na99mTc04
*
92% for 99mTc sulfur colloid (MAA)
*
91% for all other Tc radiopharmaceuticals
Buckle Up Bitches: Questions can be sneaky.... My thoughts on some potential pitfalls:
• When you check the Radiochemical Purity of 99mTc-MAA, you are checking for free
pertechnetate (TcO 4)
• Testing for Chemical Purity is NOT mandatory in NRC States
. The 99Mo and 99mTc ratio must be known at the time of ADMINISTRATION, not elution

Mo FIRST, before the Tc (to prevent issues with residual charge).
• Technically 99mTc made in a generator is not considered carrier free because of the presence of
99Tc which is technically a radionuclide (but essentially stable since it has a half life of
200,000 years).

SPECT
What is this SPECT? - The simple way to think about it is like this. CT is to plain film, as
SPECT is the planar. Basically you take a gamma camera(s) (pretty damn similar to the one
you use for planar) and rotate them around the patient. What this does is give you a 3D look
— instead of a 2D look (like you get with planar). SPECT is a single photon system - unlike
PET which uses two photons (from a positron annihilation event).
Just like a regular planar scan, SPECT is not fast. Each projection takes like 30 seconds and
the total scan time is around 15 mins. The matrix size is usually around 128 x 128 (for things
that are not cardiac). The scan uses iterative reconstruction to make a picture. Because the
data is isotropic volume, reconstruction in 3 planes is possible. Sensitivity is depth dependent,
with radiation from different tissue origins attenuated to different degrees. Special
collimators with longer holes are used to help improve the collection of photons.
Testable Point: The big advantage SPECT has over planar is improved contrast from
overlapping structures.
Testable Point: SPECT is depth-dependent (PET is not * more on that later).
Tuning Fork Artifact: Cardiac SPECT uses a 180 degree orbit. When a point source is
imaged, it should look like a point source. If there is an error with the center of rotation
(misregistration error) then it will look like a tuning fork (two lines in one direction and one
line in the other). The same appearance can be seen with motion.
254

PET
An Overview:
Remember, there are two situations that can occur when you
have too many protons and not enough neutrons. The “Rich
Guy” situation - where you have 1.02 MeV to blow - results in
the emission of a positron during the conversion of a proton to
a neutron.
This positron is going to travel a very short distance (less than
2mm) before it meets its arch nemesis the electron. The two
fight to a draw, mutually annihilating each other. The
annihilation event splits that 1.02 MeV in half, creating two
511 keV photons which are emitted exactly 180 degrees from each
other.
PET scanners work by detecting these 511 keV photon pairs -
doing lots of math to determine their location of origin.
SPECT v.v PET - There are a few differences.
(1) SPECT is a few cameras rotating around the patient. PET is a set of complete rings/
detectors surrounding the patient. The PET detectors are much more sensitive.
(2) SPECT is set up to detect medium-ish energy photons. PET is set up to detect a very
high energy photon (511 keV). Remember that Iodine 131 - which is considered “high
energy” in the planar world - has an energy of around 365 keV. So 511 is HOT. The
main difference here is the scintillation crystal used. Just like in a regular planar camera
SPECT used Nal systems - whereas PET tends to use a thicker more robust crystal
(Bismuth Germinate (BGO), Lutetium Oxyorthosilicate (LSO), or Lutetium Ytrium
Oxyorthosilicate (LYSO)
(3) PET counts are way way way more (like lOx more). This results in an image with much
less mottle.
Those are the big ones... I’ll mention a few more as we proceed with the discussion.
Let us begin first by discussing “localization.”
255

Localization / Coincidence Detection
As mentioned before, PET has a complete ring(s) around the patient.
As the 511 pairs fire off the photons are presumably going to land on
opposite sides of the ring - 180 degrees apart.
A true understanding of how this decay process results in a picture is complex and well beyond
the scope of the exam (trust me, no one writing the questions actually understands it). It will be
the same deal when we discuss MRI in the next chapter.
In the simplest terms, the computer assumes that these
two 511 photons travel in a straight line ( “straight line
o f coincidence ”) reality is that the line is never exactly
180 degrees straight. The degree to which the line of
response is not linear is referred to as “Non-
Collinearity. ”
The 511 keV photons will strike the detector elements, which are usually made of some type of
scintillation material (related trivia on these later). Each photon is evaluated separately by the
computer for 3 things: (a) spatial information , (b) energy, and (c) arrival time.
The individual photons will be considered a “pair” if they:
(1) Stay within a present energy window:
example ~ 25-35% Full Width Half Maximum
or "FWHM” - centered at the 511 photo-peak.
(2) Stay within a certain timing window
(example — 5-10 nanoseconds) - meaning they
struck opposite sides of the detector at nearly
the same time.
If two photons follow those criteria they will
be consider a pair, and the “event” which
created them will be calculated along the
“Line O f Response” or "LOR. ”
Most (> 90%) of the generated photons are
rejected in this process.
Rejecting Scatter:
Rejecting photons that don’t meet the criteria
based on time of incidence and photon peak
are mechanisms for rejecting scatter.
In a perfect world, both photons should
measure 511. If a photon undergoes scatter it
will lose energy, so you can exclude scatter
by saying you will only keep a photon at 510
keV or above.
However, if you exclude photons that are just
barely off of the 511 mark you will exclude a
ton of true events as well. So, it turns into a
balance of sensitivity and specificity. A
tighter window gives you a more specific
exam, but you are going to lose a lot of
normal counts (and decrease your sensitivity).
Various Degrees of “Non-Collinearity.”
256

The previous page described the scenario where a pair of photons would be considered to
represent a “true coincidence.” This is actually only 1 of 3 possible scenarios where the
computer will record an event. There are two additional types of “false coincidences” - which
are basically tricks / fuck ups - Scatter and Random.
True Coincidence - Two 511 photons resulting from
an annihilation reaction are detected in the same
coincident window.
Scatter Coincidence - One of the photons has a
Compton interaction and is deflected, but still hits
the detector within the coincident time window (just
not in the calculated location).
Trivia: The Compton event lowers the photon
energy. Therefore you can reduce this by using a
more narrow photo-peak window.
Random Coincidence: Two photons, from different
annihilation reactions just so happen to land within
the same coincident window - creating the false
calculation that they occurred from the same event.
Trivia: The number of random events increases with
the dose of administered activity.
Trivia: The number of random events is decreased
with lower dose / counts and a narrower time
window setting.
“Noise Equivalent Counts” or “NEC” is a metric used with PET imaging to describe the signal
to noise ratio. It is basically a ratio of true coincidences over total coincidences (true + false)
with some random “k” factor to describe stuff you don’t need to know about. There is a very low
probability of using the equation for any math. I’d just understand that if a question writer says
“NEC” they mean SNR (signal noise ratio) in the context of PET.
Trivia: If you went to the PET Scanner store and you were trying to compare models, the one
with the higher NEC (assuming injected dose is the same) will be one that can achieve superior
contrast-to-noise and signal-to-noise ratios.

Coincidence

257

Continuing with this discussion.... If the photons meet the
selected criteria and a Line Of Response has been generated, that
data will be compiled along with all the other LORs to generate
a sinogram (buncha wavy lines). That sinogram is then
converted into a picture that makes sense to a human via the
magic of complex math referred to as “iterative reconstruction
algorithms. ”
Nuts and Bolts on the Detectors:
PET uses Scintillators. Remember that Scintillators are materials that produce light when
struck with ionizing radiation (in this case 511 keV photons). The light emitted by the
scintillator is then “read” by a photomultiplier. There are several different options for crystal
materials and there pros / cons are amendable to multiple choice trivia.
THIS vs THAT - SPECT vs PET Crystals:
• SPECT: Remember that SPECT uses Nal (activated with a trace of thallium) scintillator crystals.
These crystals have an effective atomic number of 50.8, and do great with low-medium energy
photons. For example, they absorb around 90% of the Tc99m photons. However, they are
progressively less effective at higher energy levels (they only absorb around 30% of 1-131 photons).
• PET: The crystals used for PET have to be able to handle those massive 511 photons. That means
they need to be thick, have high density, and high atomic numbers.
Intrinsic Resolution is bound to the crystal thickness. The thinner the crystal the better the resolution.
The problem is a thin crystal has reduced sensitivity, compared to a thicker crystal of the same material.
Density of the material plays a key role in improving resolution by allowing for a thinner crystal to be
produced.
• Higher Density Material = Potential for reduced material size = Improved resolution.
• Reduced thickness also has the secondary benefit of producing less Compton scatter.

Inherent Limitations
There are multiple inherent limitation to PET:
• Crystal Thickness (number of detectors) - As we discussed on the prior page, the thick
crystals you need to use to contain those super high energy 511 photons are going to result
in decreased spatial resolution. This is actually the primary limiting factor that determines
the spatial resolution of PET. The smaller the crystal used - the more detectors you can
have, and the more detectors you have, the better your spatial resolution is.
• Positron Range - The positron travels a little bit prior to the annihilation event. The
detectors are tracking the position of the event - not the original location of emission. So
you are always going to be slightly off. How off you are depends on the energy of the
photon. F-l 8 (used for traditional FDG PET) has a maximum range of around 1mm in soft
tissue. Rb-82 - used in cardiac PET tends to have a little more energy and can travel slightly
longer (still only a few millimeters).
■ This idea of positron range is sometimes referred to as “the ultimate limit o f spatial
resolution ’’for PET. I guess because no matter how awesome you design the camera, this
part of the process cannot be made zero.
• Angulation / “Non-Collinearity” - Remember when I said the two 511 keV photons are
180 degrees apart ? This is only sorta true. Because there is a little tiny explosion when the
positron and electron collide sometimes there is some extra kinetic energy left over which
wobbles the takeoff. Usually these things go around 1-3 +/- degrees from 180. So 180 is a
generalization. But if you are setting the detectors up to think 180 , and it was actually
180.5 then you will be off a little bit in your localization.
• Scatter - As previously discussed, the detectors try really hard to not get fooled - by having
coincidence detection - but sometimes even the best of us make a mistake (or occasional
typo).
259

2D vs 3D
2D Systems - These employ lead (or tungsten) septa to block unwanted scatter radiation. The
downside to this is that you are also decreasing your sensitivity for un-scattered photons (you
excluded the “good guys” too).
3D Systems - These systems do NOT use septa. This results in a large increase in sensitivity,
and allows you to decrease the amount of tracer you are giving. Why can you decrease the
dose of tracer? Because 2D systems are blocking a ton of activity with those septa (both good
and bad). To pull off the 3D imaging you need faster coincidence detector. The classic spots
you see 3D done is with CNS imaging (small object - lots of activity) and PEDs (also small
object).
Potential Disadvantages to 3D
• Dead Time - It is possible to overwhelm (and
temporarily paralyze) the detectors if you have counts
rates that are very high. Another reason a dose
decrease is sometimes a good idea.
• More Random Events - 1 mentioned before that
random events increase as your counts increase. The
increased sensitivity of this mode can lead to more of
these detections and a resulting low level background
noise.
• Scatter - Without those septa you are getting a lot of
scatter. So, your coincidence detection has to bring its
“A” game.
Tim e of Flight
Here is the idea, let us say you have a cancer in the anterior most part of your liver. The FDG
uptake and resulting positron emission / annihilation event is going to take place in a location
that is not in the center of the scanning bore. So one of the photons is going to reach the
detector earlier than the other (which will have to travel a farther distance). TOF uses very
quick detection which helps estimate the actual point of annihilation. The best situation to use
TOF is large objects with low contrast (less background noise - to confuse the math). TOF
results in improved spatial resolution and image contrast — especially in “heavier”
patients.
Trivia - SUV measurements with TOF images are higher than those on standard PET.
“PET Dead Time”
When a photon strikes a detector,
the scintillation crystal produces a
light flash which is collected by one
or more photomultiplier tubes. The
photomultiplier tubes determine the
spatial position and energy of the
event. After that, the coincidence
processing occurs. During the time
required to complete all of these
tasks - the system will not respond
to new events. This is “dead time.”
Reducing Dead Time: You can use
crystals with faster scintillation
times - (LSO) or you can add more
PMT’s.
260

Attenuation Correction
Let us begin our discussion on Attenuation Correction by refreshing memories. In the prior
pages, we were discussing the various methods the scanner uses to confirm a true event and
localize that event along a “line of response.” In that discussion we mentioned that it is
possible for scatter to trick the detector, producing an event that is assigned to the incorrect
LOR. The additional issue is that when you assign the event to the wrong LOR (wrong spot in
the body) you are also removing that event from the correct spot in the body.
This removal of events can occur from both Compton interactions and Photoelectric
interactions - and be defined with the vocabulary word “Attenuation.” *In PET it is primarily
Compton scatter.
Attenuation Correction works by assuming that 511 keV photons will be attenuated
exponentially by a linear attenuation coefficient (the more tissue it passes through the greater
the attenuation).
“PET is depth independent. ” You may hear people say “PET is depth independent. ” Which is
confusing since it’s intuitive that the more tissue you are passing through the more you are
getting attenuated - so shouldn’t the opposite should be true ? The reasoning behind this
statement is that both 511 keV photons (remember there are two created from a single event)
both have to travel through the tissues at 180 degrees without interaction to meet criteria for an
event.
Still don’t believe me ? Look at this:
This Arrow is “5”
This Arrow is “4”
This Arrow is “3”
Units Long
Units Long
Units Long
Notice that regardless of the depth, the sum of the distance traveled by both arrows is the same.
261

Now having said (written) that, this is really only true if the body is a homogenous attenuator
(which it’s not). We are made of different stuff - bones, blood, fat, in some cases silicone, or
even metal. In this way it is better to think of PET attenuation as being “Tissue Dependent. ”
Before we get into that - let us be clear. I’m not trying to convince you that attenuation does not
occur to a greater degree at increased depth - obviously it does. I’m trying to say that having two
photons coming off at 180 degrees creates a scenario where things get evened out. This is
different compared to the situation for SPECT where you have a single photon at one depth vs
another photon at a different depth. This is why PET has a superior capacity to correct for
attenuation relative to SPECT.
As previously discussed, photons originating from
deep structures will undergo greater attenuation
relative to more superficial. If no corrections were
performed the values measured from tumors deep in
the body would appear artificially low (and those
near the skin artificially hot). Attenuation correction
is based on the data collected from a transmission
scan utilizing a positron emitter 68Ge rod source, and
then compared to the results of a “blank scan.” The
“blank scan” is a QA procedure performed earlier in
the day (more on this later). The transmission scan
creates a very noisy “attenuation image” on which
mathematical corrections based on variable
attenuation along each LOR can be made. This
methodology is under the influence of the F-l 8 FDG
in the body.
If you are using combined PET/CT (which is almost always the case) you can use X-Rays as
your transmission source. Obviously, using x-ray via a conventional CT scan is going to produce
a more better image that has a lot less noise. You should think about the CT part of PET-CT
being performed for two main reasons: (1) so that you can see where things are and (2) for
attenuation correction. This methodology is NOT under the influence of the F-l 8 FDG in the
body.
THIS vs THAT:
PET vs SPECT-Attenuation Correction
With PET the correction for nonuniform attenuation is applied to the data prior to
reconstruction. This is considered an advantage o f PET over SPECT
With SPECT the correction fo r nonuniform attenuation is integrated into the reconstruction
process, increasing its complexity.
Point Source - “Transmission Scan ”
262

Gamesmanship:
Classic Trick 1: Metal Objects (classic = pacemaker) will generate overcorrected false increased
activity at the site. This happens because the density of the metal is outside the conventional
range that can be processed. You can see the same thing happen with other very dense objects
including: calcified lymph nodes and IV contrast bolus injections (increased activity in the
adjacent venous structures).
This is why you always double check the uncorrected data when reading a PET.
Classic Trick 2: A sneaky trick is to ask you to distinguish between corrected and uncorrected
PET. The secret is to look at (1) the skin - which will be hot on uncorrected, and (2) the lungs -
which will also be hot on uncorrected.
Uncorrected
Corrected
" Dark Skin
- Light Skin
■ Dark Lungs
“ Light Lungs
263

Calculation of Standard Uptake Value (SUV):
SUVs are not like HU (calibrated to water). There are multiple factors which go into the
calculation of SUVs, and essentially make it highly variable from institution-to-institution and
even patient-to-patient.
The calculation is this:
SUV =
Tissue Radioactivity Concentration at Time Point 1 x Patient Weight
Injected Dose Activity
Fat vs Skinny: Fat has a low FDG uptake. So, it’s actually more accurate to use the patients
lean body mass instead of weight (this is why the value is different from patient to patient).
SUVs in fat people are overestimated (there is more sugar around for the tumor) - this can be
corrected for by using lean body mass.
Timing: The longer you wait between FDG administration and imaging, the more FDG uptake
you get. At first the uptake of FDG in the tumor is rapid (rapid in first 2 hours), then it goes up
slower. In other words, if you did “delays” you would get higher FDG values. Another point
is that if scan (1) is done after an hour, and scan (2) is done after 2 hours, then scan 2 will
falsely appear to have increased SUV. Basically, you need to do the scan at the same time
interval after FDG administration if you want to compare apples to apples.
Glucose Levels: This is one of those competitive kinetics things. The more non-labeled
glucose floating around, the less FDG the tumor can drink. High glucose = Lower SUV.
Size Matters: There is a size threshold for PET (usually 1 cm). Anything smaller than 1 cm,
will be subjected to partial volume effects, and give a falsely low SUV.
Smaller than 1 cm = Lower SUV.
Dose Extravasation: FDG is given IV (most things are - except you breath Xenon and you eat
Iodine). If you put all your FDG in the soft tissues of the arm, you have less circulating and
get a lower SUV. Dose Extravasation = Lower SUV.
Reconstruction Type: Iterative reconstruction can mess with the SUVs. The more iterations
the higher the SUV.
Attenuation Correction: Attenuation correction with CT, also adjusts for SUVs (the positrons
have to go through denser stuff too). The method used for the computer to calculate
attenuation correction varies between institutions which can make comparing SUVs difficult.
264

II
■
This has to do with differences in the field of view (FOV) from PET and CT. The classic
example is a giant monster fat person who is so fat that they have several feet of blubber
outside the CT FOV (and therefore not providing data for attenuation correction). The PET
FOV is larger than the CT so it picks up the blubber but the eventual SUV calculation will
be fucked because of the mismatch between CT and PET.

Lesions that arc on the margin of the FOV in the CT scanner may appear hotter than they
really are from attenuation over-correction. Lesions that are outside of the CT FOV - but
are seen on the PET scanner may seem artificially cold because they received little or no
attenuation correction.
Bottom line = Giant Fat People can have artificially lower (or higher) SUV on peripheral
lesions - secondary to FOV differences between CT and PET.
265

Pre FDG PET prep:
Diet - Fasting for 4 hours prior to the test is the typical recommendation. If they
just ate, they will spike their insulin and drive all the FDG into their muscles.
Minimizing cardiac activity (you might want to do if you have a thoracic cancer)
can be done with a 12 hour fast, and a low carb/ high protein & fat diet for 24 hours.
Hydration - Oral hydration, and frequent voiding decreases the dose to the bladder
and improves urinary visualization.
M uscle Uptake - There are a couple of causes for diffuse muscle uptake; exercise
(most places discourage this for 24-48 hours prior to the exam), eating or insulin
use. More focal uptake (classic forearms from stress gripping) can also be seen.
Insulin - If you have to have insulin, long acting insulins are better to avoid diffuse
muscle uptake. Metformin is ok - it won’t mess with the muscles.
Brown Fat - Excessive brown fat uptake can be distracting. You can decrease it by
(1) making the room warm, (2) giving drugs - with the big two being propranolol
and diazepam (Valium).
266

PET QA
Just like there is QA on all the other pieces of equipment in nuclear medicine, PET also has a
whole bunch of QA. I’ve chosen two QA tests which I think are the most high yield. I believe
they are high yield because they are sorta similar things and that means they’d make good
distractors for each other on multiple choice.
Blank Scan - DAILY - This can be done two ways.
1.
By using a positron source (68Ge or l37Cs) in the scanner all by itself.
No phantom. No patient. Just a “blank scan,” irradiating the detector elements uniformly.
2.
By using a uniform source - most commonly referred to as a “cylinder.” This cylinder is a
20 cm diameter phantom filled with the 511 keV positron emitting 68Ge/Ga.
The “cylinder”
is placed at the center of the FOV - and should also irradiate the detector elements uniformly.
Don’t be tricked if the question writer refers to a scan that is performed with “nothing in the field
of view.” This is the classic description of a blank scan, and it is correct for the “1” way
described above. Obviously, for “2” you are placing something in the FOV (the “cylinder”) but
this method is still referred to as a blank scan. Summary - both methods are “blank scans.”
There are two reasons for performing a blank scan.
1. The uniform data allows you to have a “zero” set to help perform the attenuation correction
calculation — you may recall I mentioned this in passing on page 246. This is done daily.
2.
As the analog of the “daily flood” scan
used for planar gamma cameras to
evaluate the overall detector response.
Just the flood or uniformity test you
want to see an image that looks very
uniform. No crazy dark lines. If you
see a dark diagonal line or band that
means a crystal / detector is fucked.
You could also use a cylinder full of F-18, but because of the short half life (109 minutes)
compared with 68Ge (270 days) I assume refilling that fucking cylinder everyday gets to be a
significant pain in the ass.
This “uniformity” portion of the scan is also done daily (some sources say weekly).
267

PET QA Continued
Normalization Scan - MONTHLY (some sources say at installation, “after major service, when
the studies look shitty, or at least annually.”)
The scanner is made of a shit ton of detector elements (like 20,000). If you have even the
slightest variation is the performance properties (i.e. one crystal is cut slightly thicker than
another) you can get small variations in the line of response (LOR) counting. This nonuniform
variability can be “normalized” by performing a normalization scan.
This is performed similar to a blank scan - either by taking a point source (usually 68Ge) and
rotating it around the periphery of the field of view, or using a cylindric source. The idea is to
expose the detectors to a uniform flux of positrons.
Trivia / Clarification -
Regarding 68Ge / 68Ga
You will sometimes (often) see 68Ge described as a positron emitter. This is not totally accurate
and could be a source of trickery on a multiple choice exam — so allow me to clarify.
68Qe / 68Ga is a generator system - just like Tc and Moly.
Germanium 68 will decay into Gallium 68 (not to be confused with Gallium 67). Gallium 68 is
the 511 keV positron emitter. So why not just used Gallium 68 ? Same deal with Tc / Mo.
Germanium 68 has a half life of around 271 days. That makes it ideal for transportation and in
house production. Gallium 68 only has a half life of 68 minutes - which would be a massive pain
in the ass for daily QA stuff.
