Disclaim er - Strategy Overview:
MRI is a bottomless pit of trivia. It’s very easy to fall into the trap of “trying to know
everything” when studying this topic. That is potentially a colossal fuck-up. People literally do
PhD work on the stuff and still don’t totally understand it. It boggles the mind how smart the
people were who invented this stuff - as it’s very difficult even for “smart people” to get the basic
concepts. I could dive into the reasons why “smart” people struggle with learning the topic -
cliff notes is I don’t believe most doctors are “smart” - 1 just think they are good at memorizing
and repeating stuff. It’s ok - that skill set will work here too. That’s what I’m trying to convey.
You do NOT need to master the subject matter. The people writing the questions don’t even
understand it that well. You just need basic concepts and that is what I’m going to give you. If
you find yourself making a mean face and saying “he didn’t even mention -blank or blah!!!” -
that’s probably because I deemed that shit not important. Yes - it’s possible I could leave a few
things out, but I promise you that the knowledge contained in the next 50 pages or so is more
than enough to get 90+ % of the questions right, and it’s way better than hunting through
volumes of 1000 page physics texts trying to “understand” how all the intricacies work.
How does MRI work?
A certain percentage of nuclei in the body are considered “active.” WTF does “active” mean?
Basically it means that there is NOT an equal number of protons / neutrons. I think of these
atoms as being off balance. Off balance atoms will “spin” in the magnet and can be “knocked
off balance.” The classic example is Hydrogen 1 - of which there is a shit ton in your body (fat,
water, etc..), but you could in theory do MRI targeted at Carbon 13, Oxygen 17, Fluorine 19, and
so on. Having said that - from here on out we are going to be talking about Hydrogen 1 nuclei -
which is basically 1 proton. So when I say “the proton” - I’m talking about the solitary proton in
a hydrogen nucleus.
The MRI magnet is always “on” - constantly generating a magnet field. Prior to entering the
magnet, the “protons” are normally randomly aligned, about half up and half down. When you
stick someone in the magnet slightly more than half of them will align with the direction of the
magnetic field. You can then slap them down with an RF pulse, and then watch how fast they
bounce back up. The micro-environment that these protons live - be it water, fat, the liver etc...
will be slightly different and the complex interactions within these environments will cause the
protons to bounce back from the “slap down” at different speeds. The difference between how
fast different protons bounce up is the “contrast” used to tell things apart - and make a pretty
picture (after an incomprehensible amount of complex math).
Think about it like this - you have a fat guy and a skinny guy on a track. One way to tell them
apart is the time it takes them to run a lap. This is the general idea for MRI.
270

W hat is this “Coordinate System ” ?
MRI employs a “coordinate system” - to describe which
direction the proton(s) are pointing. A point worth
making is that when you see an arrow pointing up or
down or sideways - this does not represent a single
proton, but instead the addition of all the protons as a
“vector”. Protons aligned in the direction of the external
magnet field are called “longitudinal magnetization.”
This is the “default setting” when someone is in the
magnet. If they are aligned perpendicular to the Z axis,
then they are considered “transverse magnetization.”
W hat is this “RF Pulse” ?
In my overview I said that you could “slap down” the protons and then time how fast they
bounce back up or “recover.” (Using the word “recover” makes you sound smart). The
“slapping” is done by a RF pulse. The pulse is a radio wave.
What? A “Radio-wave” ? Yup — Remember this thing?

“Radio-waves” are just low energy particle waves - with much longer wavelengths (less
frequency) than an X-ray. An important (testable) point is that they are NOT Ionizing.
“Knocking the protons down, ” is done with a push. Pushing protons that are spinning in a
magnet is a lot like trying to push your kid on a swing. You have to wait until he’s swinging back
at you and you push him/her as he/she is moving forward. In other words, you have to time it
just right. Another way to think about this is if you wanted to hand someone something but they
were running. You would need to run at the same speed as them to be able to hand it to them.
It’s an energy transfer.

So how do you know how “fast to run” , or “w hen to push” ?
There is this thing called the Larmor Equation.

The Larmor equation describes the precession frequency of a nuclear magnetic moment and
resonant frequency of a nucleus, and relates these aspects to the magnetic field strength. It
basically says the precession frequency gets higher as the field strength increases. The kid
swings faster.

W hat exactly does the RF pulse do?
The RF pulse actually does two things: (1) it decreases the longitudinal magnetization, and (2) it
causes the protons to sync up and precess in-phase (which establishes a transverse
magnetization).

W hat do you m ean “sync up” ?
Before the Pulse
Let’s say we are look at this vector diagram from the air - maybe
looking down on it from Donald Trump’s Helicopter. What you are
seeing is the “transverse plane.”
The normal (not-synced) situation is going to be a random
rotating / spinning of the protons.
Immediately After Pulse
Immediately after the fury of the RF pulse - if we looked down on
this thing we would see the protons all lined up together. They
would be synced up or “in-phase. ”
When can you m easure signal?
Signal can only be measured when it is NOT in the longitudinal direction. If you are running
next to someone at the same speed, you can’t really measure how fast they are going (it’s like an
Einstein relativity thing). But, if they turn and run away from you, then you can measure that.
It’s sorta like that.
273

W hat is this “ T1 w ?
After you knock the protons down with an RF pulse, they will grow back up to normal size (their
spin magnitude will re-orient in the direction of Bo). The time it takes for this to happen is
different in different tissues. The process is referred to as “longitudinal relaxation”.
Some people call this “spin-lattice relaxation ” because energy from the RF pulse is handed over
to the surrounding lattice. The “1” of TI resembles a thermometer - which is useful in
remembering that TI relaxation involves the exchange of thermal energy.
Plotting the time vs longitudinal magnetization creates the TI curve.
T1 - Recovery of Longitudinal Magnetization
The return to longitudinal relaxation follows an exponential curve approaching 100% over time.
TI is defined as the time at which longitudinal magnetization is 63% of its final value. Each
tissue has a different TI and the greater the field strength the longer the TI (because net
magnetization is greater in a larger field).
Things with short TI signals are bright. Hence the phrase “intrinsic TI shortening” is something
that is bright on TI. Things that have long T ls are dark.
Is TI different in a stronger magnet? A stronger magnet makes TI longer. Protons in this
stronger magnetic field have more energy (they precess faster), and therefore it takes longer to
hand that over to the lattice.
274

W hat is this “ T 2 ” ?
Remember I said the RJF pulse caused the protons to synch up and precess in phase (which
establishes a transverse magnetization). As time progresses the protons will slowly fall out of
sync and start doing their own thing again. This is the T2 Transverse relaxation.
Time Elapses
Plotting the time vs transverse magnetization creates the T2 curve - which resembles the
downward portion of a ski slope. I like this analogy because it helps me remember that T2 is
shorter than T1 (takes less time to go down a hill than up it).
Transverse magnetization decay is also described by an exponential curve with T2 representing
the time at which the signal has decayed to 37% of its original value of transverse
magnetization (63% of it has decayed). Another term often used to describe T2 is “spin-spin
relaxation. ”
W hat causes protons to lose th eir transverse sync (T2 relaxation) ?
There are two main reasons (1) inhomogeneities in the external field, and (2) inhomogeneities
in the local magnetic filed - within the actual tissues & tissue spin interactions. Pure things
(water) take longer to decay their transverse magnetization, and are therefore bright (the
opposite is true of impure liquids).
275

T2 vs T2* ?
The signal of T2 decays faster than various PhDs would predict based on tissue spin interactions
alone. The reason for this is that math falsely assumes the main external field is absolutely
homogeneous — it is not. This heterogeneous field creates additional interactions which further
speed decay. It is because of this that T2* decay is ALWAYS faster than T2.
T2* = Tissue Spin Interaction + Field Inhomogeneity; “random + fixed causes ”
•
T2 = Tissue Spin Interaction ; “random causes only”

W hat is this “Proton Density” ?
So you have T1 and T2 weighted sequences, and then you have “Proton Density.” So what is
this “proton density.” The best way to think about it - that PD is what you have left when you
subtract the bias of T1 or T2 weighting. With all things equal the only thing you are measuring
is how many protons are present in one thing vs another thing.
So how is that accomplished? On the prior page, I showed you that a “short-short” - TR, and TE
will result in T1 weighting - by maximizing the longitudinal relaxation differences between
tissues and minimizing the transverse relaxation differences. I also showed you that a “long-
long” - TR, and TE will result in T2 weighting - by maximizing the transverse relaxation
differences between tissues and minimizing the longitudinal differences.
Q: So what if you choose a long TR - to minimize longitudinal difference, and chose a short TE
to minimize transverse differences ?
A: Scan with PD weighting.

First let me introduce this thing called a “Fourier Transform.” A fourier transform is a

Turning K-space into an image requires an inverse two dimensional Fourier Transform (lots of

S patial Encoding
- How Do You Localize the Signal From W ithin the Body ? -
Localization of signal requires three steps:
(1) Select the desired slice
(2) Encode spatial information along the rows
(3) Encode spatial information along the columns
The localizing gradients have identical properties they are
just applied at different times and in different directions.
Because you have three gradients in three planes, you can
localize anything in the body.
(1) Selecting the Desired Slice: The use of a slice selection gradient (SSG) is used
to select the area of interest. This gradient is placed perpendicular to the desired slice plane.
The slice selection gradient determines the view (axial, coronal, sagittal, even oblique).
This vs That
Localization Gradients can
be turned “on” or “off.”
Superconducting “main”
magnet is always on.
Selective Pulse: On top of this gradient,
an RF pulse is applied - at the same
frequency as the protons in the slice
plane you want to sample. That way
only the protons in this plane will be
affected. This pulse is called a selective
pulse.
A
RF Pulse at the
Desired Slice
“Selective Pulse’
Slice Selection
Gradient
Dealing with the 180 degree refocusing pulse: In a spin echo sequence you apply a 180
degree RF pulse after your 90 degree pulse. Apparently this jacks up your nice field
(especially along the edges). To deal with this, before and after the 180 degree RF pulse you
place two identical gradients (which cancel each other out) and correct for errors around the
edges.
282

(2) Encoding Spatial Inform ation
~ in Vertical Direction (Phase Encoding):
This is the second step in the process. This gradient is
applied causing protons in the same row perpendicular to
the gradient to have the same phase. All protons at this
point will have the same frequency.
Phase encoding is much longer than frequency
encoding - this is why it’s done on the thinner portion.
They don’t want to lose time including the tip of the nose
on phase encoding - this is why it’s side to side in the
head. The opposite is true in the abdomen, where it’s
front to back. Breast is an exception to this rule (I’ll
touch on that later).
Contribution to duration o f study: The number ofphase encoding steps contributes to the
duration o f a 2D imaging sequence.
Duration = TRx Npy x Nex
TR = Repetition Time
NPy = Number o f phase encoding steps
Nex = Number o f excitations
Phase Encoding
Gradient
(3) Encoding Spatial Inform ation
~ in the Horizontal Direction (Frequency Encoding):
This gradient is applied perpendicular to the phase-enc
direction, which results in the modification of Larmor
frequency over the duration of its application. The end
result is column of protons which have identical
frequencies.
Testable Trivia: This encoding gradient is applied at the
same time as the readout.
Frequency Encoding
Gradient

(1) Slice Selection: This SSG is applied at the same time as the 90 degree RF pulse (called a

This process is repeated for as many phase encoding steps you do.

M odifications
-VERY VERY VERY HIGH YIELD TOPIC-
This next topic is probably the single most high yield topic for the purpose of multiple choice
(artifacts is probably a close second). “Modifications'” - is going to be a discussion of the
choices you make and how those choices have consequences.
The focus will be on 3 main topics:
(1) Table Time
(2) Spatial Resolution
(3) Signal to Noise
1: Table T im e $$$$
Stunna' Island Money and the Power
There are a variety of things that can subtly mess with table time. However, for the purpose of
multiple choice - all things being equal, this is the formula I want you to use.
TIME = TR x Phase Matrix x NEX
• TR = TR is the time between each RF pulse (usually
measured in milliseconds)
• Phase M atrix = The is the data the system collects from
each phase encoding step. In other words, how many
phase encoding steps.
• NEX = The number of times each set of phase encoding
steps is repeated. Often referred to as “The Number of
Excitations.” Or perhaps more easily thought of as
“how may averages (excitations) per TR ?”
This is the only equation you need for both spin echo and gradient echo sequences.
Unless they tell you otherwise - assume this is what they want.
Please resist the urge to over complicate this.
Putting Laundry Away
TR = How long To fill each
drawer
Phase Matrix = How many
drawers in your dresser
NEX = How many times you
have to open the same drawer, to
pack in more socks.
*1 read a similar analogy in the
book “MR1 in Practice ”
286

What about slice thickness ? Thinner slices is more time right?
Ugh.... this is exactly the kind of thinking you need to avoid. Stop trying to understand it and just
memorize the fucking formula!
OK... Fine! It’s probably true that using a narrower RF pulse bandwidth will take longer to excite,
which may result in a slightly longer scan. BUT for the purpose of multiple choice (assuming it’s a
2D standard sequence) I only want you to consider the big 3; TR, Phase Matrix, and NEX.
But wouldn’t thinner slices mean more Phase Encoding Steps?
Don’t think about it like that. Think about it as
“phase matrix.” If you are going to eat the entire
block of cheese, making 30 thin slices vs 10 thick
slices doesn’t change the calories - it’s still the same
block of cheese.
Same Block of Cheese = Same Calories
Are there any situations when the “Big 3 ” Formula doesn't hold up? Yes (2).
The first one is Fast Spin Echo. For reasons that will be explained later , Fast Spine Echo
acquisition time is approximately proportional to 1/Echo Train Length
The second one is 3D Imaging. Prior to this everything I’ve been talking about has been 2D, and
unless they specifically tell you that it’s 3D you should assume that it’s 2D.
In 3D Volume imaging you are acquiring
information in blocks instead of slices. This has the
advantage of improving spatial resolution (you can
really shrink your slice thickness) plus there is no
slice gap. 3D imaging also offers improved SNR
plus the ability to manipulate the angle of obliquity
(great for rectal and cervix cancer staging).
So why not do 3D all the time? The scan times are long - which means money gets lost... which
means the hospital administrator (non-MD) can’t afford a second Rolls Royce. Because what’s
better than 1 Rolls? You guessed it... 2 Rolls.
Take Home Point:
2D: TIME =TR x Phase Matrix x NEX
3D: TIME = TR x Phase Matrix x NEX x # Slices

BUT Prometheus! What About the Magnet Strength!?!
When you think about magnet strength I want you to think mostly about Signal and Noise (not
scan time). Remember - super basic here. You put the patient in the scanner (magnet is always
on) and a certain number of nuclei align with the field. Not all of them. Some...
Lets do this...
You have a 0.5 T blur machine because technical fees buy Ferraris, and Ferraris are awesome.
At 0.5 T 51% of the nuclei are aligned with the field. So
49% are against it. So the Net is 1%. You got 1% to work
with here. More than enough to find that disc budge (and
obscure the pancreatic mass in all that blur ).
Your good friend at the Academic Center has a 3T (and drives a Honda Civic).
At 3 T 90% of the nuclei are aligned with the field. So 10% are against it. So the Net is 80%.
He has got 80% to work with. More than enough to find that disc bulge (and miss the pancreatic
mass because he only reads neuro, all his other skills have atrophied, doesn’t recognize the
finding and gets sued for 10 million dollars).
The point here is that if you have a stronger magnet you will have more available net
magnetization to pull from and the SNR goes UP. If you have a weaker magnet you have less
magnetization to pull from and SNR goes DOWN. To compensate for the terrible SNR you can
(probably should) do some things and those things will make the scan time longer.
What kinds o f things?
Increasing your TR.
Longer TR = Better SNR.
Shorter TR = Shitty SNR
*Other one is the other one*
Longer TE = Shitty SNR
Shorter TE = Better SNR
If you choose to lengthen the TR to improve SNR -
you will increase the table time. So in this way a
weaker magnet could “increase” table time.
BUT unless they tell you the TR has been changed
then don’t assume that it has. Keep it basic. Chances
are the person writing the question doesn’t actually
understand physics.
*These percentages are made
up — pulled directly from the
darkest deepest crack in my
ass. I ’m ju st trying to make a
point about Signal and Noise.
291

BUT Prometheus! 1 Read that Stronger Magnets Increase the TR!
That’s Opposite o f What You Just Said!?!
So... let us be clear about some things. There is NOT a knob on the scanner that turns down or
up the field strength. It is like ultrasound. If you want a different frequency you need to pick up
a different probe. You want 1.5 T instead of 3 T then you need put the patient on a different
scanner.
BUT you know what there is a knob for? Changing the fucking TE and TR. That’s how you
decide how heavily T1 or T2 weight something is or PD or whatever..
Remember the TR controls the amount of recover in the longitudinal direction that is allowed to
recover before the next pulse is applied.
Long TR: You are giving time for full recovery. Therefore, you are allowing more to be
available to be flipped on the next go around.
•
Short TR: You are NOT allowing for a full recovery. So when the next RF pulse comes
you have less available signal.
This is why a Long TR improves SNR, and a short TR makes it worse.
BUT.... What About the Magnet...?
T1 recover depends on the ability to hand energy back. Free water is terrible at this. So it has
long T1 (dark on Tl). If you have proteins in the water they slow down the movement (like
thickening the milk shake) and make the energy transfer more efficient (proteinacous cyst = Tl
bright). Tissues with fast Tl will have more signal recovered in a given TR relative to tissues
with a slow Tl.
With a larger magnet strength you will have more energy to give back, so it
takes longer to recover. In this way stronger magnets can be thought of as
decreasing the available signal available at a given TR. So to compensate
for this you could increase the TR. We are talking about making changes for
image contrast here (not SNR - like the prior page).
Bottom Line = Field Strength doesn’t do dick to TR. You set the TR. If you choose to increase
the TR to improve your Tl signal tissue contrast then you increased the TR. Don’t be blaming
shit on the magnet when you are the one that turned the TR knob up.
Should you assume that the test writer has inferred that stronger magnets always have increased
TR? I wouldn’t but if you want to be sure then simply read the mind of the person who wrote
the question to see if they had that bias.
Images
Contrast =
The degree
o f T l / T2
weighting.

Factors affecting spatial resolution: Spatial resolution is governed by the size of a voxel. The
voxel size is determined by the matrix, field of view, and slice thickness.
€ FOV Phase
Voxel = Slice Thickness x I
X

Field o f View: The smaller the field of view, the better the spatial resolution. So, why not make
the FOV super super small? Because you will get aliasing or wrap around artifacts from signal
outside the FOV.
FOV Read
Matrix Size Read
Matrix Size: The matrix size corresponds to the image width and
height (in pixels). The larger the matrix the smaller the pixels.
Pixel = FOV / M atrix
Unfortunately, no one
can be told what the
matrix is, you have to
see it for yourself.
Gradient: A gradient with higher amplitude (more intense) or one applied for a longer period of
time results in better spatial resolution. Makes sense since the point of a gradient is to localize
stulf.
Slice Thickness: For a thicker slice you can either increase the Transmit RF pulse bandwidth (the
slice selection pulse) or you can decrease the slice selection gradient. For a thinner slice you
would do the opposite. The thinner the slice, the better the spatial resolution.
Summary - Better Spatial Resolution If:
-Sm all Voxel
-Sm all Field of V iew
-Larger M atrix
-Thinner Slices
Steep (Large) Slice Selection Gradient, and Thin Transmit Bandwidth
293

3: Signal to Noise
Noise is random variation in the signal that leads to its degradation. In MRI the primary source
of this noise is the patient’s body. Overall the SNR depends on voxel size (bigger is better),
field strength, and some random sequence parameters.
Voxel Size: Anything that makes your voxel bigger improves your SNR (the opposite of spatial
resolution).
•
Thicker slices = Higher SNR
•
Increased Transmit RF Pulse Bandwidth
•
Decreased Slice Selection Gradient
•
Larger FOV = More SNR
•
Smaller Matrix = More SNR
Field Strength: A stronger field gets you more signal. Greater Field Strength = Greater SNR.
RF Coils: Smaller surface coils improve your signal (increased SNR) compared to a coil
within the scanner.
Number o f Excitations per Slice (number o f averages): The more excitations you perform the
more signal you get (increased SNR). The main trade off is a prolonged imaging time.
Receiver Bandwidth: A fat bandwidth gives you a rapid sampling of data, a narrow bandwidth
gives you slow sampling of data. Since noise is constant, the fatter band will pick up more
noise. So, fat bandwidth = decrease SNR, narrow bandwidth = increased SNR.
Maximizing TR (long) and Minimizing TE (short) - Peaks your signal.
Sum m ary - B etter SNR If:
Stronger M agnet
Long TR
Big FOV
Large Slices
Shallow (Small) Slice Selection Gradient, and Thick Transmit Bandwidth
More NEX
Short TE
Sm all M atrix
Sm all Receiver Bandwidth
Appropriate Coil Size

Potential Fuckery -
Receiver Bandwidth vs Transm it Bandwidth
Don’t confuse these terms - they sound similar and would make good distractors.
Fat Receiver Bandwidth = Decrease SNR,
Fat Transmit Bandwidth = Large Slice = Increased SNR
Narrow Receiver Bandwidth = Increased SNR.
Narrow Transmit Bandwidth = Thin Slice = Decreased SNR
Proton Density - Signal to Noise Specialist
If you ask a MSK guy the utility of Proton Density he/she may tell you “I like it for the
meniscus.” If you ask a Neuro guy the utility of Proton Density he/she may tell you “I like
it for MS Plaques in the Posterior Fossa.” If you ask a physicist the utility of Proton
Density he/she may tell you “it has a good signal to noise ratio.” All of them are correct -
for the same reason. Proton density has excellent signal to noise.
Why?
• Remember the ways you increase SNR ?
• Long TR, Big FOV, Large Slices, more NEX,
• Short TE and Small Matrix.r-.r-:
^
• Remember how you make a Proton Density? Long TR and Short TE.
See? - Proton Density is basically designed to have excellent SNR
T ra d e o ffs : T h in g s to T h in k A b ou t:
• Thicker slices will improve SNR
• Thinner Slices are needed for Spatial Resolution
• Thinner Slices are Noisier
• Signal Can directly be improved by increasing the NEX - but increasing NEX from two
to four, doubles the scan time, but increases the signal by only the square root of two.
• Shortening TE will improve your SNR - and it doesn’t mess with table time. BUT the
loss in T2 effect can wreck your image contrast. In general you should only drop the
TE to improve SNR with Tl sequences.
• Larger receiver bandwidths may decrease SNR but they also decreases mismatch
artifacts like chemical shift or magnetic susceptibility. Thinner receiver bandwidths may
increase SNR but they also increase mismatch artifacts like chemical shift or magnetic
susceptibility.

There are a bunch of sequences, but two general categories:
Spin Echo - which uses a 90 degree flip angle and a 180 degree refocusing RF pulse
Gradient Echo - which uses a LESS than 90 degree flip angle, *and NO 180 RF pulse

As discussed in the spatial encoding section, the slice selection gradient is applied with the 90

High Yield: Duration = TR x Phase Matrix x Nex
TR = Repetition Time
Phase Matrix = The is the data the system collects from each phase encoding step
Nex - Number o f excitations
Contrast: The contrast on spin echo sequences is determined by the TR (interval between 90
degree RFs), and the TE (interval between the 90 degree RF and the receipt of the echo). As
mentioned above, a short TE and TR gives you Tl weighting, long TE and TR gives you T2
weighting, and long TR & short TE produce a proton density sequence.
High Yield Fact: Because a line in k space is filled at each TR, the TR contributes to the
duration of the sequence. So, much so that long TR times are the reason a true spin echo is of
historic significance.

Fast Spin Echo
Fast Spin Echo
The idea behind the FSE sequence is to reduce the TR, which I already mentioned is a
major contribution to the duration of the study. This is done by applying multiple 180 RF
pulses each resulting in an echo.
Echo Train Length - Number of echoes in the same TR.
Fat Signal - There is a “normal” phenomenon called “J coupling” which occurs between
the nuclei of lipid molecules, causing intrinsic shortening of T2 signal. The fast repetition
of 180 degree pulses mess up the J couples and cause the T2 of fat to lengthen.
Testable Point: T2 fat signal is longer with fast spin echo (interferes with J coupling).
Testable Vocab: With each progressive echo train the transverse signal gradually
decreases. This is called “T2 Blurring”
Testable Point: Acquisition time is approximately proportional to 1/ETL

This time instead of starting with a 90 degree RF pulse, you start with a 180 degree
“preparation” pulse. You wait for the relaxation of the thing you want to saturate (water, fat,
myocardium) to hit its null point then you slam it with the 90 degree pulse. This way that
particular tissue gives no signal.
“TI” - The time between the 180 and 90 pulse.
“STIR ” - Short TI Inversion Recovery, employs a short TI
(120- 160 ms) to suppress fat, which is based on the TI for
fat. Often used on MSK imaging to suppress the fat within
bone marrow to allow visualization of fluid (edema) within
bone.
“FLAIR ” - Fluid Attenuated Inversion Recovery, employ a
TI designed to suppress water signal (approximately 2000
ms). Frequently used in Neuro-imaging to suppress CSF
signal (based on the TI relaxation time of CSF) to allow
visualization of edema or inflammation (i.e. in multiple
sclerosis).
Testable Point: Relative to other fat suppression techniques - STIR is much less “susceptible”
to magnetic susceptibility (metal) and field inhomogeneity
Testable Point: STIR can NOT be used with Gadolinium. Gd+ enhanced tissues have a
similar TI as fat, so they may get totally nulled out.
Testable Point: A tradeoff for Inversion Recovery sequences is a longer study as the extra
pulse and subsequent “time to inversion (TI)” increases the time to repetition (TR).
299

GRE Sequences
Gradient echo sequences are different than spin
echo sequences because they (1) use a flip
angle less than 90 and (2) do NOT have a 180
pulse.
The advantage to this low flip angle is faster
recovery, shorter TR/TE times, and a faster
scan. The flip angle is used to determine how
much transverse and how much longitudinal
magnetization is going to be used.
Testable Point: Because there is no 180 pulse
you are dealing with T2* (not T2). For the
same reason GRE is more susceptible to
susceptibility artifacts.
Testable Point: Gradient sequences have a lower Specific Absorption Rate (less heating).
I f there is no 180 pulse, how do you create an echo? A bipolar readout gradient (basically a
frequency encoding gradient) is used.
Vocab: An echo in gradient is called a “Field Echo”
I f gradient is so fast and has a nice low SAR, why don’t we use it fo r everything? The signal
to noise ratio isn’t that great. Plus, you get more susceptibility artifacts.
“Steady State ” Because the TR is shortened in GRE you can get stuck with permanent
residual transverse magnetization - where the magnetization never completely goes away.
What is often the case is that the TR is shorter than the Tl and T2 of the tissues, so T2*
dephasing dominates. The two main flavors in GRE imaging are classified depending on how
this residual transverse magnetization is handled.
Spoiled (incoherent) GRE: Gradients and/or RF pulses are used to get
rid of the transverse magnetization (T2) that is persisting in the steady
state.
Refocused (coherent) GRE: The steady state is preserved by using a
“Rewind gradient.” Therefore these sequences are T2* weighted. These
refocused sequences can be partial or full. An example of the full refocus
is the SSFP (steady-state free precession) which is used in cardiac
imaging to provide a fast sequence with high signal to noise.
Spoiled
(Incoherent) =
Basically Tl
Refocused

EPI can be done with spin echo (90 + 180) or gradient echo (90 + a bunch of gradients). EPI is
MRI’s fastest acquisition method, on the order of 100 ms/slice. One RF pulse is used to
acquire data for an image (aka single shot).
The sequence works by turning the phase encoding and frequency encoding gradients on and
off very rapidly - causing a very fast filling of k space.
Compared to a regular GRE: The sequence is more vulnerable to magnetic susceptibility,
gives you better tissue contrast, and is faster.
Artifacts linked to EPI:
Magnetic Susceptibility - can be improved with segmented sequences (instead of single
shots)
Ghosting - Gradient imperfections mess with spatial encoding
Chemical Shift - A narrow readout bandwidth is used. As mentioned before, a fat readout
bandwidth improves chemical shift (lets a bigger range in), a narrow one does the
opposite.
Testable Trivia:
•
Echo-planar is the technique of choice for Diffusion Weighted Imaging
•
Echo-planar is highly vulnerable to magnetic susceptibility (even more than normal
gradient sequences)
301

Diffusion
The base sequence is either a fast GRE or an echo planar. Generally speaking, diffusion
weighted imaging works by using two very strong and symmetric MR gradients. The
acquisition is repeated in each of the 3 dimensions in space with b-factors of 0 and b-factors
of 1000. The signal differences are based on mobility and direction of water.
Scenario 1 (no net movement): The first gradient fires dephasing the spins. The molecules do
not move. The second gradient fires rephasing the same molecules - giving you high signal.
Scenario 2 (net movement): The first gradient fires dephasing the spins. The molecules move
out of the way. The second gradient fires missing the original protons - gives you low signal.
Vocab: The direction of diffusion is described as isotropic (movement in all spatial directions)
and anisotropic (movement in a single direction).
“B-Factor” - The best way to think about “B-Factors” & Diffusion is to
consider it a type of tissue weighting - just like TE and TR - which as
we’ve discussed are the factors that determine it you produce a T2, TI, or
PD. The “B-Factor” controls how much a tissues apparent diffusion
coefficient (ADC) contributes to the study. The higher the “B-Factor” the
greater the diffusion weighting.
B Zero = If you set the contribution of diffusion to zero, and assuming
your TR and TE are long - which they usually are - then you basically
have a T2. This is why people call the B Zero a “poor mans T2.” In other
words - if there is something with high signal it’s because it has a long T2
time.
B 500 or 1000 = With an increase in “B-Factor” - usually to 500 and then
1000 - the image weighting changes from T2 - to become more and more
diffusion. In other words - if there is something with high signal it’s
because it has a low apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC).
ADC (Apparent Diffusion Coefficient); - As I’m sure you know an ADC is needed to read
diffusion correctly. The ADC map is calculated by obtaining (a) set of images without a
diffusion gradient (B0 “poor mans T2”), and (b) set of images with the diffusion gradient
(B500-B1000). A negative logarithm of the ratios is performed. Low signal is true restriction,
high signal is T2 shine through.
302

Additional Sequences - Mentioned For Completeness:
Perfusion imaging
•
Perfusion MRI quantitates cerebral micro-vascularization parameters. These parameters
include regional blood flow and blood volume and mean transit time.
Functional MRI (fMRI)
•
The basics underlying fMRI are increased blood flow to local vasculature that
accompanies neural activity resulting in local reduction of deoxyhemoglobin.
Deoxyhemoglobin acts as a contrast agent because it is paramagnetic (thus alters T2* MR
signal). Take home point = fMRI depends on T2* effects.
In fMRI procedures: First the patient performs a task, then Blood Oxygen Level
Dependent (BOLD) imaging is done before and after the task. The pre and post task
images are then subtracted and overlaid on a gray-scale brain image to localize the signal
origin.
Angiography: 2D time o f flight (TOF) MRA, 3D-TOF MRA, Phase contrast MRA
•
2D-TOFMRA: Uses a gradient echo sequence where a saturation pulse is employed to
null venous or arterial blood flow. Has a SMALL VOXEL SIZE.
•
3D-TOFMRA: Is collected as a 3D volume, as opposed to slices, and allows for smaller
voxels than 2D. 3D-TOF is well-suited for high flow arterial systems like the Circle of
Willis. Benefits include a higher SNR than 2D, a shorter imaging time, more smooth
vessel contours, and better saturation (which limits venous circulation).
•
Phase contrast MRA: Uses bipolar gradients to create contrast from flow. High velocity
encoding time (VENC) is needed for arterial imaging, and low VENC for veins and
sinuses. Phase contrast MRA is a quantitative image and can measure mean blood flow
velocity and direction.
•
In general: TOF MRA is faster and less sensitive to signal loss from turbulent vessels
than Phase Contrast MRA. Phase contrast MRA has advantages in increased background
suppression and decreased sensitivity to intravoxel dephasing.
303

MRI Spectroscopy
WTF is this “M R I Spectroscopy ” ? The general idea is that the various metabolites that exist on the
cellular level (choline, lactate, N-acetylaspartate “NAA,” etc... etc...) occur in different concentrations
depending on the pathology. For example, “NAA” is a neuronal marker. Things that destroy neurons
(like tumors) will decrease NAA. So, in general the lower the NAA the higher the grade tumor. I talk
about this stuff in volume 2 o f Crack the Core.
How is this sorcery performed ?
The physics is pretty similar to conventional MRI. In fact, you can used a variety o f sequence types
including vary basic spin ECHO. Some difference worth knowing are:
(1) Instead o f using the information from the frequency gradient to provide locational information - that
data is used to help identify the different chemical patterns in a methods similar to the way NMR worked
in your undergrad organic chemistry class (electron shielding, resonance frequency, etc etc... so on and so
forth). Localization is a little more complicated - the short version is that you can use three orthogonally
selective slice with 90 degree pulses. The signal will then be produced from the intersection o f the three
planes “STEAM” they call it. There are other methods too “PRESS” is one - it’s beyond the scope of the
exam (probably).
(2) Fat and water are deliberately avoided. In conventional MRI you are using signal from the entire
region o f interest (example the brain, CSF, skull, and scalp) to make up the picture. If you tried to do that
with MRS the peaks o f fat and water would scale out to be sky scrapers, with the remaining metabolites
like little ants or lego men / women. This is avoided in two ways: (a) you need to choose a voxel for
MRS that is within the brain - avoiding the high fat areas of the bone marrow and scalp - “outer volume”
suppression is also sometimes needed - to suppression signal from the boxes around the target, (b) you
need some method o f water suppression - either an inversion sequence o f chemical shift suppression. This
entire process becomes difficult if you are using MRS on trial lawyers (bones for brains) - but that
situation is beyond the scope o f the exam (probably).
Why do is see “PPM” on these diagrams ? PPM stands for parts per million. You might think this
relates to the quantity o f that specific metabolite but you’d be wrong. It is actually the percent of the
Larmor Frequency (lppm = 1 millionth o f the Larmor frequency). This is important because each
metabolite will have a unique frequency distribution. For example NAA is a 2.0 ppm.
Why are the numbers counting backwards on the scale ? — “because I’m your mother thats why.”
How does one tell how much of something there is ? You look at the signal intensity (“Y axis”), but
you’ll find the ratios are overall more useful.
Other random physics tips ? Good “Shimming” is important - it is necessary for adequate signal-to-
noise. If your lines look flat - “shimming” is probably a good option to choose as a fix.
What about TE - Does that matter ? This is complicated. Yes... it does matter, and the nuts and bolts
of it would require a discussion o f J-Coupling. I don’t want to go there. Just think about it like this:
Initial Exam: short TE ~ 30 msec, is often used to start because it has a good signal to noise and
demonstrates a broad range of metabolites (with both short and long T2 relaxation times). This
Follow Up Exam : For More Characterization: You can sample multiple voxels with longer TEs. This
will only show metabolites with a long T2 ~ 270 msec (NAA, Creatine, Choline).
304

Fat Saturation Techniques
There are two broad categories.
•Inversion Sequences (STIR) that are based on the inversion time “T.I.” of fat
•Those that exploit the resonance difference between fat and water protons.
STIR - As described previously, this is a short inversion sequence (180, followed by a well
timed 90). It does great with metal artifact. You can NOT use Gd with it.
Selective Pulse: Remember one of the limitation of STIR is that you can’t give Gd with it -
so this is the method used most commonly for contrast enhanced studies.
Water
Unlike STIR which works by capitalizing on the differences in
inversion times. Selective saturation works by capitalizing on the
resonance difference between protons within their respective
microenvironment. For example, protons in fat precess about 74 Hz
slower than water at 0.5 Hz. This precession difference gets more
exaggerated with a stronger magnet - per that Larmor equation
which I mentioned near the beginning of the chapter (Precession
Frequency = Gyromagnetic Radio x Field Strength in Tesla).
The fat saturation is a 3 step process.
(1) Preparatory Pulse - This is done by
dropping a very narrow RF pulse
just at the resonance of fat.
(2) Spoiler Gradient - This results in dephasing of the protons
primed by the predatory pulse - eliminating their ability to
produce a signal. The remaining protons (the one’s that aren’t
fat) will be the only ones left able to give signal.
Dephasing “Spoiler” Gradient -
(3) Proceed with the scan as normal - sending the first RF pulse immediately after the spoiler
gradient.
Trivia: This requires excellent field homogeneity
Trivia: This is NOT typically done with very low field strength magnets (less that 1.0T)
because you can’t accurately separate the peaks between fat and water.
Only Fat Peak Selected
RF Pulse Sent at
the Specific
Resonance of Fat
Fat Flipped
Water NOT
305

Fat Related Topics:
In Phase and Out of Phase: The chemical environments of fat and water are different for
protons. This causes these protons to precess at different rates. To create “in and out of
phase” images a spoiled GRE is performed when the protons are spinning with each other
(about 4.4 msec at 1.5T) and directly out of phase of each other (2.2 msec at 1.5 T).
Think about this like two runners on a track. At the beginning of the race they are lined up
together. The faster of the two (water) will get out to a lead - and at 2.2msec (assuming a 1,5T
magnet) will be directly across the track from the slow one (fat) - this is when they are
“opposed.” At this point in time their signal will cancel out. As the race continues eventually
the fast one (water) will begin to lap the slow one (fat) - and at 4.4msec will have gone all the
way around and cut him. At this point they are in phase. The cycle repeats every 2.2msec
(assuming a 1.5T).
The idea is that microscopic fat
will drop out on the out of phase.
Out - Signal Loss In - Signal Normal Out - Signal Loss
From Fat Liver
From Fat Liver
From Fat Liver
Start / Peak: The signal drop out that you can visualize starts around 15% fatty infiltration and
peaks at 50%. Yes... it peaks at 50%. You will actually see less drop out at 75%. The reason
is at greater that 50% there are fewer water molecules (relative to fat) to cancel out the fat
signal.
What i f it’s a 3T magnet? Then the changes occur twice as fast.
1.5 T: Out of Phase 2.2 msec, In Phase 4.4 msec, Out of Phase 6.6 msec, In Phase 8.8 msec
3.0 T: Out of Phase 1.1 msec, In Phase 2.2 msec, Out of Phase 3.3 msec, In Phase 4.4 msec
306

Does it matter when (chronologically) you obtain the out of phase sequence? In other words in
you obtain out of phase at 2.2 msec, and in phase at 4.4 msec is that the same as getting out of
phase at 6.6 msec and in phase at 4.4 msec ? Yes/No - But ultimately yes.
The No - If you are looking for Hepatic Steatosis, then it does NOT matter. If the liver is fatty
you will see signal loss on out of phase no matter when you get it. Out of phase is out of phase,
and the reason out of phase is out of phase is because of the precision differences in fat and
water. The water is lapping the fat on the track.
The Yes - What about Hemochromatosis? The iron liver. Remember way back to the beginning
of this masterpiece of a chapter when I talked about T2*? The quick refresher is basically loss of
signal occurs faster when you have an inhomogeneous field. The 180 degree pulse of Spin Echo
is used to try and smooth things out. Remember in and out of phase is a GRE sequence - so no
180 degree refocusing pulse. Therefore T2* is alive and well.
Think about iron as a “magnet within a magnet” - and that iron liver magnet is going to fuck the
field up in that local area. So you end up having T2* effects that are even more pronounced.
That is why signal is lost with Iron. Totally different mechanism than the fat. In other words,
Signal is lost as a function of time. The
longer you wait the darker it gets. Whatever
you obtain second will look darker than the
thing you obtained first.

(or 1.1msec on a 3T).
2.2 msec - Out
4.4 msec - In
6.6 msec - Out
The Correct Way - Here we didn’t wait and
obtained out of phase at 2.2msec, and in phase at
4.4 msec.

did get the in phase at 4.4 msec, and then snagged

Chemical Shift Artifact (The Fat Artifact): This will be mentioned again when 1 talk about
artifacts, but I want to just touch on it briefly as it’s related to fat. Again, because of the
difference in proton environments between fat and water precession differences cause an
artifact in the read out (frequency encoding) direction.
There are two subtypes.
Type 1: This one manifestation is a bright rim on one side and a dark rim on the other. This can
occur on either Spin Echo or GE sequences.
Testable Trivia: Chemical shift increases with field strength (it’s not seen below 1 T)
Testable Trivia: Chemical shift decreases with increased gradient strength
Testable Trivia: Chemical shift decreases with a wider read out bandwidth
Type 1 Chemical Shift Artifact
Frequency Encoding Direction
Type 2: India Ink Artifact (Black Boundary) -
This type of chemical shift artifact shows a black
line in all directions of the fat-water interface. On
Gradient Echo sequences, if a voxel has about
50% fat and 50% water the signals will cancel out
- leaving this black line.
Testable Trivia: Using Spin Echo sequences will
get rid of the India Ink, but NOT the Chemical
Shift.
Type 2 Chemical Shift Artifact
“India Ink”
308

MR Contrast (Gd+)
Broadly speaking MRI contrast can be grouped into either “positive agents” which cause bright Tl
signal (shortens Tl), or “negative agents” which produce magnetic inhomogeneity from
susceptibility leading the T2 shortening.
Gadolinium: This is a highly toxic metal that can cause all kinds of badness even in small doses -
including liver necrosis. To solve this issue, Gd is complexed with a chelate. The toxicity, clearance
routes, bio-distribution, and relaxation properties are all attributes to the chelating agent (DTPA).
How do Gd+3 cause a Tl shortening?
• Short answer: Gd is strongly paramagnetic. “Paramagnetic” means it can be magnetized, and when
it does, it tends to be polarized in a way that is the same as the magnetic field - locally augmenting
it.
• Longer answer: Gd+3has seven unpaired electrons, and the interaction of these ELECTRONS
causes augmentation of the external magnetic field. The field of the electrons is very short so the
contrast agent needs to be right next to the tissue to have an effect on it.
Why does Gd+3 “only” shorten Tl ? It actually doesn’t. It just shortens Tl more. You could say that
the “dominant effect” is on Tl. The reason for this preferential choice is complicated, and has a lot
to do with relaxation rates of tissues in the body. The easiest way to conceptualize it is to remember
that most tissues in the body have a natural disparity in their baseline relaxation times, with Tl being
about 5 times (sometimes 10 times) longer than T2. So the effects are more noticeable on T l. If that
doesn’t make it clear then make the following flash card. On one side write “why does Gd
preferentially shorten T l” on the other side write “IT JUST FUCKING DOES.” Many MRI physics
concepts can be learned using a similar flashcard set.
Does the Tl shortening effect o f Gd change with different magnet strengthens ? Yes, it gets stronger
with a stronger field strength. However, the caveat is only between a 0.5 T and 1.5 T is the increase
significant. The change from 1.5 to 3.0 T is much less noticeable - although still present.
What about T2 effects ? At weak concentration
the T2 effect is essentially nothing. However,
at high concentration (classically seen as a
“pseudolayer” the bladder) T2 effects
dominate. MRI Perfusion techniques use this
T2 effect by using a concentrated bolus in the
first intravascular passage - causing a drop in
both T2* and T2 signal.
Tl Shortening = Bright
T2 Shortening = Dark
309

Extracellular Agents: These are the most clinically used agents that have a brief vascular
phase, followed by an equilibrium in the extracellular space. All o f these agents are very
hydrophilic.
Blood Pool Agents: Keeping the agent in the blood can be done two ways (1) by using a
large Gd ligand, or (2) by binding the Gd to a big sugar or protein molecule
Pharmacokinetics:
• Elimination is primarily renal
• Some agents (Gd-BOPTA and Gd-EOB-DTPA) have more excretion in the bile
• In general, Gd cannot pass through a normal (intact) blood brain barrier.
• Although there is that brain autopsy paper that showed Gad deposition in the dentate

people pretend it was a big deal. Also prompted a bunch of frivolous litigation against
Radiology - because “what else do we not know ?” - so why not blame all medical
maladies on the MRI contrast. Dumb! Everyone knows vaccinations are the real evil.
• Chuck Norris famously sued everyone because “MRI medicine poisoned his wife.”
• A cobra once bit Chuck Norris in the leg. After 3 days of agonizing pain, the cobra died.
Storage: Gadolinium contrast agents are stored and administered at room temperature. CT
Contrast is sometimes “warmed” prior to administration.

MRI Artifacts
A lot of artifact testable fodder has to do with phase vs frequency encoding direction. So let us
review that briefly, then we will discuss the artifacts.
When we sample signal for each plane we have an “X” and a “Y” direction for the grid - one will
be phase the other frequency. You CAN do it either way, and sometimes flipping them is a way
to deal with flow (motion) or wrap around (aliasing) artifacts.
What influences the choice?
A - Scan Time. Remember that the number of phase encoding steps is a key component to the
table time equation. More steps = More time. That is why scan time is the most important
consideration, because scan time = money (and despite what Academic Radiologists will tell
you: money = happiness). The Hospital Administrator who went to school for 6 years and makes
triple what your Chairman makes would agree also.
As a general rule phase encoding
direction is the shorter distance.
• Front-Back in the Abd,
• Side-Side in the Head.
• Sagittal Direction for 3D Spine
Shorter Direction - “usually ” the phase encoding direction
B - Artifact Reduction. Moving the pulsation from the aorta off of that liver lesion. Moving
the respiratory artifact off the posterior 1/3 of the breasts. Notable exception to the “General
Rule” = Breast MRI is typically PE in the Side to Side Direction, to keep chest wall movement
artifact off the breasts.
C - Coil Design Restrictions.
311

Image Process Artifacts:
These include aliasing, chemical shift, truncation, and partial volume.
Aliasing: This error occurs when an area is under sampled. You get wrapping of anatomy
from under sampled portions. This occurs in the phase encoding direction. Fix:
• MAKE THE FOY BIGGER: Seems obvious. The follow-up question is this... what is the
cost? Why not just make the FOVfucking huge every time to avoid this? The answer is
spatial resolution. Large FOV = Shitty Spatial Resolution. Ever try and look at the spine on
a body MRI? Think you can grade narrowing/stenosis accurately on that mother fucker?
The reason it is blurry is because your FOV is large. Large FOV = Shitty Spatial Resolution.
So the counter to the counter is that you have to increase phase encoding steps to maintain
the spatial resolution -which will lengthen the time of the scan, cost the imaging center
money, and get you a threatening email from your Insect Overlord (hospital administrator).
I, for one, welcome our new insect overlords
• Flip the PE/FE directions to move the artifact off a specific area of interest (sometimes this
works - other times it is just not possible). This also increases table time (costs money) if
you move the PE direction into the longer part of the body.
• Surface Coils - This works by minimizing signal from tissue outside the FOV. Surface coils
won’t detect signal far away from it.
• Sat Bands - Eliminating signal from tissue outside
Gamesmanship:
A tricky way to show this or ask this would be to
show you wrap around (“aliasing”) artifact in the
AP direction and ask you how phase/frequency
directions were selected.
This makes you work backward to realize that
the scan was done in a non-traditional way
(remember that heads are usually done side-side).
The artifact is occurring in the AP direction.
So, that must be the phase encoding
312

Chemical Shift: As discussed (page 291), the protons of different molecules precess at
different frequencies. The difference between water and fat is around 220 Hz at 1.5 T (even
worse at 3 T). This shift in the Larmor frequency is the so called “chemical shift.” The type 1
error occurs in the frequency encoding direction (opposite of most of artifacts which are P.E.).
Fixing Type 1:
• Usually this doesn’t cause a big enough problem to need “fixing” but for the purpose of
multiple choice exams here are a few solutions.
• Swap PE and FE positions to move the artifact off the area of interest.
• Use STIR or other fat suppression techniques
• Increase the Receiver Bandwidth
Fixing Type 2:
• Adjust the TE
• Perform Spin Echo Sequence (remember this only occurs with GRE)
Partial Volume: This is just like in CT. You have different signal intensities in different
adjacent structures. If they overlap in a signal voxel - you get averaging. This can result in an
intermediate signal from mixing of high and low intensities.
Making it better: Make your pixels smaller. More slices in the z-direction.
313

Truncation I Gibbs: The transformation of K space through inverse fourier transform
ideally (but never) resulting in a block of data. Ripples in this data - especially at abrupt intense
tissue changes result in the appearance of lines. You classically see these at high contrast
interfaces (skull -brain, Cord-CSF, and meniscus/fluid). The CSF-Cord interface is the most
classic - mimicking a syrinx. If prompted I would say the cause is limited sampling of free
induction decay. It can be seen in both the frequency encoding and phase encoding directions but
is more commonly seen in the phase encoding because many times a phase encoding matrix
that is smaller than the readout matrix is selected to reduce time.
Gamesmanship: The classic way to show this is sagittal view of the spine - looking like a syrinx.
Making it better:
• Short answer = more matrix.
• Long answer = Decreasing the bandwidth or decreasing pixel size (more PE steps, less FOV,
more matrix).
Improvement penalty: Increased acquisition time and reduced per-pixel signal to noise.
Gibbs at the Skull - Brain
Gibbs - Mimicking a Syrinx
314

Motion: This can be seen with both voluntary and involuntary (cardiac, respiratory)
movements. Motion creates difference between frequency encoding (which is fast) and phase
encoding (which is slow). You will see ghosting or smearing - primarily in the phase encoding
direction. You can also see the classic pulsation artifact from the aorta.
Making it Better:
•
If the motion is “voluntary” you should start by yelling at the patient (“SIR! SIR! PLEASE
STOP MASTURBATING! - You are creating a motion artifact that is degrading imaging
quality!).
• Perform Breath Holding - This is the best method for respiratory motion.
• Respiratory Gating (increases acquisition time)
• ROPE (Respiratory Ordered Phase Encoding) - Phase encoding steps are ordered with
respiration
•
Breathing Navigator - An echo from the diagram determines its position, then timing and
acquisition are based off this.
• Apply a fat sat band across the abdomen
• Switch the phase encoding direction.
• Fancying Shit like BLADE / PROPELLER - which use radial sampling methods (like a
helicopter “blade” or “propeller”) to oversample the center of k-space. This gives you
redundant information for error correction.
Patient Related Artifacts:
These include Motion and Magic Angle
Pulsation - From the Aorta
Breathing Artifact
315

Flow: This is a type of motion artifact, related to blood flow. Blood flow causes ghosting
in the phase encoding direction. GRE sequences are more susceptible to this than SE
sequences.
SE Sequences - Flow looks dark. Moving blood that got hit with the 90 pulse, moves
out of the way prior to getting the 180 pulse. Therefore, it doesn’t have any signal.
GRE Sequences: In flowing blood looks bright.
Making it better:
•
Apply a saturation band adjacent to the imaging section - 90 pulse followed by crusher
gradient.
W hat M akes A Flow Void?
The dark hole of a patent vessel is called a “flow void.” This is generally a spin echo
imaging finding (as stated above - flow looks dark on SE).

Why does this happen? To make an echo, the proton must be exposed to both a 90 pulse
and a 180 pulse pulse. If the blood is flowing fast it will get hit with the 90 degree, but
miss the 180 degree pulse. This means no echo, and thus no signal.
316

Magic Angle: This is an MSK artifact seen with tendons. You see this with short echo time
(TE) sequences where the focus forms an angle of 55 degrees with the main magnetic field
(magic angle phenomenon). This will NOT be seen in T2 sequences (with long TE). This
phenomenon, is reduced at higher field strengths due to greater shortening of T2 relaxation
times.
Magic Angle: You see it on Short TE (T1, PD, GRE)
Then it goes away
on Long TE (T2)
Why does it go away with a long TE ?
Remember “T2” is 37% of the original signal. Tendons that interact with the magnet have a
decrease in tissue interactions. The result is a prolonged time needed to get to that 37%.
However, if you wait long enough (longer TEs) those differences equal out. This is why longer
TE sequences (classic “T2”) are not troubled by the mischief of this sinister phenomenon.

Cross Talk: RF and FT pulses are not perfectly rectangular. So if they are placed close
enough together then you get excitation of neighboring section more than once in a single
repetition. These lead to partial saturation and lower signal. So all sections (except the ones
on the ends will be subjected to this).
RF Related:
These include Cross Talk and Zipper Artifacts
Making it better:
• Increasing the gap between sections.
• Interleave slices (all odds, then all evens).
Testable Trivia: The 3D images are not susceptible
to this artifact because the entire volume undergoes
excitation with sections within the volume acquired
with gradients.
Zipper A rtifact: There are lots of random stray RF signals (radio, tv, etc...). Remember
that the RF pulse is a “radio-wave.” Anyway, if you have defective or inadequate shielding
you can get a “zipper” of high signal - 1-2 pixels in width running across the image. This
typically extends in the phase encoding direction.
Gamesmanship: A possible scenario for this is that
anesthesia left the pulse ox monitor in the room.
Making it better:
• Try closing the door (if it’s open).
• Remove all electronic devices from the room.
• Call the tech people to repair the faulty RF
shielding.
• Alternatively, you could write an email, cc 15
people, and use the phrase “patient care”. That fixes
most problems.
Ideal Slice Profile
318

External Field Related:
This is Magnetic Inhomogeneity:
Inhomogeneous Fat Suppression: If the field is actually homogeneous you get a
uniform fat saturation by applying an RF pulse with the resonance of fat protons. In the real
world, local field inhomogeneities cause fat protons to precess at difference frequencies. These
differences allow certain areas of fat to resist suppression - which can mimic edema.
Making it better: Using an inversion recovery - STIR, especially in the setting of metal.
T2FS: Fat Sat in Marrow is ruined by
Improved with STIR
Metal messing with the local field
Shimming
Shimming is a thing that can be done to improve field homogeneity.When the magnet comes
from the manufacturer the field homogeneity is terrible. To correct for this a process called
“shimming” is used. There are two types of shimming, passive and active. Most of the time a
combination of the two is used; passive to get the thing going in the right direction and active to
optimize the field for each patient.
“
Passive Shimming - A phantom is scanned and the position of the shim plates is adjusted
until the field becomes homogeneous (are at least better than it was). This is done at the
time of installation.
•
Active Shimming - This is done by using the electromagnetic coil, and can be done after
each patient (or sequence). Essentially, gives you the chance to have a homogeneous field
(or nearly) regardless of the size of the patient.
319

Magnetic Susceptibility:
This includes diamagnetic, paramagnetic, and ferromagnetic
Susceptibility (includes “m etal”) - This refers to the ability of a substance to become
magnetized by the external field. Obviously metals have large susceptibility. Calcium
hydroxy apatite and accumulations of gadolinium chelate can do the same thing.
Generally speaking, susceptibility affects all pulse sequences, but is most severe with GRE
images and least severe with SE (because of the 180 degree refocusing pulse - to lose those T2*
effects).
A less prominent version occurs at tissue interfaces (bone and muscle, or air and bone). The
classic location for this is the transition from paranasal sinus to skull base.
Making it better:
• Using SE and FSE instead of GRE.
• Reduce the field strength (move them off the 3T onto the 1.5)
• Fast / Turbo Spin Echo sequences - High receiver bandwidth and short echo spacing
• Thin Slices
• Align the longitudinal axis of a metal implant with the axis of the main field.
• STIR does way better than frequency selective fat suppression.
Notice the blooming artifact from the metal clip is worse on the In Phase. That is
because in phase is traditionally done later (more time = more blooming).
A key concept is that susceptibility artifact worsens on in phase imaging relative to out of phase.
This has nothing to do with the phase of water and fat. Instead it has everything to do with in
phase being done later. The longer TE, the more susceptibility (T2*). Remember, air will do the
same thing.
It’s all about that T2* effect worsening over time
320

Gradient Related:
This includes Eddy Currents
Eddy Current: These things called “Eddy Currents” are generated when gradients are
rapidly turned on and off. The actual location of said currents can be in the magnet, the
cables, the wires or even in the patient. This looks like distortion (contraction or dilation of
the image) or shift/shear.
Testable Trivia: Most severe with DW1 pulses.
Making it better: Optimizing the sequence of gradient pulses.
FLAIR - for non distorted
Diffusion - Notice the stretch / smear at the brain - bone
comparison
interface. This is from Eddy Currents.
321

Errors in Data:
This includes dielectric effects and crisscross
Dielectric Effects / Standing Wave effects - Biologic tissues have a dielectric
constant that results in reduction of wavelength by the inverse of some constant. Interactions
can cause local eddy currents in the imaged tissues. Since RF waves are shorter at 3T - the
effects are worse with a stronger magnet. You also see this worsen with large bellies,
especially if they have ascites. Larger body parts (the abdomen) are primarily affected.
Classic Look / Location: Dark signal in the
central abdomen over the left lobe of the
liver.
Making it better:
•Application of dielectric pads - placed
between patient and anterior body array coil
•Parallel RF transmission (SENSE) - RF
pulses from a set of coils; each coil sends an
independent RF pulse. Gives you a longer
pulse.
Crisscross or Herringbone: If you see obliquely oriented stripes throughout the
image, you are probably dealing with this artifact. The cause is data processing and/or
reconstruction errors.
Making it better: Reconstruct the image again.
Dielectric Artifact

Special Topic - Cardiac MRI
It’s best to think of cardiac imaging in two flavors: (1) Bright Blood,
and (2) Dark Blood.
Bright Blood: These are gradient sequences, and they make up
the majority of the cardiac work load.
Steady State Free Precession “SSFP” - This is a type of gradient
sequence that is the primary sequence used for wall motion and
volume analysis. If you see a CINE MRI of the heart it is probably
a SSFP.
Dark Blood: The short answer is spin echo sequence. The longer
answer is double inversion recovery. WTF is “double inversion ” ?
Basically it’s two consecutive 180 pulses - one that is “non-
selective” and the next which is “selective” to the blood and heart.
Basically what you end up with is that all your in-slice tissue is left
alone, and your blood (which is flowing into the slice) is nulled -
hence the black blood.
Why use black blood? It’s good for anatomy. Also because it’s a
spin echo (and not GRE) you have less susceptibility artifacts, so
they can be of benefit when looking at the mediastinum in a patient
who has had a prior sternotomy (with metal wires).
Gradient Echo - Bright Blood
Spin Echo - Dark Blood
Inversion Recovery: Just like STIR nulls fat signal, and FLAIR
nulls CSF signal, cardiac MRI uses an inversion technique to null
myocardium. The reason you do this is to look for delayed
enhancement (scar).
Trivia to know about inversion recovery:
-
A “PSIR” or Phase Sensitive Inversion Recovery or a T.I.
Scout Series is done to help choose the correct time to use
for inversion. This will produce a series of hearts. You
want to pick the one with the darkest myocardium. This
will give you the correct inversion recovery time.
Why not just do it like FLAIR or STIR, with a set time?
Although myocardium usually nulls at around 330 msec, it’s
highly variable depending on the person
Nulled Myocardium
324

Special Topic - Breast MRI
There are a few artifacts and technical differences specific to breast MRI that are worth knowing
about. MRI of the breast is done for several reasons (high risk screening, implant rupture evaluation,
surgical planning, etc...). The reason for the exam will dictate sequences.
Testable Trivia: Breast CA screening will have dynamic post contrast sequences
Testable Trivia: Implant Rupture screening will NOT have contrast, but instead have a fat and water
saturated sequence (only silicone will be bright).
Testable Trivia: A breast specific coil is used to increase SNR.
Artifacts:
Chemical Shift: You see this all the time in breast MRI,
at the fat water interface. Remember this can be corrected by
increasing the bandwidth to capture both fat and water in the
same phase.
Motion / Breathing / Cardiac: Breathing and the beating of the heart would normally
degrade the image on breast MRI. This is corrected for by running the phase encoding direction side
to side , instead of front to back (like it is in body imaging).
Phase Encoding Direction:
-
Axial: Breast is left to right (Body Imaging is front to back)
-
Sagittal: Breast is top to bottom
Signal Flair - If the breast is too close to the coil element,
it will not fat sat out correctly (look bright). You can fix this
by repositioning the patient in the scanner.
Flair Artifact
Chemical Shift Artifact
325

Safety, B ioeffects, and FDA lim its
I want you to think about MRI safety in two basic categories:
(1) Potential Safety Risk of the Magnet Systems (of which there are 3 categories)
(2) Patient Exposure Limits (set by the FDA).
Potential Safety Risks - There are 3 main categories broken down by the primary systems
involved. (1) Main Magnet, (2) Gradient Set, and (3) RF Transmit-Receive.
- Main M agnet -
Quick overview into how the magnet works: The magnet is basically a large coil of wire,
wrapped around the bore of the magnet. When you apply an electric current through the wire
you generate a magnetic field. Obviously the magnet is a very strong magnet so it requires a
shit ton of juice (100s of Amps). To put that much juice through a coil of wires requires you
to maintain a very low temperature (helps reduce resistance). MR scanner keep the wires cold
by submerging the coil (within a canister) in liquid helium.
When the scanner is first installed they ramp it up very slowly (hours). Shutting the magnet
down safely requires a similar procedure. As I’ve mentioned before the magnet is always on.
Mainly because it’s such a pain in the ass to ramp it down and back up again.
Emergency Shutdown
What if you have to shut it down fast? There is a process called a “quench.” The “quench”
works by venting all the liquid helium out of the room (via a pipe system - to the outside).
Without the super cold liquid helium the resistance in the wire increases rapidly and the
magnet fails.
Two scenarios when (the medical student) should push the button:
1.
If the patient or a tech is pinned by a wheel chair, stretcher, oxygen tank etc... (med
students and residents will have to wait for a slow ramp down - quenching is expensive)
2.
A fire in the room which endangers the life of the patient or tech (med students and
residents will report to the burn unit at the end o f their call shift)
326

Emergency Shutdown - Cont
Real life advice - Always have someone else push the button - preferably a tech, medical
student, or non-English speaking observer. It costs the hospital a TON of money - and they will
want your balls if you touch that button. Have the med student do it - tell him it’s required for
the rotation. Then when hospital administration comes after you - do what your attending would
do to you - deny you ever told him to do it. No one will believe him - especially when he’s
wearing one of those dorky short white coats.
Quench - If the hospital administrators don’t kill you, the actual quench might. The process
alone can be dangerous. Remember it works by venting all that liquid helium out of the room
(and building) through a pipe. Well.... what if there is a hole in the pipe? Have you seen the
maintenance workers at your hospital? Those are the guys putting the pipe in. One little crack in
that thing and the room will be flooded (under pressure) with helium. This is really bad for a few
reasons (1) it will displace the oxygen , (2) the pressure will pin the door closed , making escape
impossible.
Protocol to Avoid Death:
(1) Have the tech or medical student push the button
(2) Get the fuck out of the room before the quench starts. No one should be in that room - other
than the trapped patient (or staff member). I’m serious - if there is a hole in that pipe your
ass is deader than fried chicken.
(3) If the hospital administration investigates why the button was pushed - blame everything on
the techs and medical students.
Gamesmanship - A code in the scanner is NOT a reason to quench the magnet.
The 5 G Line
If you’ve gone into the room with the magnet (zone 4)
before - you may have noticed a line drawn around the
magnet with “5G” written on it.
This is the 5 - Gauss Exclusion Zone. Essentially
outside of this line the magnetism from the field won’t
fuck up your pacemaker, insulin pump, or vagus nerve
stimulator. It’s a risk to implanted devices line.
Important Point: This does NOT mean translational force (pulling) can’t occur. 5G has
nothing to do with that.
Per NASA, at the surface of the Earth, the magnetic field
has a strength of around 0.5 Gauss
327

Implanted Devices:
Speaking of implanted devices, now is as good of a time as any to discuss the trivia related to
these things. There are book (large books) full of safe vs unsafe devices. Compatibility is
classified as either - Safe, Unsafe, or Conditional. Conditional means safe for specific MR
environments - for example a certain magnet strength, etc...
Each of these classifications comes with a
specific sign.
MR Conditional
MR Unsafe
MR Safe
There are so many damn devices that are safe or unsafe that it is simply not worth the effort to
try and memorize them all. Plus, the list is constantly in flux as manufacturers create new
“MR Safe” versions of devices. Am I saying the question writers will not ask you about a
device ? Of course not. There is no limit to the bullshit they might ask. 1 am saying it is
extremely low yield and not worth the effort. If the different between passing and failing has
come down to you knowing which brand of penile implant is MR conditional then, in the
immortal words of Kurt Osiander, “you fucked up a long time ago.”
Translational Force
If a magnetic object is within the field of the MR unit - that object is subject to translational force
(getting pulled into the magnet). This is the oxygen tank firing across the room. Wheel chair
flying across the room. Stretcher flying across the room.
Things to remember:
(1) Don’t bring the oxygen tank into zone 4 (room with the magnet).
(2) Translational force has NOTHING TO DO WITH THE 5 G LINE!
328

- G radient Set-
Noise: MRIs are noisy, and they can be very noisy depending on the sequences you run. The
noise (most of it anyway) actually originates from the gradient coils - secondary to rapid
changes in current.
Testable Trivia: Gradient Intensive Sequences - such as echo-planar imaging are the loudest.
Remember diffusion is an example of echo-planar.
Testable Trivia: The FDA sets a maximum of 140 dB for MR (99dB for patients with hearing
protection).
Testable Trivia: Damage to the fetal ossicles is part of the theoretical (complete bullshit)
reason fetal MRI / pregnancy MRI is considered possibly dangerous. The real reason is
Radiologists tend to be dickless weenies (present company excluded of course).
Neurostimulation: Not as fun as it sounds... What we are talking about here are induced
electrical currents leading to painful neurostimulation. You typically hear people complain
about this in the arms and legs, where the gradient magnetic field is changing most rapidly.
Trivia: High-bandwidth readouts and rapid gradient switching (echo-planar imaging) are the
usual culprits.
Trivia: Reducing the risk - (1) Reduce readout bandwidth, (2) Increased the TR
329

- RF Transm it - Receive -
SAR - This brings us to a discussion on SAR. SAR stands for Specific Absorption Rate -
which is basically describes the ability to cook (heat) a patient via the energy of the RF pulse.
The scientific quantification of this is measured in watts/kg
Let’s look at the formula - only for understanding some possible questions.
SAR = Bo2 x Alpha2 x Duty Cycle
Let me explain it - because it’s written in “math talk.”
Bo2 The strength of the magnet. The Important Relationship is the “2.” So if you double the
field strength from 1.5T to 3.0T then you quadrupled your SAR.
Alpha2 Alpha is the “Flip Angle.” Again the Important Relationship is the “2.” So if you
double the flip angle from 15 to 30 then you doubled your SAR. You can infer that spin echo -
with a 90 degree flip is going to have more SAR than a GRE with flips less than 90.
Duty Cycle - 1 like to think about this as the “cool down.” The longer your TR - the more cool
down you get. So increasing your TR, will decrease your Duty Cycle. It’s a little confusing, but
just remember the concept of letting the tissue cool down. Also notice it’s a 1:1. So Double the
TR - will half the Duty Cycle. Half the Duty Cycle will Half your SAR.
Potential ways you could ask a question about this:
1. I f you double the Bo, you do what to the SAR ? It Quadruples
2. I f you double the Flip Angle (example 15 to 30), you do what to the SAR ? It Quadruples
3. I f you double the Duty Cycle (make the TR 1/2), you do what to the SAR? It Doubles
4. Which has a higher SAR, Spin Echo or Gradient? Spin Echo (they have higher flip angles -
especially inversions).
5.
What are the limits? Standards stipulate that no tissue shall endure a temperature increase of
greater than 1 degree C. FDA limits are 4 W/kg over 15 minutes and 3 W/kg over 10
minutes.
330

Misc Heating / Burning Issues:
" Touching The Bore - Nonconducting pads are used to provide separation between the skin
and the side of the magnet bore, which usually contains both radiofrequency and gradient
coils. Touching the bore = possible burn. Way to prevent? Pad the skin.
" Cables, Jewelry, and even Medicated Patches can heat up and bum the patient. Remove
them prior to the scan.
■ Douchey silvery shirts - i.e.“Affliction” shirts , shouldn’t be worn into the magnet - they
can heat up and bum. Hospital gown is the safe thing to put the patient in.
■ Closed Loops - It’s possible that a bum causing loop can be formed between one extremity
touching another (hand on leg, leg on leg). The solution is to pad the area between the
knees.
" “Antenna Effect” - occurs when an electric field resonant coupling with a wire. When can
that happen? Abandoned Intracardiac Pacemaker Lead - are at risk for this and therefore
contraindicated. Temporary Epicardial Leads are supposedly safe.
C ontrast A gent S afety Issues
The concern with Gadolinium contrast agents is Nephrogenic Systemic Fibrosis. It’s rare as
hell - but is a real thing.
Critical Point = GFR must be > 30 to administer Gadolinium.
Critical Point = “On Dialysis” doesn’t make it ok. You may (or may not) be able to dialyze
the Gadolinium out.

Weekly QC for accredited MR scanners (per ACR and performed by MR technologist):
Center Frequency, table positioning, setup and scanning, geometric accuracy, high
contrast resolution (verified by phantom), low contrast resolution, artifact analysis, film
quality control, and visual checklist.
•
Annual QC for accredited MR scanners (per ACR and performed by medical physicist or
MR scientist) includes: Magnetic field homogeneity, slice position accuracy, slice
thickness accuracy, radiofrequency coil check, display monitor check

MRI Zones
What are the 4 zones?
Zone I: No restriction. This is basically outside the building.
Zone II: No restriction. This is the waiting room and the dressing room. This is where
you can screen patients and control access to Zone 3 and 4.
Zone III: Restricted Room. This is typically the control room, where the MRI tech does
his/her thing. There should be some kind of a lock on the door between zone 2 and 3.
Zone IV: Restricted Room. This is the actual MRI scanner room (the same room as the
magnet).
