MRI Zone Trivia
Are there any exceptions for entry into restricted access zones? Access is restricted to zone
3 and 4 (without filling out the screening forms ect..
There are NO exceptions to the
guidelines restricting entry to zone 3 and 4. Even if the patient codes in the scanner there
are no exceptions (per the Kanal article cited below). The MRI techs should get them out of
the scanner, start CPR / stabilize the patient then get them out — preferably into zone 2 for
the code team.
Depending on who you ask or what you read - it might be ok to code
them in zone 3 (never zone 4). The cleanest answer would be code
them in zone 2.
If there is a code in the scanner should you quench the magnet? No!. The magnetic field
will still be around for a minute or two. Plus, that is really expensive and the bean counters
who run the hospital will be pissed. Instead, do CPR then bring them out of Zone 3 and 4.
MRI Screening begins with? A focused history to identify patients who may have metal in
them.
What if the Patient is insane, unconscious, or has terrible body odor and you can *t initiate
the screening process with him /h er ? You will need to ask family members, consult the
medical record, and possibly do screening x-rays (orbits etc...) as needed.
Kanal, Emanuel, et al. "ACR guidance document for safe MR practices: 2007. "American Journal of
Roentgenology 188.6 (2007): 1447-1474.

“Exposure” - The ability of x-rays to ionize air, measured in Roentgens (R). This is the
concentration, in air, of radiation at a specific point - and is the ionization produced in a specific
volume of air.
“Absorbed Radiation Dose” or “Radiation Dose” - The amount of energy absorbed
per unit mass at a specific point. It is measured in Gy or Rads (1 Gy = 100 rads). This is how
much energy from ionizing radiation has been absorbed in a small volume.
“Equivalent Dose” (EqD) - The absorbed dose of different types of radiation creates
different levels of biologic damage (thus measured in Sv). A weighting factor is used to adjust the
value. For example, an alpha particle can do more damage than an electron.
EqD = Dose x Weight Factor
Weight Factor = for x-rays and gamma rays this is 1, for alpha particles it s 20.
“Effective Dose” (EfD) - This takes into account whether radiation
has been absorbed by the specific tissue. In other words you are taking into
account the type of radiation and the variable sensitivity of the organ / body
part. If all the dose is absorbed then 1 Gy = 1 Sv. If you are dealing with
organs or specific body parts you have to use a “tissue weighting”
conversion factor.
EfD = EqD x Tissue Factor

K erm a
Kerma is a confusing topic, so I’m going to try my best and provide you with (a) some
conceptual understanding, and (b) trivia regarding how questions might be asked.
To start out with: KERMA stands for “kinetic energy released per unit mass.” I know that’s
not at all helpful for understanding it - but I just want you to realize it stands for something.
The concept is this - KERMA is a measurement someone invented to help estimate how much
PRIMARY energy actually gets transferred when you expose a person to x-rays (or any type
of radiation). Why did I write PRIMARY in caps and underline it? Well because I want you to
imagine that I yelled that word at you. So WTF do I mean by “PRIMARY” ? What I’m trying
to say is we are talking about the energy transfer from the primary interaction of photons on
tissue atoms. NOT the secondary production of scatter electrons which also contribute to the
overall dose.
What is this “Air Kerma ” ? Remember the entire point is to not count all the secondary
scatter etc... If you measure KERMA in the body you are going to have a hard time separating
all the secondary stuff. Plus you are going to have trouble estimating the kinetic energy lost
as heat. So someone smarter than me figured out that you should/could estimate KERMA in
the air prior to it interacting with tissue as a “pure” measurement of energy. If you measured
the KERMA right before it entered the body you might call it Entrance Air Kerma.
Kerma based on photon energy:
Low Energy (The Jeb Bush Photon): If you are dealing with a low energy photon, then
Kerma is going to be the same as the absorbed dose. All the dose gets absorbed - P.E.
Domination.
High Energy: If you are dealing with a high energy photon, then Kerma is going to be
MORE than the absorbed dose. This is because you are going to have photons shooting
right through - not contributing to dose - although these will still be counted in Kerma.
Tissue Dose Vs Kerma:
Remember the whole point of KERMA is to estimate dose. So how well does it hold up? In
general, tissue doses are higher than air kerma (usually around 10%). Why is that?
Because x-ray interaction with tissue is going to create scatter and secondary electrons which
will contribute to dose - but by definition not be included in KERMA.
337

What is “Kerma-Area Product (KAP)” also called “Dose
Area Product” ?- This is the amount of Kerma (potential
dose) multiplied by the cross sectional area of the x-ray
beam. This provides a better way to measure the total
radiation potentially incident on the patient. Think of it
more as a measurement of total radiation used in the exam,
more than the actual dose in the patient.
KAP = Dose x Cross Sectional Area
Conceptually understand that if you increase the juice, but concentrated it in a smaller area,
that you might not change your KAP (even though you increased the juice).
KAP Source Distance Independent
KAP - This is best thought of as “the whole beam.” The highest yield most testable point is
that KAP is totally independent of the source distance. You can measure it at any point
along its path and it will be the same.
How can that be? As you get further and further away from the source the dose decreases via
the inverse square law BUT the area increases via divergence also by the square. The two
cancel out.
Practical / Testable Point o f this source independence? Geometric Magnification. If you bring
the patient closer to the source you will increase the magnification. The testable point is that
geometric magnification will increase Entrance Skin KERMA (greater risk of a bum), but
NOT change KAP.
KAP Collimation
Collimation will DECREASE the KAP. The reason should be obvious - as
KAP = Dose x Cross Sectional Area
\x Will Be Decreased
With Collimation
Collimation will generally INCREASE the entrance air kerma. This is because the automatic
brightness monitor is going to call for an INCREASE in juice. The effect is marginal - as the
risk of bums etc is pretty minimal at least for fluoro doses.
338

Trivia:
• High Yield Trivia = KAP represent the total energy incident on the patient
• High Yield Trivia = KAP does NOT indicate the risk of skin bums - remember the example
of geometric magnification.
• High Yield Trivia = KAP is a “good indicator” of stochastic risk
• High Yield Trivia = Air Kerma is an indicator of deterministic risk
T h is vs T h a t - R isk M odels; D e te rm in is tic vs S to c h a s tic E ffe c ts
Deterministic (Non-Stochastic) - This risk model is best thought of as “cause and effect.” If I get
“x amount” of radiation I get skin bums. If I get “x-amount” of radiation I get cataracts. If I get “x-
amounf ’ of radiation I get gut necrosis and I die. Well... I poop a lot of blood, moan loudly, then die
Key Point - There is a threshold. Once you cross the threshold you get the result (bum, cataract, gut
necrosis etc..).
Key Point- After you cross this threshold, the more dose you get, the more severe that thing gets.
Stochastic - This is the “Shit happens” model. Why do I call it that? Because it’s a no-threshold by
chance model. How much radiation till you get cancer? A little or a lot - no one knows. BUT there
is a relationship with dose and chance. The more dose you get, the more likely you will get cancer -
but there isn’t a threshold.
Another example of this would be “Big River Man” - How the fuck is that
guy not dead? If you aren’t familiar with him he is a big bellied Slovenian
man who drives drunk, eats horse burgers, and swims naked in the Amazon
river (among other rivers). There is a Netflix documentary on the dude.
This guy is filled with Amazonian river parasites, and he’s not dead.
Meanwhile you have random bad luck - trees falling on people etc.. who are
very healthy and don’t swim naked in the Amazon. Obviously his behavior
increases his risk - but there is no threshold - cause and effect death model.
Key Point - Stochastic Effects are Chance-Based - and follow a Linear Quadratic No Threshold
model - where risk increases with dose but doesn’t automatically result in a bad outcome.

339

In teractio n of R adiation w ith Tissue
As discussed before, you have two primary methods with which x-rays interact with matter;
the photoelectric effect and compton scatter. The key interaction is the production of a free
electron, which can then transfer its energy to other electrons by ionization and excitation.
The actual damage done is the result of ionization produced by x-rays / gamma ray
photons giving energy to orbital electrons and alpha / beta particles interacting
electromagnetically with orbital electrons.
What Determines the Biologic Effects from Ionizing Radiation?
Primary variables include those inherent to the cells and the conditions of the cells at the time
of irradiation. There are also variables related to the radiation (absorbed dose, dose rate, type
of radiation, and energy of radiation).
Damage to biologic systems occurs in this order:
Molecular -> Cellular -> Organic
Molecular damage is always first. Ionized atoms do not bind properly to other molecules.
Loss of function in the molecule leads to loss of cellular function.
340

Charged Particle Tracks:
When radiation crashes into a biologic material, energy is deposited along the tract. The
pattern of this deposited energy depends on the type of radiation involved.
X-Rays / Gamma Rays: X-ray ionization density is low along the track
Neutrons, Protons, and Alpha Particles: Ionization events occur much more frequently.
Electron Scattering: tends to have a length
greater than its range / depth.
Heavy Particles: tend to have a linear path that
is equal to its range
Concepts / Vocabulary:
Linear Energy Transfer (LET) - This is the average amount of energy deposited per unit
path length of the incident radiation. LET is important for assessing the potential tissue and
organ damage. LET comes in two flavors:
•
High LET: Neutrons, Protons, Alpha Particles, and Heavy Ions. These are MUCH
MORE DAMAGING (“has a higher quality factor”).
•
Low LET: Photons, Gamma Rays, Electrons, and Positrons
Relative Biologic Effectiveness (RBE) - This is the relative capability of radiation with
differing LETs to produce a particular biologic reaction.
RBE = Dose of 250 kV x-rays / Dose in Gy of Test Radiation
Example Problem: A reaction is produced by 5 Gy of test radiation. It takes 10 Gy of 250
kVp x-rays to produce the same reaction. What is the RBE ?
10 / 5 = 2 ; In this example the test radiation is 2 times as effective in producing this biologic
reaction than the “standard” 250 kVp x-rays.
341

The “Kill Effect*
As LET increases, RBE will increase... to a certain point.
Above 100 keV/micrometer of tissue, RBE decreases with increasing LET - because the
maximum potential damage has already been done - additional increase in LET is wasted
dose.
Oxygen Enhancement Ratio (OER) - This is the relative effectiveness of radiation to produce
damage at different oxygen levels. The idea is that biologic tissue is more sensitive to radiation
in an oxygenated state. A testable piece of trivia is that OER really only matters for low LET
radiation. With high LET radiation the OER is often 1 (biologic damage without oxygen =
biologic damage with oxygen).
Direct vs Indirect Ionizing Radiation: Actions are called “direct” if they act “directly on the
DNA.” Actions are called “indirect” if they act on water. The majority of irradiation in living
cells is the result of indirect action on water (your body is 70% water and <1% DNA). This
indirect action on water creates a free radical which then jacks the DNA.

342

Effects on DNA
Single Strand Break - Ionizing radiation can cause a break in one of the chemical bonds (point
mutation). This is more likely with low - LET radiation. Repair enzymes can move in and fix
this.
Double Strand Break - Ionizing radiation can cause a break in multiple chemical bonds. These
are more likely with high - LET radiation. These are harder to fix.
Mutation - It’s possible for radiation to cause a loss of or change in the nitrogenous base in the
DNA chain. If the cell doesn’t die from this, this incorrect information will be transferred as
the cell divides.
Testable Point: The syndrome with the most sensitivity to x-rays is Ataxia Telangiectasia.
Common distractors include Bloom Syndrome and Fanconi Anemia (both of which have
genetic instability but no particular relationship with x-rays). Xeroderma pigmentosa is
another classic distractor - but it is more sensitive to UV radiation.
Chromosome Anomalies - Two types have been described at metaphase.
Chromosome Aberrations - Damage occurs early in interphase (before DNA synthesis).
Both chromatids are broken so each daughter will get a broken copy.
Chromatid Aberrations - Damage occurs later in interphase (after DNA synthesis).
Only 1 chromatid is going to have a break (the other one will be fine)
343

Effect on the Cell:
Testable Point - An x-ray or gamma ray dose of 1000 Gy in a period of second / minutes will
cause instant death of a large number of cells.
“Mitotic Death ” - This is when a cell dies after 1 or more divisions. A relatively small dose of
radiation can cause this.
“Mitotic Delay ” -A very small dose (0.01 Gy) just before a cell starts to divide can cause a
delay or failure in the timing of the normal dividing.
Cell Cycle Phase - Cells are MOST sensitive during M phase (mitosis). They are more
sensitive during late G2 phase. They are less sensitive during G1 phase. There are the LEAST
sensitive during late S phase.
Testable Point: In order of sensitivity: M > G2 > G1 > S.
Testable Point: G1 is the part of the cell cycle that is the most variable in length (shorter in cells
that turn over quickly).
Surviving Cell Synchronization - If you blast a group of cells with radiation, the ones that survive
will have their cell cycles synchronized. The reason is that the most resistent part of the cell
cycle is late synthesis. So most of the surviving cells are in this phase.
Law o f Bergonie and Tribondeau - Cell sensitivity is directly related to their reproductive
activity, and inversely related to their differentiation. So, the more a cell turns over (skin, blood,
GI tract lining, reproductive cells), the more sensitive they are. The less cells turn over and more
differentiated they are (brain, nerves, muscles), the less sensitive they are.
Testable Point: The small bowel is the most radiosensitive part of the GI tract.
344

Key Points:
Higher Dose Rate makes a smaller shoulder, and steeper drop in the curve
•
Low LET will have a shoulder, High LET will NOT have a shoulder
•

Effect of Ionizing R adiation of Blood:
Lymphocytes are the MOST sensitive blood cells in the body. A dose of 0.25 Gy is enough to
depress the amount circulating in the blood.
Lymphocytes can actually be stimulated to divide and you can score the number of chromosomal
aberrations at first mitosis to reflect average total body dose.

High Yield Point: Early Vomiting is a marker o f severity / poor prognosis.
Effect of Ionizing R adiation - Lethal Dose 50/30:
This is the dose which will kill 50% of people within 30 days. The LD 50/30 for a person is
around 3-4 Gy without treatment. With medical treatment you may be able to tolerate up to
8.5 Gy (depending on if you ate your Wheaties).
Lethal Dose 50/60 = Lethal dose at 60 days for 50% of the population. This is used for bone
marrow failure. This is about 3-4 Gy.
Lethal Dose 50/4 = Lethal dose at 4 days for 50% of the population. This is used for GI
failure. This is about 10 Gy.
Genetically Significant Dose (GSD) = This term is used for expressing genetic risk as an index
of presumed impact on the entire population. GSD is the dose, if received by all members of
the population, that would result in the same hereditary damage as the actual doses received
by the gonads of the people who actually get radiation exposure. This depends on gonadal
dose, and child bearing potential (age of patient, sex of patient).
347

Radiation Effect on a Fetus:
• Teratogenicity of radiation is dose-dependent, and timing-dependent.
• First two weeks (implantation) = 50-100 mGy may cause fetal loss. If the baby
doesn’t die from the dose in the first weeks he/she is likely to have no lasting effects
{all or nothing).
• Between 8-15 weeks = The MOST VULNERABLE TIME. Doses over 100-200
mGy are associated with reduced head diameter and mental retardation.
• After 15 weeks the brain is less sensitive to radiation.
• Trivia: IQ is said to drop 30 points per 1 Sv, with the risk of retardation being 40%
• Trivia: It takes a very low dose (just a few radiographs) to the fetus to increase the
risk of childhood leukemia.
• Trivia: The fetal thyroid does NOT take up iodine prior to week 8. So, if mom gets
1-131 prior to week 8 the fetus will not be hypothyroid (after that it’s hosed).
Increased Risk of Cancer:
The risk of cancer (estimated by BEIR 5 and UNSCEAR) was 8% / Sv. A reduction factor of
2 is used for low dose and low rate - so the working population has a risk more like 4%-5%
/Sv. Some people throw around the following calculation:
Risk for Hereditary Effect o f Radiation:
0.2 /100 x Dose
Example: Women gets CT scan (0.3 Gy) then gets pregnant 1 year later. Chance of
radiation induced hereditary defect? 0.2 / 100 x 0.3 = 6 x 10 4 ■ or 6 in 10,000
Latency: The time between exposure and appearance of cancer is sometimes called the
latency. It’s variable for cancers. For example, leukemia has a short latency of 5-7 years,
whereas solid tumors have a latency as long as 20-50 years.

Carpentry - Essential Trivia
Things you might need to know if you start moonlighting as a carpenter building
mammography rooms:
• Dry wall should be 2 layers of 5/8-inch drywall
• Doors should be equivalent to 1 millimeter of steel.
» Wood doors without additional shielding can NOT be used for mammography rooms without
placing the vertical / operators barrier by the doorway. A wooden door would need to be
7 inches thick to be equivalent to two layers of 5/8-inch drywall
• Floors and ceiling - you can use the typical concrete found in most
• Standard concrete construction found in most office buildings is usually ok.
352

High Yield Radiation Biology/Safety Blitz:
The majority of energy received by biologic material from x-rays is transferred by electrons
Two-thirds (around 60%) of x-ray damage to biologic material is mediated by free radicals
Interaction of two separate chromosomal breaks can lead to aberrations such as dicentrics
and rings
Double stranded DNA breaks are the most important lesions caused by x-rays.
The final number of double stranded DNA breaks is more important than the initial number
of breaks - because some will be repaired.
You can score whole body radiation exposure by stimulating lymphocytes to divide.
Risk of Radiation induced CA: 4-5% per Sv = Adult, Up to 15% per Sv for Child, About
1/10th that for someone older than 50
Risk of Radiation induced CA: One sievert (effective dose) = 5.5% chance of developing
cancer
Transient skin erythema can be seen in hours, with the main wave occurring after 10 days
Radiation-induced sterility in males does NOT affect hormone levels or libido.
Radiation-induced sterility in males has a “latent period” between irradiation and sterility
Radiation-induced sterility in females causes symptoms similar to menopause.
Carcinogenesis by radiation is stochastic (all or nothing) - based on Beir 5 and UNSCEAR
committees
Sentinel Event = 15 Gy
The thyroid can have radiation induced benign and malignant nodules
Radon workers get more lung cancer
Cataracts caused by radiation start in the back of the lens
If Al-Qaeda attacks your hospital with a dirty bomb, remember that the causalities may
present a risk to medical personnel (because they are contaminated with radionuclides).
Inhaled Radon (an alpha emitter) contributes about 55% of the effective dose to the US
population, and is the largest single contributor to effective dose.
The greatest source of exposure to ionizing radiation for the general population of the United
States from due to human activity is medical imaging.
For MRI; you must have a controlled access so that the fringe field outside the area doesn’t
exceed 5 Gauss.
For MRI; there is a thing called “Specific Absorption Ratio” - which is the RF power
absorbed per unit mass. The SAR should not exceed 4 W/kg for the whole body for 15 mins.

The QA officer has several important duties including (1) Reading hand washing statistics to
the faculty once a month, (2) Stirring up trouble, and (3) Making everyone’s lives miserable.
Who is the QA Officer, and why does he hate puppies, sunshine, and colors? He is a man with
no soul. Bom without warm blood, only cold black oil fills his veins. He is the official trouble
maker of the Radiology department.
This vs That: QC, QA, and QI
The terms QC, QA, and QI are often used interchangeable to describe the various sinister deeds of the
QA officer. However, there are differences and those differences are potentially testable.
QC - Quality Control
As a resident you are responsible for doing all the work in the
department. You can think about the work you do as “generating
a product.” The product you are making is a radiology report.
That product (your report) is checked for quality to see if it
meets certain requirements. This “checking for quality” is
quality control.
Example 1 "The Readout ” - The attending performing a
readout is doing the QC. Your product (the shoulder MR read) is
checked by your attending to make sure it meets acceptable
parameters. Note that this happens multiple time a day.
Example 2 “Berating the Tech ” - You (the Resident) may find it therapeutic to periodically yell at the
Tech - For example, your attending disagrees with you on that shoulder MR read. You said the labral
tear was 270 degrees and he thinks it’s 290 degrees. He makes a big deal about this and embarrasses
you in front of the med student who wants to do ortho and thinks he’s learning something by
watching the readout. To make yourself feel better you decide to yell at the Tech. The next case that
comes through is a chest x-ray on a 500 pound vented ICU patient. You notice the right costophrenic
angle is partially clipped. You immediately call the tech room and berate them for their poor quality.
You mention the phrase “patient care” a lot to really make them feel bad. You don’t let up until the
tech begins to cry. You like the way it makes you feel and decide that a career in academics is right
for you. This process of checking the film for quality is an example of QC.
Example 3 “Bean Counting” - Bean Counters check your ultrasound reports to make sure you
mention all the stuff you need, so medicare will pay. “Can you put an addendum on that and mention
the IVC???” The process of checking your reports for the IVC is an example of QC.
Quality Control = Checking to
see if the product meets the
acceptable parameters.
Think about this as Yes or No.
This is NOT a process for
improvement. No solutions are
provided. It is simply - “good
dog” or “bad dog.”
357

QA - Quality Assurance
This is a set of activities for checking quality in the
processes by which products are created.
Example - Lead Tech trains the other techs to position the
patient’s arms up during the body CT scan. The Lead Tech
(an overweight man with a mustache) periodically gets off
Facebook (he is an avid Farmville player) and pokes his
head in the scanner room to make sure the patient’s arms
are up. If they aren’t up then he has to hold a training
session, which means less time for Farmville, which means
he gets PISSED!
During the training session the Lead Tech yells “you did it
wrong!”
The point is to “prevent” a defect in the product. This prevention is typically done with periodic
audits of the operations of the system.
Ql - Quality Im provem ent
In my example where the Lead Tech has to retrain the other techs at the expense of his Farmville
time, this would be an example of QI. So, to be clear:
QA - The Lead Tech performs the audit, by checking to make sure the patient’s arms are up.
After noticing the error has already occurred (arms are down) he reacts and yells “you did it
wrong!”
QI - The Lead Tech retrains the techs to make sure they understand the importance of keeping
the arms up. During the training he raises his arms many times. He is an overweight man and
sweats a lot. The perspiration stains under his armpits make the other techs very uncomfortable
so they look at the floor. He becomes angry that they aren’t looking at him during his
demonstration and questions their commitment to patient care. The Lead Tech demands that a
check list be attached to the scanner and a mark must be made on the paper indicated that the
arms were placed up prior to the scan. The whole scene is just ugly, and the techs never make that
mistake again. The QI intervention is a great success.
Another concept regarding QA, QC, and QI is this idea that an active improvement is best. This
is how I remember this. Remember when your PD/Chairman said they had to take your book
fund / travel fund away because of Obama? The real reason was they hired a QA officer and
needed to free up some cash to pay him. Most QA officers are paid in silver (30 pieces).
For that QA officer to have a job he/she has to continuously stir up trouble, and he/she does that
by looking for projects. Why??? Because “we can always improve.” “I for Improve, I for QI”

Making People Miserable - The H aw th o rn e Effect:
Monitoring your work for “training purposes” - falls under the purview of the QA Officer.
People behave differently when they think they are being watched.
At the institution I trained I used to have a lot o f “unproductive conversations” while on night float. Me
and my teammate on call would discuss a variety o f topics including (but not limited to);
How much I hated various Attendings and the Program Director(s),
Which female Residents/Attendings I would like to have sexual encounters with (with the priority
debated and ranked),
My thoughts on various political opinions (Mexico will pay for the wall!), gun rights - including
my desire to buy a fully automatic Israeli assault rifle (for home protection), making / blow shit
up with homemade tannerite, the hoax o f global warming, how all the religions are wrong
(except the one I practice), various drugs I’ve tried - including DMT (the spirit molecule),
That time I told a Surgeon to go fuck him self
That time I told most o f the ED to go fuck themselves
That time I told that Medicine Intern to go fuck himself.
Obviously these are not conversations that I would want to be made public or listened to by anyone. If
you take in house call I imagine you have similar conversations, or have similar conversations in the
privacy o f your own home.
Now imagine if your chairman / PD told you he placed a recording device in the call reading room.
Those conversations probably wouldn’t happen, and you’d be a lot more productive. That is the
Hawthorne effect - you shut up and play by the rules when you are being watched.

Making People Miserable - T h e W e b e r E ffe ct:

People love to talk shit about the new guy. New attending shows up and everyone starts looking at
him/her very closely. He said this or that. He missed this or that. Look at the way he eats! If you ever
switched schools growing up you probably experienced this first hand.
It happens with drug adverse reactions a lot. The reporting of “adverse events” is highest in the first
year, then starts to taper off.

What is this Benchmarking? Basically how well you are doing something relative to a standard

give their patients a lollipop and a sticker after their agonizing MR experience. Perhaps we should try
that too.

Stirring Up Trouble - Best Practices
A “Best Practice” is a method that shows superior results compared to other methods.
On the prior page, we discussed the use of benchmarking to see how much our metastatic cancer
patient population enjoys laying in the magnet for hours without moving. We were distressed to find
out that they don’t like it very much. We also discovered that our competition offers lollipops as an
incentive to raise customer satisfaction scores. Offering the lollipop is an example of a practice that
is showing superior results relative to not offering the lollipop - i.e. a “best practice.”
However, a critical detail is that “best practices” continue to evolve.
So a new strategy is employed to see if even better results can be obtained. Instead of having our
elderly, poorly mannered, former public school bus driver, secretary pass out the lollipops we
hire a 20 year old, large breasted college student to administer the candy. Satisfaction scores sky
rocket among our metastatic prostate cancer patients - and this is found to be the new “best
practice.”
Stirring Up Trouble - Key P erform ance In d icato r (K PI)
This is the metric for which the QA Officer will judge you buy. A poor performance here and he may
recommend “additional training.”
In other words, it could be the bottom line ($$$) or it could be how fast you get that allergy bracelet
on once they hit the front door of the ED.
Example - The KPI for the President of the United States is probably
the unemployment rate. That’s the thing most people will quote for
how well he is doing (at least from an economic point of view).
Example - In Radiology this might be a tum-around time metric or
simply the bottom line in the department. How much money the
slaves (residents/fellows) saved you by taking in house call at night.
Stirring Up Trouble - Dashboards
A “dashboard” is a tool that displays the most important information all on one screen or page.
Examples might include an “urgent” work-list or daily RVU counter.
Dashboards need to provide immediate feedback (you read the study, it drops off the list) and be
simple to be effective.

Making People Miserable - Lean
If the QA Officer had his way he would run the hospital just like a Japanese car factory
(“LEAN” is the workflow style developed by Toyota).
Testable Point - This overall strategy involves “engaging” the workforce. *Not just thinking
problems are solved at the management level.
Huh? Basically - the techs, reading room coordinators, and even the lowest of the low (the
residents) understand the inefficiencies in the process and can have great insight in
improvement of the process.
Testable Point - LEAN = PULL. Memory strategy; Meat Heads (who are big not lean) only do
bench press (a Pushing exercise). Real athletes do power cleans and snatch - bench is for idiots.
WTF is "PULL” ?
This vs That: PUSH vs PULL
PUSH = This is really something that happens in industry and not really in the hospital. But it’s
a vocab word so it’s potentially testable. The basic idea is that you make something even if no
one is buying it. On January 1 - the board of directors got together and said we are going to
make 500 cars a month. Even if no one is buying them, we are still going to PUSH 500 cars off
the assembly line into the warehouse. Why? Cuz that was the plan. This seems stupid right?
On January 1st the Chairman says we are going to take 300 chest x-rays a day. Regardless of the
number ordered or the number of patients in the hospital, 300 chest x-rays are getting done.
Makes no sense... so this isn’t the one to pick on the test.
PULL = This is the idea that you PULL resources to match the demand. The floor orders 10
portables, one tech can handle that and one resident can read that. The floor orders 100
portables - gotta PULL a tech in to help. I’d say the resident would get help also, but this isn’t a
science fiction book. These scenarios have to be at least partially grounded in reality
“Kandan”
Another "Getting Lean” Concept, that basically means don’t let your supplies run out, otherwise
you can’t do the next case and you have to wait around. So, when supplies run low, you get a refill.
Memory Tool - Kandan = Kan Dan. Dan is the guy who gets you more supplies before you run out.
Hey Dan... Kan you restock the PICC Lines? We only have 2 left, and I usually drop at least one on
the floor per case. If anyone Kan.... Kan-Dan. Dan Kan get you more supplies.

“Getting Lean ” concept that is basically the idea of standardizing work (when possible).
Some people might disagree, but I think structured reporting style is actually a faster way and
safer way to read. That way you hit all the stuff you need to, in the same order, and minimize
the thinking. It makes everyone’s reports look similar which is nice for the ordering
providers - makes finding the one or two things they actually care about easier. As opposed

Don’t make more than you need. Don’t make a shitty product (going back and fixing stuff

m
Yet another "Lean ” Concept. Basically this is having a smooth continuous workflow.
Example: The ultrasound tech has been doing multiple portable exams, but waits until 7:59
to drop the tech worksheets on your desk, because he/she leaves at 8:00. That way they can
bail before you can ask for additional images, or complain about how shitty their work is (you
labeled the bile duct the portal vein... again). In addition to ducking work, this also causes a
work flow issue for you, as you were just sitting there with a clear list thinking you were
going to leave your swing shift on time, and now you are stuck till 9:30 cleaning up this
ultrasound dump. Had they provided you with a few ultrasounds at a time, you would be way
more efficient with your time. This would be a “just in time ” way to do things. No dumps...
you are working continuously through the time you have... just in time.

Making People Miserable - DMAIC Methodology
This is very similar to the PDSA method - which essentially follows a common sense approach.
But instead of just saying that, the QA officer tries to act like they invented something by calling
it another vocabulary word.
Define -> Measure -> Analyze -> Improve -> Control
In other words, state the problem, find a way to quantify or measure the problem, guess where
the problems are, make an intervention, and then see if it worked. If they work - they act like a
control to keep you from sliding back into your pattern of screwing up.

Trivia: DMAIC is the Primary Tool for Six Sigma Projects (although DMAIC is not exclusive to
Six Sigma), it can be used for any random project.

What does “multi-disciplinary ” mean ? It means you let the nurses and techs pretend they are important

Swiss Cheese Trivia = Adding layers isn’t always the answer, sometimes that bogs the system down,
increasing its inefficiency. Sorta like government bureaucracy - the cure to bureaucracy isn’t always
more bureaucracy (you socialist pig!).
Short Run Goal = “Quick Fix” - Place immediate policy / countermeasure so that it doesn’t happen again
(amputating more than one wrong limb in a week is frowned upon). In other words, when you amputate
the wrong leg on Monday - and blame it on the resident, you don’t want to amputate the wrong arm on
Tuesday. Mainly because the resident you blamed it on Monday will be fired, and if you try and blame it
on another resident the higher-ups might get suspicious that you are actually the problem. So you want to
place a short term fix - threaten all the residents that you will make them all take call on Christmas if this
happens again. This is not a long term fix (plus some of them might be Jewish / Muslim / or even worse
not acknowledge the existence of a Celestial Dictator)
Long Run Goal = This is the long term fix - a system fix. Mark the right leg pre-op before the patient is
sedated.

Any time I enter a patient care area, the first thing I do is look for the person I’m going to

What is a “Trainee ? ” A Trainee is the new word for resident. It was developed by
bureaucrats to help maintain the hierarchy of academic medicine, with the eventual goal of
providing no salary or benefits (why would you pay the trainee?).
When can a “Trainee” be referred to as a Doctor? There are only two scenarios: (1) If there is
a menial task that requires a physician signature (writing scripts, doing H&Ps, consenting
patients, etc....) or (2) There has been a complication and someone needs to be blamed.
Trivia: How many people die per year from medical error? Between 44,000 and 98,000

Fundamental Errors:
(1) Shitty Communication - Basically the left hand doesn’t talk to the right hand. You have lots
of different providers, EDs, Dr. Oz, etc providing medical advice and therapy and they don’t
talk to each other. One doc puts you on a med, the next takes you off, then Dr. Oz sells you
miracle weight loss potion.
a. Buzzword = “Non-System ”
b. Buzzword = Decentralized Health Care Delivery
(2) Idiot Test Writers - License Exams don’t focus on error
a. Turns out memorizing what chromosome Wilms tumor is on doesn’t make you a
better / safer doctor.
(3) Blood-Sucking Lawyers - In America, Lawyers or “Scum Sucking Fuck Pigs” (the technical
term for a lawyer) ruin everyone’s fun. People are afraid o f getting sued, so errors aren’t
reported. If errors don’t get reported, then mistakes never get corrected.
(4) $$$ - Providers are not incentivized to emphasize safety, and since the real reason 99.9% of
people actually go into medicine is to get paid - if you aren’t paying me to do it, then I’m not
doing it. Want me to do something? Then pay me... or take the hospital administrator
strategy and threaten not to pay me, and keep the extra money for yourself.
Types of Errors (per the model classification)
(1) Diagnostic Error - Family medicine wants a non-con chest to rule out a PE.
(2) Treatment Error - Family medicine wants you to drain that gluteal abscess (which is actually
a massive necrotic tumor). Now the track is seeded and they have to resect his entire
posterior compartment - leaving the patient unable to stand up.
(3) Preventive Error - Grandma falls out of bed. Happens at home - no ones fault. Happens at
the hospital - it’s your fault (the Trainees fault).
(4) “Other Error” - Communication Errors and Equipment Errors. You say Mag Sulfate but the
Nurse hears Methotrexate.
371

This vs That: - Latent vs Active Errors
Latent Error - Conditions in the system which allow the error to occur
Example 1: Have you ever pulled up to a stop light, and you went a little too far into the
intersection so you put the car in reverse and back up a little bit so you don’t get hit? But then a
song comes on the radio that you really like and you forget to put the car back in drive - so you
are sitting there waiting for the light to turn green with the car in reverse. You are truly an
accident waiting to happen. This is a latent error. Conditions in the system that allow the error to
occur, i.e. Justin Bieber on the radio distracted you - so you forgot to put the car back in drive. If
you didn’t have music distracting you - this wouldn’t have happen
Example 2: Sharps box is bolted to the wall away from the procedure area, so you have to walk
the needles over to it in a crowded room. Why the fuck is the box on the other side of the room?
System Failure = Accident Waiting to happened
Example 3: Shitty EMR allows you to potentially enter lethal doses of pain medicine.
Active Error: A mistake that immediately causes an injury. You did the fucking up. It’s
tough to blame the system - although you can still try and blame the resident.
Example 1: Administration of the wrong dose of medication
Example 2: Cutting the hepatic artery while trying to remove the gallbladder.
This vs That: Blunt End vs Sharp End:
Blunt End = Think about the blunt end as the part you are holding - not the part doing the
actual damage. It’s the tool you are wielding. Latent error occurs at the “blunt end.” The blunt
end might be the EMR which allows for a medication to dosed incorrectly.
Sharp End = Think about the sharp end as the part actually doing the damage. Active Error
occurs at the “sharp end.” Think about this as the surgeon holding the “sharp end” of the scalpel.
He is the active user / operator. He is the one making the error. Another example would be the
nurse who actually gave the wrong medication.
Trivia: The sharp end is the “last line of defense” against an error.
372

If you haven’t figured it out yet - Radiology is hard. It’s very very hard. But at the same time
it’s simple (and made complicated by poor teaching at most academic centers). It’s hard
because in the real world the volume is high and you have to maintain constant concentration for
hours and hours without a lapse. This is why Radiology is much tougher than Medicine or even
Surgery. What??? I thought radiology was supposed to be easy? Yeah... we all did. They lied
to us. Turns out it is hard. Medicine and Surgery docs have a ton of walking around /
bullshitting time during their day. They sleep on call (at least part of the night), and they have
other people checking their work (Nurses, Pharmacists, etc...). Radiology is just you, and you
get no break. There are 2 main categories for fucking up in Radiology:
• Perceptual - Majority - (60-80%)
This is when you didn’t see it. You called the
pancreas normal... it wasn’t normal. There was a
cancer. This is by far the most common type of
mistake. This is why you have to have a search
pattern, and you have to be a fucking robot / zombie
that never breaks the search pattern. Academic
Radiologists often push this idea that
“communication” is why people get sued. It is... in
a minority of cases. The main reason radiologists
get sued is missing the finding.
Slip / Lapse
• Cognitive / Interpretive - (20-40%)
You saw it but you called it the wrong thing. “Hypodense lesion in the cirrhotic liver is
likely focal fat - no follow-up needed” .... Wrong ! - that’s a 6 cm HCC. You’ve heard the
expression “you can’t fix stupid”.... Search pattern won’t help you here bro, you better read
some books. Actually, if you find yourself making these kinds of mistakes you’ll want to try
and switch into an administrative role - something non-clinical like QA Officer. Or even
better, try and get into medical legal work so you can get paid helping to sue other Doctors,
who are actually way better / smarter than you. You can wear a nice suit, and strut up to the
witness stand. Practice that strut in the mirror, it’s important to have the swagger to convince
the jury that you know what you are talking about.

This vs T h at - M istake vs Slip
The difference occurs at the “level of intention” -
You make the decision to act - you are driving in an unfamiliar city and you come upon an intersection.
You must make a choice (turn right or turn left) - if you turn the wrong way that is a “mistake.” If you
decided to turn right, but for some reason turned left instead this is a “slip” - your intention was correct...
you just fucked up.
M istake = Error that occurs during active problem solving. You choose to turn right, but should have
turned left
M istake = In a just culture you get re-trained *more on this later
Slip = Error that occurs during automatic cognitive task. You choose to turn right, but your body turned
left instead. Classic example - calling your wife your girlfriends name while in the throes of passionate
lovemaking.
Slip = In a just culture you get consoled. *more on this later Unfortunately wives are not just in these
particular setting - you will likely be punished (which is only appropriate for reckless behavior).
Slips can NOT be prevented with additional training, but can be prevented with “forcing functions.” If
you bite your lip, you won’t call out anyones name and this won’t happen. Or... I guess you could not
commit adultery.

“Skill” is the guy who ties your shoes for you. If you have ever attempted to teach someone how
to tie their shoes you will realize how complicated it is. Lots of little steps - making a loop,
pinch, hold, pull, and its gotta be just right. When you were a little kid this took massive
concentration to do. Now you totally take it for granted and you can tie your shoes without even
looking down while having a conversation about something else. It’s almost like someone lives
inside of you, and just takes over with little tasks like this. It’s the same guy who drives you to
work in the morning when you are tired and not paying attention. Where does that guy come
from??? Lots of reps. After you tied your shoe 10,000 times you started being able to do it
without paying attention. It’s on auto pilot. Practice doesn’t make perfect, it makes “permanent.”

Blam ing it on th e Train ee
Must Read For Anyone Considering a Career in Academics
You’ve made a terrible mistake and you need someone to blame it on. If you are an Attending, the
answer is obvious - blame your “Trainee.” If you are a Resident - 1 would try and pin it on a
Medical Student or Nurse / Tech. You might be able to blame the Attending if he/she is new to the
department or not well liked by his/her peers. That’s more tricky and you could experience what
Ron Paul referred to as “blow back.” In other words, they may crush you.
What if you feel compelled (the hospital lawyer / risk management guy is making you) to confess
your mistake to the patient or family?
You are supposed to handle it in a specific way— “the sacrament o f confession
(1) Break it down in a wav they can understand. Usually when a patient asks me a question I
use the most complicated medical jargon / anatomy possible to alpha dog them / make them
feel stupid and hopefully discourage them from wasting my time with another question. But
in this situation you are supposed to break it down.
a. Example - “Remember when I said I would try and biopsy the liver ? I missed and
biopsied the blood vessel that brings the liver blood. Without that blood your
transplanted liver got very thirsty and sick.” “Biliary Necrosis” is the fancy doctor
word.
b. Example - “Remember when I said I would try and drain that infection in your belly
with a tube? I accidentally thought your bladder was the infection and put the tube in
it instead.” “Iatrogenic Bladder Rupture” is the fancy doctor word.
(2) Take responsibility. The whole point of this is to convince the patient not to sue you. To
achieve that goal you want to seem honorable / likable. So this is the rare exception where
you want to actually own up and take responsibility. Now when you have your departmental
QA meeting... that is when you want to throw the Trainee directly under the bus. It’s best to
not give them any warning. Just say - “it would be best - for your education - if you came
to the QA meeting. Please sit in the front row where everyone can see you.”
(3) Apologize-Again I’d never advise this under normal circumstances, but you are trying to
not get sued.
(4) Explain what will be done to prevent similar errors. American culture is all about getting
famous. For most; fame is even more desirable than getting rich. To take advantage of this
cultural psychopathology you can tell the patient that they have sparked an entire policy /
rule and that they will go down in the annals of medicine as a famous teaching / learning
case. They may even get a painting in the department. There is no greater honor in academic
medicine than having a painting of you, with a smug look on your face, hanging in some
random room or hallway.

Since you made the terrible mistake of being a doctor, your punishment is to spend your career
worrying about getting sued and eventually getting sued. This can only be prevented by (1)
working at a VA, or (2) quitting medicine and going to law school (so you can do the suing).
What gets you sued?
*
Errors in diagnosis are the most common cause of malpractice suits against Radiologists
*
Imaging findings related to breast cancer are the most common subject of malpractice
suits against Radiologists
*
Communication Errors (with referring M.D., or failure to recommend another test) is
actually a much less frequent cause of litigation - when compared to interpretive error.
Whang, Jeremy S., et al. "The causes of medical malpractice suits against radiologists in the United
States. "Radiology 266.2 (2013): 548-554.
What must be proven?
All 5 Elements must be proven for a successful medical malpractice case:
1. A duty was owed: You owe that duty once you read that case, or do that procedure.
2. A duty was breached: You didn’t follow the standard of care.
3. The breach caused an injury: Your failure to follow the standard of care is what hurt the
patient.
4. Deviation from the accepted standard: You didn’t follow the standard of care in your
specialty (this is similar to #2).
5. Damage: The patient must have gotten hurt (physical or emotional). If they didn’t get
hurt, it doesn’t matter if you were negligent or not.
Basically, it was your case, you missed the finding or didn’t communicate a critical finding (or
other violation of the standard of care), and the patient got hurt. If they didn’t get hurt, then you
are ok. If they got hurt but you followed the standard of care you are ok.
- If you drive an expensive car, you will also be more susceptible to character assassination,
which will aid the prosecution.

Culture of Safety

Basically - to create an environment that emphasizes safety you need to first (1) recognize that
complications occur. If you aren’t having complications then you are not doing enough procedures. To
reduce the number of complications you want to create an environment where (2) people can confess their
sins without punishment so that mistakes don’t get repeated. You want the “Trainees” / Techs to not be
afraid of you (3) so they can tell you if a mistake is going to happen. You have to be willing to (4)
commit resources - usually time into developing safety protocols - checklists, QA meetings, etc..
Trivia: The “AHRO Patient Safety Culture Survey” is the tool used to measure the culture of safety at a
given institution.
Trivia: This AHRQ survey is brought to you by the bureaucracy of the Dept of Health and Human
Services.
Steep Authority Gradient
Fear and punishment are the two primary tools available to an Attending in academic medicine. They
keep those no good, lazy, Trainees in check while the courageous Attendings take naps in their offices and
get coffee.
However, as amazing as it may sound, this type of culture actually contributes to more errors. The reason
is the Trainee is terrified of getting fired / humiliated publicly , and/or being given more call. So he/she
covers up mistakes. If mistakes aren’t out in the open - you are doomed to repeat them.
Bottom line:
Steep Authority Gradient = More Coffee Breaks / Mid-day naps for Attendings
Steep Authority Gradient = More Errors and Less “Culture of Safety”
Trivia: Communicating in an authority gradient requires an “SBAR” style to avoid immediate death and
destruction.
S - Situation
•
B - Background
A - Assessment
R - Recommendation
The way to think about SBAR - is like the old oral boards style of case taking. Describe the findings
(situation and background), make a diagnosis (assessment), and if necessary give a recommendation.

Blood Transfusion-
How do you identify the correct person? You have to use two separate indicators. You can’t just ask him
his name because maybe he’s Vietnamese and like all Vietnamese people his name is Nguyen (seriously
it’s like 40%). You gotta go Name + Birthday, or Name + Med Record Number (or hospital ID number).
Trivia - what can NOT be used as identifying factor? - Basically anything that changes - classic
examples are (a) Patient’s Location, or (b) Patient’s Room Number
As a nurse, what is the verification process for giving blood?
(1) Check to make sure the blood is blood... and not platelets or something
(2) Match the Patient to the Blood. The blood bank should label them - it’s a compatibility thing.
Remember 2 Identifiers for the patient... in case they are a Nguyen.
(3) Two nurses should be involved in the verification.... In case both the nurses are also named
Nguyen.
382

What gets Labeled? The rule on this is - before the procedure starts you are supposed to label
medicines, and I mean ALL the meds that are not labeled. This includes medicines in syringes,
cups, and basins. If you know what’s good for you, you better label those fucking basins.
What if there is only one medication being used? You still have to label it.
Is there any exception to the rule? Yup - In the 2014 recommendations they say label stuff “not
immediately administered” - implying things that are immediately administered don’t need to be
labeled. They even go as far as defining this exception very clearly “An immediately
administered medication is one that an authorized staff member prepares or obtains, takes
directly to a patient, and administers to that patient without any break in the process”.
Where are you supposed to do the labeling? In the area where medicines and supplies are set up.
What do you have to write on the labels? You better write small - because they pretty much want
everything on there. Medication name, Strength, Quantity, Diluent and volume (if not apparent
from the container), Expiration date when not used within 24 hours, Expiration time when
expiration occurs in less than 24 hours.
Exceptions to the label-writing rules? You don’t have to put expiration date and time - if you
are doing a short procedure.
What if the person doing the preparing / labeling is not the person doing the administering?
Then verification (both verbal and visual) is done by two people qualified to participate in the
procedure (the janitor doesn’t count).

Medication reconciliation is awful and it’s one of the reasons people who aren’t interested in

The idea is - these people are on tons of meds, of which they may or may not even be taking.
You are supposed to compare the meds they should be on (and actually using) to new
medications that are ordered and resolve any discrepancies.
Testable Point - If the patient is unwilling or unable to provide you an accurate list, a “good
faith” effort is enough to meet the guideline requirement.
Testable Point - You are supposed to provide the patient with written information on the
medications they should be taking when they are discharged.
Testable Point - Medication Reconciliation is supposed to be done at (a) Admission, (b) Transfer,
and (c) Discharge.

Wrong Site
If you amputate the wrong leg people are going to make a huge deal about it. I get it, mistakes
happen. “To Err is Human ” as the Institute of Medicine would say... but for some reason wrong
site procedures are unforgivable.
Trivia: Any procedure where the patient can’t say “Hey! I thought the osteosarcoma was in my
other leg?” makes it high risk. In other words big risk = general anesthesia or deep sedation.
Trivia: Pre-procedure verification can (a) be done more than once - if that makes you happy, (b)
has to be before the procedure - with the actual time dictated by the hospital, (c) it’s optimal to
involve the patient. “The cancer is in your left leg right?” - “Right” - “Right or Left?” - “Left”
Trivia: Mark the site of the procedure. Especially if it’s possible to do it on the wrong side.
Example = you have one nose, might not need to mark that... but you have two ears... gotta mark
those.
Trivia: For the spine you need to do a fluoro spot film (to be interpreted by the Department of
Radiology a week later) for locating and marking the exact vertebral level.
Trivia: Gotta mark the site before the surgery starts. Can’t mark it after you amputate the wrong
leg. Also, by the book you want the patient awake when you mark the right (left) site.
Trivia: You gotta mark with something that won’t just fall off or wash off during the prep and
draping. In other words - you can’t use a sticky note (or other adhesive marker).
Trivia: What if the patient’s religion forbids them from being marked (or wearing cotton and wool
at the same time) ? You’ll need to use a written alternative process.
Trivia: Other examples of when you need a written site mark alternative? - Teeth, Mucosal
Surfaces, or Premature infants (the mark could cause a permanent tattoo).
Trivia: Best time to get a permanent tattoo? When you are a premature infant.
Trivia: The Time Out - What if you are doing two procedures on the same patient? You need to
perform the time out before each procedure (you can’t do them both in a row at the beginning).
Trivia: The Time Out - You need to agree on (a) Correct Patient, (b) Correct Site, and (C) Correct
Procedure. Also , you have to document the time out happened.
Trivia: I f the patient is demented (or smells bad) and you can’t (or don’t want to) ask him/her for
identifying factors - who else can you obtain this information from? Basically anyone including
relatives, guardians, homosexual domestic partners, and even healthcare providers who “know the
patient”
385

Patient Specific Assessment - This is a fancy way of saying a “pre-procedure note.” This
is required before any kind of image guided procedure, and involves review of the H&P, sedation risk
(if they are getting sedation), and review of labs (H&H, coags, etc...). I know... it sounds horrible.
The good news is you (the Radiologist) don’t have to be the one to do it. A “Qualified Assistant” i.e.
Nurse Practitioner or PA can do it for you. This is why having one of those is 100% essential if you
do procedures - otherwise you will be stuck spending part of your day talking to icky smelly
patients. In academics you can just make your Resident do it.
Trivia - Who can perform it (3) :
•
Radiologist Performing the Procedure,
your “assistant” - NP/PA,
•
or even the referring Provider - even better!
Informed Consent:
Who can obtain it? - You (the Physician) or if you are smart you can have a “qualified assistant” - in
other words a Resident (in academics) or a PA/NP in private practice.
Who can give it ? Patient or a legal representative - they have to be the one listed as the go-to in the
chart.
Ultimate Responsibility? Unfortunately it’s you (the Physician performing the procedure) - if they
have additional questions, etc... you are supposed to answer them.
Components of the Consent:
• What the procedure involves ? - We are going to put a tube through your grandmother’s skin into
her stomach so she can eat.
• What is the benefit ? Although there is probably no longer term survival benefit given her
advanced Alzheimer’s and other co-morbidities - BUT this will really decrease the guilt you have
about not visiting her in the nursing home. Now you can tell yourself you “helped her.”
• What are the risks? It will periodically get clogged and they will transfer her to the ED in the
middle of the night to unclog it/ give her IV fluids. They will probably call you and wake you up
in the middle of the night to come in and give consent for her etc...
• Reasonable Alternatives ? Let her die in dignity with hospice.
• Risks of Refusing ? Your lack of understanding of medicine coupled with American culture will
make you feel very guilty for not “helping her”, and you may get accused of “killing grandma” by
one of your drunken relatives at the next family barbecue.
386

(1) Patient has dementia or delirium (can’t understand the
procedure, risks/benefits etc).
(2) Is a minor (and not pregnant)
What to do? Gotta get consent from an appointed representative
What about in emergencies? You can treat without consent to prevent serious disability or
death / or to alleviate great pain or suffering.

° PMMIITIffliUS IM M URT, MM* -
Communication Practice Standards:
**Disclaimer - the following tirade Reflects my (the author’s) understanding of the practice
parameter.
**Disclaimer - my opinion on this document does in no way represents those of the ACR, or
ABR. They are my own opinions.
Do communication standard exists? - Yes. There is a document written by the ACR referred to
as “ACR Practice Guideline for Communication of Diagnostic Imaging Findings.”
Is this document helpful ? In my opinion it is 10% helpful, and 90% very very very harmful.
When I first read this document it completely blew my fucking mind. It appears to place you
(the Radiologist) at significant litigation risk for every single case you read, if you don’t call the
provider and read the entire report to them (then demand a re-back). Although, the document
explicitly claims this is not its intention.
Prometheus those are strong words - why do you feel like this ?
I’m deeply concerned about the section on “non-routine communication” specifically part iii.
Here is a brief summary of this section:
Section 2 “Non Routine Communications” ** this is from the 2014 revision.
i — Findings that suggest a needfor immediate or urgent intervention (i.e. Pneumothorax) — ok, totally
reasonable.
ii - Findings that are discrepant with a preceding interpretation of the same examination and where
failure to act may adversely affect patient health - ok , also totally reasonable.
iii - Findings that the interpreting physician reasonably believes may be seriously adverse to the patient s
health and may not require immediate attention but, if not acted on, may worsen over time and possibly
result in an adverse patient outcome - This is where they destroy you. They define these findings as
“acute infectious processes, possible malignant lesions, or other unexpected findings that may impact
patient care if not treated. ” In other words... anything. Literally any positive scan. Pulmonary nodule?
Well that could turn into cancer - gotta call on it. Possible pneumonia vs atelectasis ... gotta call on it.
Renal hypodensity that is 16 H.U.s probably a cyst but recommending an ultrasound just to cover my
ass... gotta call on it. So if you don’t work in academics and have residents to call on all these findings
you literally cannot get any work done. It’s incredibly to me that this was put into writing. Absolutely
incredible.
391

But you can just send them an email right??? They specifically say in this document that you
have to confirm receipt. How do you know they read that email?? Could have gone into the junk
folder. Plus there are HIPAA concerns about sending emails. They strongly encourage telephone
or in person communication. Are they not merciful by letting you do it on the telephone? I
assume to make it even more impossible to follow that for the next edition only in person
communication will be ok. You need to walk down to their clinic and tell them about the 3 mm
nodule in person.
Is the document actually legally binding? NO Well, they say it’s not. They state that this
document is not intended to set a national standard of care, and that the Iowa Supreme Court
ruled on this. Unfortunately, the wording is such that in my opinion you are truly at the mercy of
the court.
Plus they throw this line in there: “a practitioner who employs an approach substantially
different from the guidance in this document is advised to document in the patient record
information sufficient to explain the approach taken. ”
Per the document who is the ultimate judgement regarding the course o f action up to? It is up to
the Practitioner (Radiologist) - who takes in all the practical circumstances.
Then why all the hate Prometheus? I’m just worried about the following situation. Remember
regardless of what it said - this is still an official guideline from our main national body:
Lawyer: Why didn’t you communicate the finding of a 1 cm indeterminate renal cyst ?
Radiologist: I did
Lawyer: Really? Where is it documented ?
Radiologist: It’s impression line 3 - and I gave a specific follow-up recommendation
Lawyer: But did you call?
Radiologist: N o... I assumed the ordering provider would read the report for the multi-thousand
dollar exam they ordered.
Lawyer: You assumed??? ACR - your professional leadership “suggests” that you call on
findings that could potentially harm the patient at some point in the future.
Radiologist: That guideline is not legally binding. Ultimately the judgement to
communicate is up to the practitioner.
Lawyer: So you exercised poor judgement - since 10 years later this lesion turned out to be a
very slow-growing malignancy. Did you document in the chart the reason you didn’t
communicate this finding- as suggested in the document, since you decided to deviate from their
suggested course of action?
Radiologist: I surrender.. .(begins to cry) take all my money. I’ll sleep under the bridge
Lawyer: Excellent - My secretary will tell you who to make the check out to.

Have your group meet with medicine and surgery and come up with a standardized communication protocol.
We will call you on these 15 things. These other things we will send an email or text or something. All other
minor things, will be in the report. Then you document this meeting happen. Now you have established an
“institutional standard of care.”
Practical Solution (this is what I did with my private practice group) :
Ok do you have any other beefs with the paper? Yup. The paper specifies that the person
generating a radiology report should be “a physician.” Not a radiologist.... But a physician.
Neurologist, Cardiologists, ER, Ortho etc.. please go right ahead and read the imaging. In fact,
we will train you to read it.
Are they not merciful? They do at least take a stand against nurses generating reports? Yes...
real helpful. The financial security of the specialty is now safe.
Is there anything in the paper that you agree with? I like the stance on “informal reads.” The so
called - “curb side” read, or “can you look at this real quick?”, “just a quick wet read - can you
stage this cholangiocarcinoma real fast for me?” The ACR doesn’t like this, says it should be
avoided and if you have to do it you should document it. I almost completely agree.
(1) I don’t work for free. You want me to give an opinion on that study? Put in an order for
official interpretation and I’ll read it.
(2) There is no greater way to fuck up then by looking at a study
quickly with some medicine doctor over your shoulder asking
you to point out basic anatomy. Yes... that is the liver. You
will break your search pattern, and cause confusion. Try to
avoid this.
I f you are an IR doc, and you order a study then read it (it’s a self-referred study), who should
you notify when you have a critical result? The patient. Yes... you actually have to talk to the
patient. I know, its horrible. My advice? Get an NP or PA to make the call for you, and
document.
I f you are reading a study referred by some random 3rd party (Lawyer, Insurance Company,
Employer, etc...) and you have a critical result, who do you notify? Again you might have to
actually talk to the patient. My advice? If you encounter this situation, delete your report and
put the study on your partner’s desk.

P areto C harts
This is a chart named after Vifredo Pareto, who accomplished many great things in his life
including popularizing the term “elite” , marrying a women 30 years his junior (WIN!), and
collecting a large number of cats - which he famously treated better than his house guests (he fed
the cats first, and made his dinner guests wait). Oh, he also made a chart.
The chart is essentially a prioritization exercise. I got 99 problems, which l(s) is the major
contributor. You take causes to a problem, and rank them in the order that they contribute to that
problem. The problems sum together to 100% of the problem (bars are ranked in descending
order, creating a concave function). The point is to show how 2 or 3 things can cause 80% of the
issue. The entire purpose of the chart is to highlight the most important factor among a set
of factors. The idea is that fixing the main cause may fix the problem.

R eceiver O perator Curve - ROC
This is a graph to describe how good your test is. The perfect (accurate) test will have an area
under the curve of 1.0. The worst piece of shit test will have an area under the curve of 0.5 (50%
chance, i.e no better than a coin flip) The graph shows the tradeoff between sensitivity and
specificity.
Questions You Can Ask About ROC Curves:
(1)What are the axes of the graph ? Sensitivity, and 1-Specificity
(2) What kind of a line would have no predictive value ? The straight one as above
(3) What kind of a line would occur from random chance? Same question, just different
wording - still the straight one as above.
(4) Where is the “Ideal” or “Perfect” or “Gold Standard” test? Top left comer.
(5) When the accuracy improves how does the curve change? Shifts towards the upper left
comer.

This vs That Validity vs Reliability
-
Validity = Does it do what it is supposed to do? Does the tool measure what it’s supposed to
measure? Does the simulation device actually prepare you for a real case / accurate mimic a real
patient?
Reliability = The degree to which the tool produces consistent / stable results. Example = you
step on the bathroom scale and it says you are fat. You get off and step back on 10 seconds later
and is says “Still fat.” This is reliable.
Tools can be reliable but not valid and vice versa.
Validity Sub-Type Trivia (using the example of a sim-lab)
Face validity = Does the doll have a human-looking face? Or does the virtual colon thing look like the
real colon on endoscopy?
Constmct Validity = Can it determine if you suck or not? Which should be the overall point of simulation.
In other words - does the test do what it says it does ?
There are two subtypes of Construct Validity:
Discriminant Validity = Does the simulation test have the ability to discriminate between things
that are not supposed to be related. Classic example - a test that measures narcissism (a disorder
that plagues the high ranks of academic medicine) is not simply measuring self-esteem.
Convergent Validity - Does the simulation test have the ability to determine if two measurements
of things that should be related - are actually related (the opposite of discriminant). Classic
example - a test that measures self-esteem can show that measures of similar constmcts, such as,
confidence and self-worth, are also related.
Content Validity = Does it teach you what you need to know?
Concurrent Validity = Does the simulation test correlate with other similar performance tests ?
Predictive Validity = Does success in the sim lab = success in the real world ?

Image Gently
Formed as an attempt to capitulate to the corporate media driven frenzy of radiation-induced cancer. It’s
basically a social marketing campaign, with the end result being increased liability risk from having to try
and interpret shitty low dose scans all the time.
It started out as a Peds thing - which is actually reasonable in some cases. Then QA officers (who’s job
description is “trouble maker”) decided to start applying low dose protocols to 95 year old patients
because saying you “reduced dose” sounds great on paper - especially if you aren’t the one who has to
try and read (and accept liability risk) for the shitty exam.
Trivia = There were 3 phases of propaganda in this social marketing campaign
Phase 1 = Convince the Radiologists it’s a good idea (easy since most don’t understand physics,
and those who do are huge pussies and will do whatever they are told without questioning)
Phase 2 = Convince referring Physicians it’s a good idea. There may actually be a secondary gain
to this - in that Family Medicine doctors who might have been afraid to order a CT on a 90 year
old because of “radiation” could feel more comfortable knowing it’s “low dose.”
•
Phase 3 = Convince the public that it’s a good idea. “Radiation hormesis” — it’s a real thing.
Google it. If you can convince people that colonics are good for them with late night
infomercials than you can certainly sell them on needing a CT once or twice a year to help
stimulate and activate repair mechanisms.
Trivia = “Step Lightly” = what you call it for Pediatric IR
Trivia = “Image Wisely” = what you call it for Adults.
Radiation Risk
Discussing with Patients - Obviously you want to avoid talking to patients at all costs. But sometimes
you just can’t avoid it. Here are some tips on discussing Radiation dose
Risk vs Benefit- What are the risks of not doing the exam? I understand that you are concerned about
developing a malignancy from the radiation exposure of your PET CT Scan but you already have widely
metastatic pancreatic cancer. Without the test your “Cancer Doctors” can’t tell if the chemotherapy is
working or not. Oh... you aren’t taking chemo - you refused that too? You are using “homeopathic juice
therapy” to focus your natural immune system....
Comparison to other exposures - Hours in an airplane or number of chest x-rays
What’s my risk of harm from this single exam? Risk models are developed based on populations. There is
no way to assess a specific individuals risk from a scan.
400

Biologic Effects of Ionizing Radiation (BEIR) Committee - Also known as BEIR 7 Report
What were the sources o f data used? Atomic Bomb Survivor data, medical radiation studies,
occupational radiation studies, and environmental radiation studies. The primary source was
the bomb data.
What was the primary task o f this committee? To develop the best possible risk estimate for
human exposure to low-dose (<100 mSv), low-LET ionizing radiation.
What did the BEIR 7 Report conclude? That there is a thing called a linear, no-threshold dose
response between radiation and the development of cancer in humans.
More specifically, what does BEIR 7 accuse medical imaging of? Giving everyone cancer.
BEIR 7 predicts that 1 in 100 will get a solid cancer or leukemia from a dose of lOOmSv above
background.
Is BEIR 7 legit? N o... it’s probably a load of monkey shit. If you look at how they get their
data, they use a risk model that is “x” times excess relative risk + (l-“x”) times the excess
absolute risk. In this equation, “x” is determined by committee! In other words... they just made
it up. Basically, all estimates are based on multiple models and assumption with confidence
intervals that are subjective and partly based on opinion. Cancer estimates from BEIR 7 are not
proven facts.... Having said all that, the test will probably want you to treat them as such. Who
knows... just say linear no-threshold based on bomb data.

G etting Paid
The only reason to go to medical school and endure residency / fellowship is the chance to make
enough money to buy a Porsche and/or Boat. This is still not guaranteed, as hospital
administrators make more and more and doctors make less and less every year.
Step 1: Getting Credentialed
Testable Trivia revolves around two processes:
(1) Focused Professional Practice Evaluation (FPPE) - This is basically current medical
staff checking your work (proctoring) you for the first few studies you read or a
committee evaluating if you can do a new procedure or not.
(2) Ongoing Professional Practice Evaluations (OPPE) - This is a continued review of
your performance - typically q6 months, that is used for reappointment. Your department
determines the scope of the review.
Step 2: Getting A Paycheck
Testable Trivia Regarding Payment:
Resource Based Relative Value System (RBRVS) - This is a system based on some Harvard
study , that said doctors should get paid based on the time it took to do something and how
difficult it was to do it. RBRVSs are adjusted based on geography with a “Geographic Practice
Cost Indicator”, so that you make more in LA than you do in rural South Dakota or New Mexico.
Omnibus Budget Reconciliation Act of 1989 (OBRA 89) - This is the name of the Medicare
payment reform law.
Participating vs Non-Participating Physicians
-
Participating - If you agree to take what medicare gives you and not try and get the rest of
the money owed to you by the patient.
- Non- Participating - You refuse to sign the agreement, and can attempt to collect the rest of
the money from the patient. The government tries to discourage this by only paying you
95% of what is paid to the participating physicians.
403

Sustainable Growth Rate System (SGR) - This was put in place in 1997 to control the growth
of Medicare. It was based on domestic product per capita. This was totally flawed, and would
have resulted in you making no money at all and being homeless eating out of the dumpster
behind McDonalds. The only thing that kept you from enjoying two day old Big Macs is a
“patch fix” congress did yearly (at the stroke of midnight). The AM A tried to repeal the SGR,
but until 2015 lacked enough funds to “convince” members of congress to act on its behalf.
On April 16, 2015, the Medicare Access and CHIP Reauthorization Act of 2015, was signed by
Obama - ending the use of the SGR. Having said that - this occurred after the first CORE exam
was written. So, it’s possible questions on this topic haven’t been updated to account for this
change. Impossible to say...
Two Lists of Diseases/Procedures Made by Two Different Bodies for Billing:
1. Current Procedural Terminology Codes (CPT) - The AMA editorial panel is actually
in charge of these. They create a “uniform” description of the procedure.
2.Intemational Classification of Diseases (ICD) 9 - This is a list of diseases made by the
World Health Organization (WHO). The “9th” version of this list was the most popular
one. In October of 2015 - ICD 10 was bom, and the world has never been the same.
Two Ways the Government Punishes you for Going to Medical School:
1. Physician Quality Reporting System (PQRS) — Medicare uses carrots (stick to come
later) to force you to report “quality clinical data” on certain conditions.
2. Meaningful Use - Forcing you to have an electronic medical record, and then prove
(by random button clicking) that you are using it.
